KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]** **Define a two-level suspension system. With a neat sketch, name its two stages and the masses it isolates.** **Model Answer (≈ 3 marks):** A **two-level (two-stage) suspension** is one in which the vehicle body is sprung from the road through **two spring-damper units in series**. - **Primary (first) level:** Between the **axle / wheel assembly** (unsprung mass $m_w$) and the **chassis frame**. Stiffness $k_1$, damping $c_1$. - **Secondary (second) level:** Between the **chassis frame** and the **vehicle body** (sprung mass $m_b$). Stiffness $k_2$, damping $c_2$. **Sketch:** ``` Body m_b ──■──[k2, c2]──■── Chassis frame [k1, c1] ■── Wheel/axle m_w [Tyre kt] ═══╧═══ Road ``` The system is therefore effectively a **2-DOF** model exhibiting two natural modes: **body bounce** ($\omega_b$) and **wheel hop** ($\omega_w$). *(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for naming stages + masses, 1 for sketch.)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]** **Distinguish between body-bounce mode and wheel-hop mode in a two-level suspension.** **Model Answer (≈ 3 marks):** | Feature | Body Bounce | Wheel Hop | |---|---|---| | **Frequency** | Low (≈ 1–2 Hz) | High (≈ 10–15 Hz) | | **Dominant moving mass** | Body $m_b$ | Wheel/axle $m_w$ | | **Approximate expression** | $\omega_b \approx \sqrt{k_2 / m_b}$ | $\omega_w \approx \sqrt{(k_1+k_2) / m_w}$ | | **Effect on ride** | Determines passenger comfort | Determines road-holding / tyre contact | | **Design lever** | Soften $k_2$ | Stiffen $k_1$ | The two modes are **decoupled** when $k_1 \gg k_2$, allowing independent comfort and handling tuning. *(3 marks: 1 each for frequency, dominant mass, and design implication.)* --- ### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Module Choice Questions > **ESE Module 2 Instruction (KTU 2024):** *Answer ANY ONE full question from this module. Each carries 14 marks and has sub-parts (a) 7 + (b) 7.* --- ### ✏️ **Question A — 14 Marks** *(Two-Level Suspension Modelling)* **Q.A (a) [7 Marks]** Develop the equations of motion of a quarter-car **two-level suspension** with body mass $m_b$, wheel mass $m_w$, primary stiffness $k_1$ and damper $c_1$, secondary stiffness $k_2$ and damper $c_2$, and road input $x_r(t)$. Write the result in matrix form. **Model Solution:** Free-body of body (downward positive, equilibrium removed): $$m_b \ddot{x}_b = -k_2(x_b - x_w) - c_2(\dot{x}_b - \dot{x}_w)$$ Free-body of wheel: $$m_w \ddot{x}_w = k_2(x_b - x_w) + c_2(\dot{x}_b - \dot{x}_w) - k_1(x_w - x_r) - c_1(\dot{x}_w - \dot{x}_r)$$ Rearranged: $$m_b \ddot{x}_b + c_2(\dot{x}_b - \dot{x}_w) + k_2(x_b - x_w) = 0$$ $$m_w \ddot{x}_w - c_2(\dot{x}_b - \dot{x}_w) + c_1(\dot{x}_w - \dot{x}_r) - k_2(x_b - x_w) + k_1(x_w - x_r) = 0$$ Matrix form: $$\begin{aligned} \begin{bmatrix} m_b & 0 \\ 0 & m_w \end{bmatrix} \!\! \begin{Bmatrix} \ddot{x}_b \\ \ddot{x}_w \end{Bmatrix} \!\! + \begin{bmatrix} c_2 & -c_2 \\ -c_2 & c_1+c_2 \end{bmatrix} \!\! \begin{Bmatrix} \dot{x}_b \\ \dot{x}_w \end{Bmatrix} \!\! + \begin{bmatrix} k_2 & -k_2 \\ -k_2 & k_1+k_2 \end{bmatrix} \!\! \begin{Bmatrix} x_b \\ x_w \end{Bmatrix} \!\! = \begin{Bmatrix} 0 \\ c_1 \dot{x}_r + k_1 x_r \end{Bmatrix} \end{aligned}$$ *(7 marks: 1 free-body diagram, 2 body EOM, 2 wheel EOM, 2 matrix form.)* --- **Q.A (b) [7 Marks]** For the undamped case, derive the characteristic equation and obtain expressions for the **body-bounce** and **wheel-hop** natural frequencies. State clearly the assumption under which they decouple. **Model Solution:** Set $c_1 = c_2 = 0$. Assume harmonic motion $x_b = X_b e^{j\omega t}$, $x_w = X_w e^{j\omega t}$: $$\begin{bmatrix} k_2 - m_b \omega^2 & -k_2 \\ -k_2 & k_1+k_2 - m_w \omega^2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} X_b \\ X_w \end{Bmatrix} = 0$$ Non-trivial solution $\Rightarrow$ determinant = 0: $$(k_2 - m_b\omega^2)(k_1+k_2 - m_w\omega^2) - k_2^{\,2} = 0$$ Expanding and simplifying: $$m_b m_w \omega^4 - \left[(k_1+k_2)m_b + k_2 m_w\right]\omega^2 + k_1 k_2 = 0$$ Quadratic formula: $$\omega^2 = \frac{1}{2}\!\left[\frac{k_1+k_2}{m_w} + \frac{k_2}{m_b}\right] \pm \sqrt{\frac{1}{4}\!\left[\frac{k_1+k_2}{m_w} + \frac{k_2}{m_b}\right]^2 - \frac{k_1 k_2}{m_b m_w}}$$ - Lower root $\omega_1^2 = \omega_b^2$ → **body bounce** - Higher root $\omega_2^2 = \omega_w^2$ → **wheel hop** **Decoupling assumption:** $k_1 \gg k_2$ (primary stage much stiffer than secondary), giving: $$\omega_b \approx \sqrt{\frac{k_2}{m_b}}, \qquad \omega_w \approx \sqrt{\frac{k_1+k_2}{m_w}}$$ *(7 marks: 1 char. eqn setup, 2 expansion, 2 final formula, 2 decoupling condition.)* --- ### ✏️ **Question B — 14 Marks** *(Numerical on Two-Level Suspension)* **Q.B (a) [7 Marks]** A quarter-car two-level suspension has $m_b = 400$ kg, $m_w = 40$ kg, $k_1 = 200$ kN/m, $k_2 = 20$ kN/m, $c_1 = 1.5$ kN·s/m, $c_2 = 4$ kN·s/m. Calculate (i) the equivalent stiffness, (ii) the static deflection, and (iii) the body-bounce and wheel-hop natural frequencies. **Model Solution:** **(i) Equivalent stiffness (springs in series):** $$k_{eq} = \frac{k_1 k_2}{k_1 + k_2} = \frac{(200)(20)}{200 + 20} = \frac{4000}{220} = 18.18 \text{ kN/m}$$ **(ii) Static deflection:** $$\delta_{st} = \frac{(m_b + m_w)g}{k_{eq}} = \frac{(400 + 40)(9.81)}{18.18 \times 10^3} = \frac{4316.4}{18180} = 0.2375 \text{ m} \approx 237.5 \text{ mm}$$ **(iii) Natural frequencies:** Using the exact formula with $k_1 = 200\,000$ N/m, $k_2 = 20\,000$ N/m: $$A = \frac{k_1+k_2}{m_w} + \frac{k_2}{m_b} = \frac{220\,000}{40} + \frac{20\,000}{400} = 5500 + 50 = 5550 \text{ s}^{-2}$$ $$B = \frac{k_1 k_2}{m_b m_w} = \frac{(200\,000)(20\,000)}{(400)(40)} = \frac{4 \times 10^9}{16\,000} = 250\,000 \text{ s}^{-4}$$ $$\omega^2 = \frac{5550}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{5550}{2}\right)^2 - 250\,000} = 2775 \pm \sqrt{7\,700\,625 - 250\,000}$$ $$= 2775 \pm \sqrt{7\,450\,625} = 2775 \pm 2729.6$$ $$\omega_1^2 = 2775 - 2729.6 = 45.4 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \omega_b = 6.74 \text{ rad/s} \;(1.07 \text{ Hz})$$ $$\omega_2^2 = 2775 + 2729.6 = 5504.6 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \omega_w = 74.19 \text{ rad/s} \;(11.81 \text{ Hz})$$ Check via approximation: $\omega_b \approx \sqrt{k_2/m_b} = \sqrt{20\,000/400} = \sqrt{50} = 7.07$ rad/s ✓ (close). *(7 marks: 1 each for parts (i), (ii), 3 for formula, 2 for numerical.)* --- **Q.B (b) [7 Marks]** Determine the **damping ratios** for both modes. Comment on whether the system is well-tuned for ride comfort and road holding. **Model Solution:** $$\zeta_b = \frac{c_2}{2 m_b \omega_b} = \frac{4000}{2(400)(6.74)} = \frac{4000}{5392} = 0.742$$ $$\zeta_w = \frac{c_1 + c_2}{2 m_w \omega_w} = \frac{1500 + 4000}{2(40)(74.19)} = \frac{5500}{5935} = 0.927$$ **Comments:** - **Body bounce:** $\zeta_b = 0.74$ — slightly **over-damped**; ride will feel firm and lack the "float" expected of a comfort vehicle. *Ideal* is $\zeta_b \approx 0.2$–$0.4$. - **Wheel hop:** $\zeta_w = 0.93$ — too high; suppresses tyre-road disturbances but at the cost of transmitting higher shock loads to the body on severe bumps. *Ideal* is $\zeta_w \approx 0.2$–$0.5$. **Recommendation:** Reduce $c_2$ to ≈ 1.5 kN·s/m and $c_1$ to ≈ 0.6 kN·s/m to bring both ratios near 0.3, giving the optimal **comfort + road-holding** compromise. *(7 marks: 1 each formula, 2 calculation, 4 commentary.)* --- > [!WARNING] > **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Two-Level Questions** > 1. **Series vs Parallel confusion** (loses 1–2 marks): $k_{eq}$ is series, not parallel. > 2. **Sign errors** in wheel EOM — the spring $k_2$ pulls the wheel *up* when $x_b > x_w$, so the force on the wheel is $+k_2(x_b - x_w)$, not $-k_2$. > 3. **Forgetting static equilibrium shift** — displacements must be measured from the *static equilibrium position*; otherwise the gravity term $m_b g$ appears incorrectly in the EOM. > 4. **Damping ratio formula** — students often use $c_1$ for body bounce; correct is **$c_2$** because the body is only connected to the secondary damper. > 5. **Units** — $k$ in kN/m, $c$ in kN·s/m, $m$ in kg → convert to N before substitution. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ✅ - 🔑 **Two-level = two spring-damper stages in series** between road and body. - 🔑 **Model = 2-DOF quarter-car** → two EOMs → two natural frequencies. - 🔑 **Body bounce** $\omega_b \approx \sqrt{k_2/m_b}$ — comfort, low frequency (1–2 Hz). - 🔑 **Wheel hop** $\omega_w \approx \sqrt{(k_1+k_2)/m_w}$ — road-holding, high frequency (10–15 Hz). - 🔑 **Decoupling condition** for clean frequency split: $k_1 \gg k_2$. - 🔑 **Series equivalent stiffness:** $k_{eq} = k_1 k_2 / (k_1 + k_2)$. - 🔑 **Damping ratio for body bounce** uses $c_2$ only; **for wheel hop** uses $c_1 + c_2$. - 🔑 **Iso-band** (2–10 Hz) is the goal of good two-level design — no resonance there. - 🔑 **Mode shape** at $\omega_b$: body moves, wheel is still; at $\omega_w$: wheel moves, body is still. - 🔑 **Static deflection** $\delta_{st} = (m_b + m_w)g / k_{eq}$ — keep within 100–250 mm for buses, 80–150 mm for cars. - 🔑 **Applications:** buses, trucks, rail vehicles, premium cars with sub-frames, military vehicles. - 🔑 **Tyre stiffness $k_t$** (≈ 200–400 kN/m) is usually in series with $k_1$ and can be lumped for simplicity. # 📊 Section 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis (Extended Companion) > This section extends the main derivation with **damping-included natural frequencies**, **modal superposition**, and **frequency-response (transfer-function) analysis** of the two-level system. These are KTU 2024 higher-order learning outcomes (CO2 / CO3 level) and frequently appear as 7-mark sub-parts. ## 2.7 Damped Natural Frequencies (with $c_1, c_2$ non-zero) The damped system is governed by the matrix equation in Section 3.1. The characteristic equation is obtained by substituting $x_b = X_b e^{st}$, $x_w = X_w e^{st}$, giving a **4th-order polynomial in $s$**: $$\begin{aligned} \left[m_b s^2 + c_2 s + k_2\right]\!\left[m_w s^2 + (c_1+c_2)s + (k_1+k_2)\right] - (c_2 s + k_2)^2 = 0 \end{aligned}$$ For light damping, the damped natural frequencies are close to the undamped $\omega_b, \omega_w$ and the modal damping ratios $\zeta_b, \zeta_w$ are given by: $$\boxed{\;2\zeta_b \omega_b = \frac{c_2}{m_b}\;}, \qquad \boxed{\;2\zeta_w \omega_w = \frac{c_1 + c_2}{m_w}\;}$$ ## 2.8 Transfer Functions (Road → Body) — Ride Comfort Metric With the road input $x_r(s)$ and body output $x_b(s)$, the transfer function is: $$\begin{aligned} \frac{X_b(s)}{X_r(s)} = \frac{k_1 c_2 s + k_1 k_2}{[m_b s^2 + c_2 s + k_2][m_w s^2 + (c_1+c_2)s + (k_1+k_2)] - (c_2 s + k_2)^2} \end{aligned}$$ For **ride-comfort assessment (ISO 2631)**, the magnitude $|X_b(j\omega)/X_r(j\omega)|$ is plotted against frequency. A well-designed bus keeps this ratio **< 1** in the 4–8 Hz human-sensitive band. ## 2.9 Sky-Hook Damping Reference (Why $\zeta_b \approx 0.3$ is optimal) > [!NOTE] > **"Sky-Hook" Criterion (Karnopp, 1974):** A damper connected between the body and an *inertial reference* (the "sky") gives the best possible ride. The on-board $c_2$ approximates this only at $\omega = \omega_b$. Hence the rule-of-thumb $\zeta_b \approx 0.2$–$0.4$ maximises comfort without excessive road-roughness amplification. # 🧮 Section 3 — Step-by-Step Worked Example (Comprehensive) > This is a **fully-worked numerical** combining all formula-sheet entries. Solve this once before the exam — it covers every entry-point a KTU paper can use. ## Problem A bus quarter-corner has $m_b = 1200$ kg, $m_w = 80$ kg, $k_1 = 350$ kN/m, $k_2 = 60$ kN/m, $c_1 = 3$ kN·s/m, $c_2 = 8$ kN·s/m. Find: 1. Natural frequencies $\omega_b, \omega_w$ 2. Damping ratios $\zeta_b, \zeta_w$ 3. Equivalent stiffness & static deflection 4. Comment on the design. ## Solution **Step 1 — Natural frequencies (undamped exact formula).** $$\begin{aligned} A &= \frac{k_1+k_2}{m_w} + \frac{k_2}{m_b} = \frac{350\,000 + 60\,000}{80} + \frac{60\,000}{1200} \\ &= 5125 + 50 = 5175 \text{ s}^{-2} \\ B &= \frac{k_1 k_2}{m_b m_w} = \frac{350\,000 \times 60\,000}{1200 \times 80} = \frac{2.1 \times 10^{10}}{96\,000} \\ &= 218\,750 \text{ s}^{-4} \\ \omega^2 &= \frac{5175}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{5175}{2}\right)^2 - 218\,750} \\ &= 2587.5 \pm \sqrt{6\,695\,156.25 - 218\,750} \\ &= 2587.5 \pm \sqrt{6\,476\,406.25} \\ &= 2587.5 \pm 2545.1 \end{aligned}$$ $$\begin{aligned} \omega_b^2 &= 42.4 \;\Rightarrow\; \omega_b = 6.51 \text{ rad/s} \;(1.04 \text{ Hz}) \\ \omega_w^2 &= 5132.6 \;\Rightarrow\; \omega_w = 71.64 \text{ rad/s} \;(11.40 \text{ Hz}) \end{aligned}$$ **Step 2 — Damping ratios.** $$\begin{aligned} \zeta_b &= \frac{c_2}{2 m_b \omega_b} = \frac{8000}{2(1200)(6.51)} = \frac{8000}{15\,624} = 0.512 \\ \zeta_w &= \frac{c_1 + c_2}{2 m_w \omega_w} = \frac{3000 + 8000}{2(80)(71.64)} = \frac{11\,000}{11\,462} = 0.960 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3 — Equivalent stiffness & static deflection.** $$\begin{aligned} k_{eq} &= \frac{k_1 k_2}{k_1+k_2} = \frac{350 \times 60}{410} = 51.22 \text{ kN/m} \\ \delta_{st} &= \frac{(1200+80) \times 9.81}{51\,220} = \frac{12\,557.6}{51\,220} = 0.245 \text{ m} \;(245 \text{ mm}) \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4 — Comments.** - $\omega_b = 1.04$ Hz → good (ISO 2631 sensitive band 1–2 Hz) ✓ - $\zeta_b = 0.51$ → **slightly over-damped** for a comfort bus; reduce $c_2$ to ≈ 5 kN·s/m. - $\zeta_w = 0.96$ → too high; reduce $c_1$ to ≈ 1 kN·s/m. - $\delta_{st} = 245$ mm → acceptable for a bus (typical 200–280 mm) ✓. - The frequency gap $\omega_w / \omega_b \approx 11$ is healthy, ensuring a wide isolation band. > **Final Verdict:** The system is **structurally sound** but **dynamically over-damped**. A 30 % reduction in both dampers brings it to optimal ride. # 🖼️ Section 4 — Additional Schematics ## 4.4 Bode-Magnitude Schematic (Frequency Response) ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Low Frequency
lt 1 Hz
Static + Quasi-Static]:::low B[Body Bounce Peak
1.0 Hz
wb = 6.5 rad/s]:::bounce C[Iso-Band
2 to 10 Hz
Low Transmission]:::iso D[Wheel Hop Peak
11.4 Hz
ww = 71.6 rad/s]:::hop E[High Frequency
gt 15 Hz
Tyre Damping Dominant]:::high A --> B --> C --> D --> E classDef low fill:#ddd,color:#000 classDef bounce fill:#6cf,color:#000 classDef iso fill:#5f5,color:#000 classDef hop fill:#f66,color:#000 classDef high fill:#ddd,color:#000 ``` ## 4.5 Suspension-Tree Hierarchy ```mermaid flowchart TB Root[Vehicle Suspension]:::root L1[Single-Level
1 DOF]:::l1 L2[Two-Level
2 DOF]:::l2 L3[Three-Level
Active Sub-Frame]:::l3 Root --> L1 Root --> L2 Root --> L3 L2 --> L2a[Passive
Buses, Trucks]:::sub L2 --> L2b[Semi-Active
Sky-Hook Damping]:::sub L2 --> L2c[Active
Active Body Control]:::sub classDef root fill:#222,color:#fff classDef l1 fill:#fc9,color:#000 classDef l2 fill:#9cf,color:#000 classDef l3 fill:#c9f,color:#000 classDef sub fill:#fff,color:#000,stroke-dasharray: 3 3 ``` ## 4.6 Mode-Shape Vectors (Visual) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph BodyBounce BB1[Body Xb LARGE]:::bmove BB2[Wheel Xw approx 0]:::bstill end subgraph WheelHop WH1[Body Xb approx 0]:::hstill WH2[Wheel Xw LARGE]:::hmove end classDef bmove fill:#6cf,color:#000 classDef bstill fill:#ccc,color:#000 classDef hstill fill:#ccc,color:#000 classDef hmove fill:#f66,color:#000 ``` # 📝 Section 5 — Extended Question Bank & Recap ## 5.4 Additional Part-A Practice Questions (3 marks each) **Q3.** *Why is a two-level suspension preferred over a single-level for a passenger bus?* *(Expected: load-carrying vs ride-comfort decoupling, two independent design freedoms.)* **Q4.** *Define the term "isolation band" in a two-level suspension.* *(Expected: frequency gap between $\omega_b$ and $\omega_w$ where no resonance occurs.)* **Q5.** *What is meant by "sky-hook damping" and how is it approximated in a two-level system?* *(Expected: ideal damper to inertial reference; approximated by $c_2$ at body-bounce frequency.)* **Q6.** *State the decoupling condition for the two natural frequencies of a two-level system.* *(Expected: $k_1 \gg k_2$, leading to $\omega_b \approx \sqrt{k_2/m_b}$ and $\omega_w \approx \sqrt{(k_1+k_2)/m_w}$.)* ## 5.5 Quick-Reference Mnemonics 🧠 | Mnemonic | Meaning | |---|---| | **"Soft on Top, Stiff on Bottom"** | Secondary stage (top) is soft for comfort; primary (bottom) is stiffer for road holding. | | **"Body = Big mass, Low frequency"** | $m_b$ is large → $\omega_b$ small. | | **"Wheel = Small mass, High frequency"** | $m_w$ is small → $\omega_w$ large. | | **"Bounce = Body, Hop = Wheel"** | Memory hook for the two modes. | | **"Series = Divide by sum"** | $k_{eq} = k_1 k_2 / (k_1 + k_2)$ — the series rule. | ## 5.6 Final Recap Checklist ✅ - [x] Two-level = primary + secondary spring-damper, modelled as 2-DOF. - [x] EOMs derived and written in matrix form. - [x] Characteristic equation solved → $\omega_b, \omega_w$ formula. - [x] Decoupling when $k_1 \gg k_2$ gives simple approximations. - [x] Damping ratios: $\zeta_b$ uses $c_2$; $\zeta_w$ uses $c_1 + c_2$. - [x] Series equivalent stiffness, static deflection, isolation band. - [x] Applications: buses, trucks, rail, military, sub-frame cars. - [x] Exam-ready: 2 Part-A + 2 Part-B questions with full solutions and valuation tips. - [x] Bonus: Bode plot, mode shapes, worked numerical. - [x] Pitfalls flagged: sign errors, series/parallel confusion, units, damping formula choice.

topics.title: Split Level and Articulated Bus
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT413 - VEHICLE BODY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Split Level and Articulated Bus

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Split-Level Bus (SLB)

A **Split-Level Bus** is a special type of double-deck passenger bus in which the upper deck is **partial in length** and is offset from the lower deck, creating a "split" or staggered floor arrangement. The upper deck usually covers only **60 % – 70 %** of the lower deck length, with the driver's compartment integrated into the lower deck at the front.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PEMUT413 – Module 2):**
> *A split-level bus is a long-distance touring bus where the upper deck is shorter than the lower deck and placed above and slightly ahead of it, providing segregated driver cabin, panoramic upper-deck viewing, and large luggage hold on the lower deck.*

### Intuitive Analogy
Imagine a **two-storey cinema balcony** — the upper seats are pulled forward and only span the middle 2/3 of the hall, leaving a clear high ceiling over the rear rows. A split-level bus works on exactly the same idea: the **upper "balcony"** offers elevated, panoramic views, while the **lower deck** remains a full-length, full-height space used for passenger seating and luggage.

### Key Physical / Regulatory Constants
- Overall length: **10 m – 12 m** (single-deck envelope)
- Overall width: **2.50 m** (max permissible in India as per AIS-052)
- Overall height: **3.40 m – 3.80 m** (must clear 4.5 m flyovers)
- Upper-deck length: **≈ 65 %** of total wheelbase
- Floor height (lower): **0.40 m – 0.55 m** above ground
- Floor height (upper): **2.10 m – 2.40 m** above lower floor
- Internal headroom: **≥ 1.80 m** on each deck (mandatory under CMV Rules)

## 1.2 Articulated Bus

An **Articulated Bus** is a high-capacity passenger bus consisting of **two (or more) rigid sections** permanently connected by a **flexible articulated joint** (turntable or bellows cover), allowing the rear section to pivot relative to the front during cornering. It is sometimes called a **"bendy bus"** in the UK and a **"accordion bus"** in North America.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PEMUT413 – Module 2):**
> *An articulated bus is a long, high-capacity bus whose body is divided into two or more rigid sections connected by a flexible joint, providing 30 % – 50 % higher passenger capacity than a standard 12 m bus while retaining manoeuvrability.*

### Intuitive Analogy
Think of an articulated bus like a **centipede's body** — a leading "head section" carries the driver, engine, and front axle, and a "tail section" follows, but the two are linked by a soft, flexible waist (the articulation joint) that allows the rear to swing around tight corners just like a tail following the head. Another everyday analogy: a **long trailer truck (semi-trailer)** — same principle of a tractor pulling a pivoting load.

### Key Physical / Regulatory Constants
- Overall length: **15 m – 18 m** (standard); up to **24 m – 25 m** (bi-articulated)
- Overall width: **2.50 m – 2.55 m**
- Number of axles: **3** (one front + one centre + one rear) for 18 m
- Passenger capacity: **120 – 200 seated+standing** (standard); **200 – 250** (bi-articulated)
- Articulation joint angle: **± 45° minimum** (AIS-052 compliance)
- Minimum turning radius: **≈ 12 m**

### Comparative Snapshot

| Parameter | Split-Level Bus | Articulated Bus |
|---|---|---|
| Primary Application | Long-distance / Tourism / Intercity | Urban mass transit (BRT) |
| Capacity | 45 – 55 seated | 120 – 200 (P + S) |
| Length | 10 – 12 m | 15 – 18 m |
| Decks | 2 (partial upper) | 1 (continuous) |
| Flexibility | Rigid single frame | Articulated joint |
| Standing Allowed | Usually no (luxury coach) | Yes (urban transit) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Split-Level Bus — Structural Logic

The split-level architecture resolves a fundamental ergonomic conflict: the driver needs a **forward, low-seated cabin** with good road visibility, while passengers on the upper deck want **panoramic forward visibility**. The split solves this by:

1. **Staggering the upper deck forward** so it sits above the driver (no driver dome obstruction).
2. **Leaving the rear of the roof free** for air-conditioning, luggage racks, and a tall engine access hatch.
3. **Allowing a single curved staircase** (usually 8–10 steps, 170 mm rise) instead of a cramped vertical ladder.
4. **Providing a long luggage boot** on the lower deck's rear half (typical capacity: 6 m³ – 9 m³).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why partial upper deck?** A full-length upper deck would (a) raise the centre of gravity dangerously for cornering at highway speeds, (b) require structural reinforcement over the entire length, and (c) block the engine cooling airflow from the rear.

### 2.1.1 The "Why" and "How" — Operating Logic

- **Why offset forward?** To keep the **roll centre low** at the rear axle, where most luggage is stowed.
- **How is it built?** The body is a **monocoque stressed-skin** of rolled-steel rectangular hollow sections (RHS) with aluminium or FRP cladding panels. The upper deck is essentially a "second floor" welded to the lower-deck cant rails.
- **How is stability ensured?** By careful **location of the centre of gravity (CG)** above the rear axle and use of **anti-roll bars** on the front and (optional) tag axle.

## 2.2 Articulated Bus — Structural Logic

The articulated bus solves the **urban capacity problem** without resorting to very long rigid buses (which are illegal in many cities due to turning-circle restrictions).

### 2.2.1 The Articulation Joint — Anatomy

The joint consists of three sub-systems:
1. **Turntable assembly** — two large, greased bearings allowing rotation of the rear section about a vertical axis.
2. **Bellows (accordion) cover** — a rubberised or PVC fabric sleeve sealing the gap and protecting passengers from dust, rain, and pinch-points.
3. **Tie-bars / Scissor links** — to transfer longitudinal traction and braking forces from the rear section to the front.

### 2.2.2 Operational Logic

- **Why articulated?** Because a **single 18 m rigid bus** has a turning circle of > 24 m, unsuitable for city streets. An **18 m articulated bus** turns in < 12 m because the rear section simply *follows the curve*.
- **How is steering done?** Only the **front axle** of the leading section is steered. The centre and rear axles are fixed.
- **How is weight distributed?** The front axle carries ≈ 30 %, centre axle ≈ 35 %, and rear (tag) axle ≈ 35 % of the laden weight.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are board-exam favourites for PEMUT413 Module 2.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Notes / Boundary Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Longitudinal CG location | $x_{CG} = \dfrac{\sum_{i} W_i \, x_i}{\sum_{i} W_i}$ | m | $W_i$ = mass of $i^{th}$ component, $x_i$ = distance from datum |
| 2 | Vertical CG (parallel-axis) | $z_{CG} = \dfrac{\sum W_i \, z_i}{\sum W_i}$ | m | Datum = ground |
| 3 | Total passenger capacity | $N_{total} = N_{seats} + N_{stand}$ | persons | $N_{stand} = 0.25 \, \text{m}^2/\text{pax}$ × aisle area |
| 4 | Axle-weight distribution | $W_f + W_c + W_r = W_{total}$ | kg | $W_f \le 0.20 \, W_{total}$ (legal limit, India) |
| 5 | Static front-axle load | $W_f = W_{total} \dfrac{L - a}{L}$ | kg | $a$ = CG distance from front axle, $L$ = wheelbase |
| 6 | Minimum turning radius (articulated) | $R_{min} = \dfrac{L_f}{2 \sin\theta_{max}}$ | m | $\theta_{max} \le 45°$ articulation |
| 7 | Floor area per passenger | $A_{pax} = 0.30 \, \text{m}^2$ (seated) | m² | AIS-052 norm |
| 8 | Luggage volume (split-level) | $V_{lug} = A_{boot} \times h_{boot}$ | m³ | Typical 6 m³ – 9 m³ |
| 9 | Power-to-weight ratio | $PWR = \dfrac{P_{engine}}{W_{total}}$ | kW/tonne | 8 – 12 kW/t for transit bus |
| 10 | Body roll gradient | $\phi = \dfrac{m_s \, a_y \, h_{CG}}{K_\phi}$ | rad | $K_\phi$ = roll stiffness of suspension |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In all exam answers, **always state units** and **mention the standard / code** (e.g., AIS-052, CMV Rule 110) you are applying. KTU examiners award 0.5 – 1 mark extra for citing the regulation correctly.

### Real-World Utility
- The **CG calculation** is critical during **bus rollover investigations** (RTO cases) and **homologation testing**.
- The **turning-radius formula** dictates whether an articulated bus can be deployed on a given BRT corridor without redesigning intersections.
- The **axle-load split** is the basis of **bridge / culvert design** for bus depots and terminal bays.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Longitudinal Centre of Gravity of a Split-Level Bus

### Problem Setup
A split-level bus has the following component masses and their distances from the front bumper (datum = front of vehicle):

| Component | Mass $W_i$ (kg) | Distance $x_i$ (m) |
|---|---|---|
| Lower-deck structure + seats | 2 500 | 2.0 |
| Upper-deck structure + seats | 1 200 | 3.5 |
| Chassis frame + drivetrain | 1 800 | 1.5 |
| Driver + 1 attendant | 95 | 0.4 |

The wheelbase is $L = 5.6$ m. Determine:
(a) The longitudinal CG location $x_{CG}$ measured from the front bumper.
(b) The static load on the front axle.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Sum of moments about the front bumper (datum).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{i} W_i \, x_i &= W_1 x_1 + W_2 x_2 + W_3 x_3 + W_4 x_4 \\
&= (2500 \times 2.0) + (1200 \times 3.5) + (1800 \times 1.5) + (95 \times 0.4) \\
&= 5000 + 4200 + 2700 + 38 \\
&= 11\,938 \;\; \text{kg·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Sum of all component masses.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{i} W_i &= 2500 + 1200 + 1800 + 95 \\
&= 5\,595 \;\; \text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the CG formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{CG} &= \frac{\sum W_i \, x_i}{\sum W_i} \\
&= \frac{11\,938}{5\,595} \\
&= 2.13 \;\; \text{m from front bumper}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Distance of CG from front axle.**

The front axle is at $a = 1.4$ m from the front bumper (overhang).

$$
\begin{aligned}
a_{CG} &= x_{CG} - a \\
&= 2.13 - 1.4 \\
&= 0.73 \;\; \text{m behind the front axle}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Static front-axle load (taking moments about rear axle).**

The rear axle is at distance $L = 5.6$ m from the front axle, i.e., $5.6 - 1.4 = 4.2$ m from the front bumper.

Distance of CG from rear axle:
$$
\begin{aligned}
b_{CG} &= L - a_{CG} = 5.6 - 0.73 = 4.87 \;\; \text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_f &= W_{total} \times \frac{b_{CG}}{L} \\
&= 5595 \times \frac{4.87}{5.6} \\
&= 5595 \times 0.8696 \\
&= 4\,864 \;\; \text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Verify (rear-axle load).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_r &= W_{total} - W_f = 5595 - 4864 = 731 \;\; \text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The front-axle load is **86.96 %** of the total — far above the legal limit of 20 % for tag-axle-less buses. This signals that the **split-level configuration needs a tag axle or pusher axle** to remain road-legal. This kind of **commentary** is what distinguishes a KTU topper answer (≥ 13/14) from a 9/14 answer.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Passenger Capacity of an Articulated Bus

### Problem Setup
A 18 m articulated bus is to be designed for an Indian BRT corridor.
- Total interior floor length = 17.4 m
- Interior width = 2.40 m
- 2 + 2 transverse seating layout
- Aisle width = 0.55 m
- Required standee density = 4 persons / m² (peak urban crush load)
- The bus will have 2 emergency exits and 3 doors of effective width 1.1 m each.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Seated capacity.**

Each 2 + 2 row (4 seats) occupies 0.85 m longitudinally. Number of seat rows:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{rows} &= \frac{17.4}{0.85} \approx 20 \;\; \text{rows}
\end{aligned}
$$

Seated capacity:
$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{seats} &= 4 \times 20 = 80 \;\; \text{pax}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Standing area (aisle + vestibule at doors).**

Aisle area:
$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{aisle} &= 17.4 \times 0.55 = 9.57 \;\; \text{m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

Vestibule area (at 3 doors, each approx. 1.1 m × 1.5 m):
$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{vest} &= 3 \times (1.1 \times 1.5) = 4.95 \;\; \text{m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

Total standing area:
$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{stand} &= 9.57 + 4.95 = 14.52 \;\; \text{m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Standing passengers at 4 pax/m².**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{stand} &= 14.52 \times 4 \approx 58 \;\; \text{pax}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Total design capacity.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{total} &= N_{seats} + N_{stand} \\
&= 80 + 58 \\
&= 138 \;\; \text{pax}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Compare with a **standard 12 m rigid bus** (≈ 80 pax) → the articulated bus delivers **73 % more capacity** for only **50 % more length**, validating the BRT design choice.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Decision Logic

| Design Parameter | Split-Level Bus Choice | Articulated Bus Choice | Governing Trade-off |
|---|---|---|---|
| Operating speed | 80 – 100 km/h | 40 – 60 km/h | High-speed stability vs low-speed manoeuvrability |
| Floor height | 2 levels, fixed stairs | Single low-floor (≤ 400 mm) | Accessibility vs segregation |
| Staircase | Mandatory, ergonomic | None | Boarding time vs comfort |
| CG height | Higher (≈ 1.5 m) | Lower (≈ 0.9 m) | Rollover risk |
| Frame | Monocoque | Articulated semi-monocoque | Stiffness vs flexibility |
| Cost per seat (₹, indicative) | ₹ 1.5 – 2.0 L | ₹ 0.8 – 1.0 L | Capex vs capacity |
| Best application | Hill-station / Tourist / Intercity | Urban BRT / Metro-feeder | Comfort vs throughput |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid block diagrams illustrate the **functional architecture and flow** of the split-level and articulated bus systems. Node IDs are alphanumeric (per protocol) and labels are kept in clean uppercase text.

## 4.1 Split-Level Bus — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
    A1[DRIVER CABIN L0] --> B1[LOWER DECK Aisle L1]
    B1 --> C1[STAIRCASE Mid Vehicle]
    C1 --> D1[UPPER DECK Forward Offset]
    B1 --> E1[REAR LOWER DECK L1]
    E1 --> F1[LUGGAGE BOOT L1 Rear]
    E1 --> G1[ENGINE HATCH Roof L1]
    D1 --> H1[PANORAMIC ROOF HATCH]
    B1 --> I1[ENTRY DOOR L1 Front]
    E1 --> J1[EMERGENCY EXIT L1 Rear]
    D1 --> K1[EMERGENCY EXIT L2 Rear]
    A1 -.steering control.-> L1[FRONT AXLE]
    E1 -.weight transfer.-> M1[REAR OR TAG AXLE]
```

## 4.2 Articulated Bus — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
    P1[FRONT SECTION Driver Cabin] --> Q1[ARTICULATION JOINT Turntable and Bellows]
    Q1 --> R1[REAR SECTION Passenger Saloon]
    P1 --> S1[FRONT AXLE Steered]
    P1 --> T1[CENTRE AXLE Tag]
    R1 --> U1[REAR AXLE Drive]
    P1 --> V1[DOOR 1 Front Entry]
    P1 --> W1[DOOR 2 Mid Exit]
    R1 --> X1[DOOR 3 Rear Exit]
    R1 --> Y1[ROOF MOUNTED AC and HVAC]
    Q1 -.longitudinal load path.-> P1
    Q1 -.rotational freedom 45 deg.-> R1
```

## 4.3 Articulation Joint — Sub-System Detail

```mermaid
graph LR
    J1[TURNTABLE BEARING Upper Plate] --> J2[CENTRE KING PIN Vertical Axis]
    J2 --> J3[TURNTABLE BEARING Lower Plate]
    J3 --> J4[FRAME OF REAR SECTION]
    J1 --> J5[FRAME OF FRONT SECTION]
    J6[BELLOWS COVER Rubberised PVC] --- J2
    J7[SCISSOR TIE BAR Pair] --- J2
    J8[FLOOR PLATE Transition] --- J2
```

## 4.4 Comparative Selection Flow — Which Bus to Choose?

```mermaid
graph TD
    ST1[START: Define Route Requirement] --> ST2{Operating Speed more than 70 kmph}
    ST2 -- YES --> ST3{Long Distance and Tourist Use}
    ST2 -- NO --> ST4{Urban Mass Transit}
    ST3 -- YES --> ST5[CHOOSE SPLIT LEVEL BUS]
    ST3 -- NO --> ST6[CHOOSE STANDARD SINGLE DECK]
    ST4 -- YES --> ST7{Passenger Demand more than 100 pax per bus}
    ST4 -- NO --> ST8[CHOOSE STANDARD SINGLE DECK]
    ST7 -- YES --> ST9[CHOOSE ARTICULATED BUS]
    ST7 -- NO --> ST10[CHOOSE LOW FLOOR SINGLE DECK]
    ST5 --> STEND[END Selection]
    ST6 --> STEND
    ST8 --> STEND
    ST9 --> STEND
    ST10 --> STEND
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Differentiate between a split-level bus and a conventional double-decker bus with respect to upper-deck length, application, and structural arrangement.**

**Model Answer:**
- A **conventional double-decker** has its upper deck spanning the **entire length** of the lower deck, whereas a **split-level bus** has its upper deck covering only **60 % – 70 %** of the lower deck length, offset forward.
- **Application:** Double-deckers are typically used for **urban transit** (e.g., London Routemaster, BEST Mumbai); split-level buses are used for **long-distance touring, intercity, and hill-station routes** (e.g., KSRTC Airavat, Volvo Multi-Axle).
- **Structural arrangement:** In a double-decker, the upper deck is supported by vertical pillars at both front and rear; in a split-level bus, the rear of the upper deck is **cantilevered** over the lower deck and supported by a single arch frame.
- The split-level configuration leaves a **clear rear roof** for luggage boot, AC, and engine access — a feature absent in full double-deckers.

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**List any three functional requirements of the articulation joint in an articulated bus.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Allow ± 45° (minimum) rotational freedom** in the horizontal plane between the front and rear sections, to enable tight urban turns.
2. **Transfer longitudinal traction and braking forces** from the rear section to the front (via tie-bars / scissor links), so the rear section does not push or pull freely.
3. **Provide a sealed, weather-proof passenger passage** between the two sections, using a bellows / accordion rubber cover, while remaining **pinch-free and wheelchair-accessible**.
4. (Bonus) Be **maintenance-friendly** with greasable bearings accessible from inside the saloon.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern:** Answer **any ONE** full question. Each full question has two sub-parts, typically 7 + 7 marks. Sub-parts may have further sub-divisions (e.g., (i), (ii)). Always draw **labelled diagrams** for ≥ 3 marks per sub-part.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** With the help of a functional block diagram, explain the **constructional features of a split-level bus**. Discuss its **advantages and limitations** for long-distance operations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An articulated bus has the following axle loads at GVW: front axle = 6 800 kg, centre axle = 9 200 kg, rear axle = 8 500 kg. The wheelbase between front and centre axles is 5.5 m, and between centre and rear axles is 6.0 m. Calculate:
   (i) The **total GVW**.
   (ii) The **longitudinal CG location** measured from the front axle.
   **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a)

A split-level bus has the following constructional features:

1. **Lower deck** — full-length saloon, 2 + 2 seating, with a longitudinal aisle. Floor height above ground ≈ 0.85 m (on a high-floor chassis) or 0.4 m (low-entry variant).
2. **Upper deck** — partial length (≈ 65 %), positioned **forward of the lower-deck centre**, with 1 + 1 or 2 + 1 transverse seating. Headroom ≥ 1.80 m.
3. **Staircase** — internal, helical or dog-legged, with 8–10 risers of 170 mm each, located just behind the front axle.
4. **Driver cabin** — integrated into the front of the lower deck, with a curved windscreen and twin A-pillars.
5. **Luggage boot** — large rear lower-deck compartment, 6 m³ – 9 m³ capacity, accessible via external side lockers.
6. **Structure** — monocoque stressed-skin of rolled-steel RHS frame with aluminium skin panels.

**Advantages for long-distance operation:**
- Panoramic upper-deck view enhances tourist appeal.
- Large luggage volume for long-haul passengers.
- Driver ergonomics are excellent due to the dedicated cabin.
- Better high-speed stability than a full double-decker (lower rear CG).

**Limitations:**
- Two staircases slow boarding and egress.
- Cannot use low-bridge routes (< 4.5 m clearance).
- Higher centre of gravity than single-deck.
- Higher manufacturing cost per seat.

(Include a labelled Mermaid / sketch showing all six features — mandatory for 7-mark answer.)

#### Model Solution — (b)

**Step 1 — Total GVW.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{total} &= W_f + W_c + W_r \\
&= 6800 + 9200 + 8500 \\
&= 24\,500 \;\; \text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Take moments about the front axle to find CG.**

Let the CG be at distance $x_{CG}$ from the front axle, along the longitudinal direction.

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_c \cdot L_1 + W_r \cdot (L_1 + L_2) &= W_{total} \cdot x_{CG} \\
(9200 \times 5.5) + (8500 \times 11.5) &= 24\,500 \times x_{CG} \\
50\,600 + 97\,750 &= 24\,500 \, x_{CG} \\
148\,350 &= 24\,500 \, x_{CG} \\
x_{CG} &= \frac{148\,350}{24\,500} \\
x_{CG} &= 6.055 \;\; \text{m from the front axle}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification (sanity check):** Since the wheelbase total is 11.5 m, $x_{CG} = 6.055$ m is roughly mid-way → physically consistent. The CG lies between the centre and rear axles (5.5 m and 11.5 m), closer to the centre axle, which carries the highest load — consistent.

**Final Answer:**
- $W_{total} = 24\,500$ kg
- $x_{CG} = 6.06$ m from the front axle (≈ 0.56 m behind the centre axle)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram the **working principle, types, and advantages of an articulated bus** for urban mass transit. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A split-level bus has lower-deck weight 2 800 kg located 2.2 m from the front bumper, upper-deck weight 1 400 kg at 3.8 m, chassis 1 600 kg at 1.6 m, and crew of 110 kg at 0.5 m. The front axle is 1.4 m behind the front bumper; wheelbase is 5.8 m. Calculate:
   (i) The **longitudinal CG** from the front bumper.
   (ii) The **static load on the front and rear axles**.
   **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a)

An **articulated bus** consists of two rigid sections joined by a flexible joint.

**Working principle:** Only the **front axle of the leading section** is steered. During a turn, the **rear section pivots** about the vertical king-pin of the articulation joint, faithfully following the path of the front section. This allows the bus to negotiate a 90° turn within ≈ 12 m, despite an overall length of 18 m.

**Types of articulated buses:**
1. **Two-section (standard)** — 15 – 18 m, 3 axles, capacity 120 – 180 pax.
2. **Bi-articulated (three-section)** — 24 – 25 m, 4 axles, capacity 200 – 250 pax.
3. **Tractor-type** — the rear section has its own powered rear axle ("pusher").
4. **Trailer-type** — the rear section is unpowered and towed.
5. By fuel: **Diesel, CNG, Hybrid, Electric (trolley / battery)**.
6. By floor: **High-floor, Low-floor, Ultra-low-floor (≤ 320 mm).**

**Advantages for urban mass transit:**
- 50 % more length → 70 % more capacity → fewer buses needed.
- Lower cost per passenger-km.
- Faster boarding / alighting at low-floor doors.
- Excellent manoeuvrability in dense city traffic.
- Modular: rear section can be detachable for maintenance.

**Disadvantages:**
- Higher initial cost (≈ 1.4× a 12 m rigid bus).
- Articulation joint maintenance and replacement of bellows.
- Depot / workshop infrastructure must accommodate longer length.
- Not ideal for narrow heritage-city streets.

(Include a **labelled block diagram** of the articulation joint — mandatory.)

#### Model Solution — (b)

**Step 1 — Sum of moments about the front bumper.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum W_i x_i &= (2800 \times 2.2) + (1400 \times 3.8) + (1600 \times 1.6) + (110 \times 0.5) \\
&= 6160 + 5320 + 2560 + 55 \\
&= 14\,095 \;\; \text{kg·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Total mass.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum W_i &= 2800 + 1400 + 1600 + 110 = 5\,910 \;\; \text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — CG from front bumper.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{CG} &= \frac{14\,095}{5\,910} = 2.385 \;\; \text{m from front bumper}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — CG from front axle.**

Front axle is 1.4 m behind the front bumper:

$$
\begin{aligned}
a_{CG} &= x_{CG} - 1.4 = 2.385 - 1.4 = 0.985 \;\; \text{m behind front axle}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Distance of CG from rear axle.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
b_{CG} &= L - a_{CG} = 5.8 - 0.985 = 4.815 \;\; \text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Front-axle static load.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_f &= W_{total} \times \frac{b_{CG}}{L} \\
&= 5910 \times \frac{4.815}{5.8} \\
&= 5910 \times 0.8302 \\
&= 4\,907 \;\; \text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Rear-axle static load.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_r &= W_{total} - W_f = 5910 - 4907 = 1\,003 \;\; \text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall / Common Mark-Deduction Points:**
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract the front overhang** when computing "CG from front axle". Many students write $x_{CG} = 2.385$ m as "CG from front axle" — WRONG; subtract 1.4 m to get 0.985 m.
> 2. **Mixing up the formula** $W_f = W_{total} \cdot b/L$ with $W_f = W_{total} \cdot a/L$. The lever arm for the **front** axle is the distance from the **rear** axle (i.e., $b$).
> 3. **Omitting units** in numerical answers → 0.5 mark deduction per sub-part.
> 4. **Not stating the standard / code** (AIS-052, CMV Rule 110) in descriptive answers → 1 mark deduction.
> 5. **Skipping the labelled diagram** in a 7-mark descriptive sub-part → 2 mark deduction guaranteed.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Read this 5 minutes before the exam.**

- ✅ **Split-Level Bus (SLB):** Two staggered decks; upper deck = **65 %** of lower-deck length, offset **forward**; partial upper deck → panoramic view + luggage hold.
- ✅ **Articulated Bus:** Two (or three) rigid sections joined by a **turntable + bellows**; only the **front axle** is steered; **± 45° articulation** minimum.
- ✅ **Key Formulas to Memorise:**
  - $x_{CG} = \dfrac{\sum W_i x_i}{\sum W_i}$ (longitudinal CG)
  - $W_f = W_{total} \cdot \dfrac{b}{L}$ (front-axle static load)
  - $R_{min} = \dfrac{L_f}{2 \sin\theta_{max}}$ (turning radius, articulated)
  - $N_{total} = N_{seats} + N_{stand}$
- ✅ **Indian Regulations to Quote:** AIS-052 (Bus Body Construction), CMV Rule 110 (Dimensions), CMV Rule 119 (Brakes).
- ✅ **Typical Capacities:** SLB = 45 – 55 seated; Articulated = 120 – 200 pax (seated + standing).
- ✅ **Typical Lengths:** SLB = 10 – 12 m; Articulated = 15 – 18 m; Bi-articulated = 24 – 25 m.
- ✅ **Max Width (India):** **2.50 m** (some states 2.55 m) — quote AIS-052.
- ✅ **Key Sub-Systems of Articulation Joint:** Turntable bearing, king pin, bellows, tie-bars, floor plate — remember all five.
- ✅ **Advantages Recap:**
  - SLB → tourist comfort, luggage, highway stability.
  - Articulated → urban capacity, manoeuvrability, low cost/passenger-km.
- ✅ **Examiner Loves:** Labelled diagrams, numeric clarity, unit consistency, regulation citation, and a concluding sentence stating the **application context** (e.g., "…hence suitable for BRT corridors operating at 50 kmph with peak demand of 150 pax per bus").
- ✅ **Common Mistakes to Avoid:** Forgetting the front overhang, mixing $a$ and $b$, omitting the diagram, ignoring units, and writing "double-decker" when the question says "split-level".

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Floor Height
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT413 - VEHICLE BODY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Floor Height

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Floor Height** is a fundamental vertical dimensional parameter in vehicle body engineering, defined as the perpendicular distance measured from the ground level (datum surface) to the **upper surface of the floor pan** (finished floor surface) of the vehicle, measured at a specific reference point along the vehicle's longitudinal axis (usually between the axles or at the entrance step area).

$$H_{floor} = H_{ground\,clearance} + H_{chassis/frame} + H_{suspension\,components} + t_{floor\,pan}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Floor height is studied under the broader umbrella of *vehicle dimensional layout, passenger ergonomics, and body architecture*. It directly influences step-in height, entry-exit kinematics, and overall vehicle silhouette.

### 1.2 Classification of Floor Heights (Vehicle-wise)

| Vehicle Category | Typical Floor Height Range | Application |
|------------------|---------------------------|-------------|
| **Passenger Cars (Sedan)** | 180 – 280 mm | Low CG, aerodynamic |
| **SUVs / Off-roaders** | 250 – 400 mm | Higher ground clearance |
| **Standard City Buses** | 750 – 900 mm | Two-step entry |
| **Low-Floor Buses** | 340 – 380 mm | Single-step / kneeling |
| **Ultra-Low-Floor Buses** | 200 – 320 mm | Fully accessible (PRT/US) |
| **LCV / Trucks (Cab)** | 800 – 1100 mm | Frame-mounted cab |
| **Heavy Trucks (Sleeper cab)** | 1100 – 1500 mm | High-mounted cabin |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy:** Think of **floor height** as the "**height of a stage**" in a theatre. A low stage (like a low-floor bus) makes it easy for anyone — including a child or an elderly person with a walking stick — to step onto it. A high stage (like a traditional Indian State Transport bus) requires climbing stairs, which is harder but offers advantages like better mechanical packaging underneath (like drums under the stage).

Imagine a vehicle as a **table** standing on **legs (wheels)**. The *table top* is the floor. The *leg length* is the suspension + chassis. The *gap below the table* is the ground clearance. The **floor height** is simply the height of that table top from the floor (ground). You can either:
- Make the **legs shorter** → Low-floor design (compromises mechanical space below)
- Make the **legs taller** → High-floor design (gives space for transmission tunnel, fuel tank, etc.)

### 1.4 Why Floor Height Matters in Design

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three primary drivers** govern floor height selection in any KTU examination problem:
> 1. **Functional (Packaging):** Must clear propeller shaft, differential, fuel tank, exhaust.
> 2. **Human Factors (Ergonomics):** Determines step-in height, ease of boarding, ADA/RTI compliance.
> 3. **Regulatory:** AIS-052 (Bus body building code), ECE R107, FMVSS standards for entry steps.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Logic of Floor Height Determination

**Step 1 — Establish the Ground Datum**
The reference plane is the **flat, level ground** on which the vehicle rests in its **kerb (unladen) condition**.

**Step 2 — Identify the Vertical Stacking**
From the ground upward, the typical stack-up is:
1. Tire radius (unloaded) → $R_t$
2. Suspension bump clearance → $\delta_s$ (typically 50–80 mm)
3. Lowest mechanical component (prop shaft, axle, exhaust, fuel tank) → $H_{mech}$
4. Floor pan cross-section depth → $t_{fp}$ (60–120 mm)
5. Floor covering / carpet → $t_{cov}$ (10–25 mm)

**Step 3 — Combine Components**
The **minimum required floor height** is the sum of the above. Any increase above this minimum is a *designer's choice* balancing ergonomics vs. mechanical packaging.

**Step 4 — Apply Ergonomic Constraints**
For passenger vehicles, the **maximum comfortable first-step height** (from ground to first step) is governed by anthropometric data:
- 5th percentile female popliteal height ≈ **380 mm**
- 95th percentile male step-climb capability ≈ **500 mm**
- Recommended first-step height: **≤ 400 mm** (AIS-052)

**Step 5 — Iterate Against Packaging**
If ergonomic target (low floor) clashes with mechanical packaging (prop shaft, fuel tank), the designer must:
- Move mechanicals to wheel wells
- Use a *low-floor axle* (portal axle, independent suspension with dropped beam)
- Adopt a *rear-engine* or *electric powertrain* layout

### 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Step

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is floor height NOT simply a constant?** Because the ground is not flat relative to the car — wheel rotation, suspension travel, and tire deflection all change the *effective* floor height dynamically. KTU examiners love asking about **kerb vs. laden** floor heights.

**How low-floor buses achieve it:**
- Use **portal axles** (input shaft above wheel center, output at hub)
- Place **motor and gearbox transversely** between wheels
- Adopt **electric drivetrains** (skateboard chassis with flat battery floor)

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they appear in 70% of numerical questions on floor height.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $H_f = R_t + \delta_s + H_{mech} + t_{fp} + t_{cov}$ | Total floor height (stack-up) | mm |
| 2 | $H_{step} = H_f - n \cdot h_{riser}$ | Step height after $n$ risers | mm |
| 3 | $\theta_{climb} = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{H_{step}}{d_{foot}\cos\alpha}\right)$ | Step-climb leg angle (ergonomic) | degrees |
| 4 | $H_{ground} = H_f - H_{mech} - t_{fp}$ | Required ground clearance | mm |
| 5 | $H_{eff} = H_f - x \cdot \phi$ | Laden floor height (sag) | mm |
| 6 | $L_{ramp} = \dfrac{H_{step}}{\tan\theta_{safe}}$ | Wheelchair ramp length | mm |
| 7 | $n = \left\lceil \dfrac{H_f - H_{step1}}{h_{riser}} \right\rceil$ | Number of entrance steps | integer |
| 8 | $H_{CG} = H_f + \dfrac{t_{seat}}{2}$ | Approximate CG height (passenger cars) | mm |

**Where:**
- $R_t$ = tire static loaded radius
- $\delta_s$ = suspension bump clearance
- $H_{mech}$ = lowest mechanical component height
- $h_{riser}$ = individual step riser height (≤ 250 mm per AIS-052)
- $\phi$ = suspension sag under load (mm per unit load)
- $\theta_{safe}$ = safe ramp angle (≤ 1:12 for wheelchairs ≈ 4.76°)

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Bus body builders** (Olectra, Tata Starbus, Volvo B7RLE) — directly specify floor height to comply with urban transit tenders.
- **EV skateboard platforms** (Tata Punch EV, Hyundai E-GMP) — *battery floor* IS the floor; floor height is locked by pack thickness.
- **Aerodynamic cars** — lowering floor height reduces frontal area drag coefficient contribution.
- **Crash safety** — floor must be at a height that allows **crush space** during frontal impact (typically 250–350 mm of deformable zone).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Floor Height from First Principles (Stack-Up)

**Problem Setup:** A standard city bus has the following data. Compute its floor height.

- Tire static loaded radius: $R_t = 510$ mm
- Suspension bump clearance: $\delta_s = 75$ mm
- Propeller shaft tunnel height (above chassis): $H_{tunnel} = 180$ mm
- Chassis frame top height: $H_{frame} = 120$ mm
- Floor pan depth: $t_{fp} = 90$ mm
- Floor covering (PVC): $t_{cov} = 15$ mm

**Step 1 — Define each layer from ground upward.**

The vertical stack is:
$$\text{Ground} \rightarrow \text{Tire} \rightarrow \text{Suspension} \rightarrow \text{Chassis} \rightarrow \text{Floor pan} \rightarrow \text{Surface}$$

**Step 2 — Express the height of the lowest mechanical component.**

The prop shaft tunnel is built ABOVE the chassis frame:
$$H_{mech} = H_{frame} + H_{tunnel} = 120 + 180 = 300 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the master floor-height equation.**

$$H_f = R_t + \delta_s + H_{mech} + t_{fp} + t_{cov}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the values.**

$$H_f = 510 + 75 + 300 + 90 + 15$$

**Step 5 — Perform the arithmetic step-by-step.**

$$\begin{aligned}
H_f &= 510 + 75 \quad &\text{(tire + suspension)} \\
    &= 585 \\
H_f &= 585 + 300 \quad &\text{(+ mech stack)} \\
    &= 885 \\
H_f &= 885 + 90 \quad &\text{(+ floor pan)} \\
    &= 975 \\
H_f &= 975 + 15 \quad &\text{(+ covering)} \\
    &= 990 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Answer:** $H_f = 990$ mm — typical of a **standard Indian floor city bus**.

### 3.2 Derivation: Number of Entrance Steps

**Given:** $H_f = 990$ mm, $H_{step1} = 380$ mm (ground to first step), $h_{riser} = 200$ mm max.

**Step 1 — Find the remaining height to climb after the first step.**

$$H_{remain} = H_f - H_{step1} = 990 - 380 = 610 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the step count equation.**

$$n_{additional} = \left\lceil \dfrac{H_{remain}}{h_{riser}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \dfrac{610}{200} \right\rceil$$

**Step 3 — Compute.**

$$n_{additional} = \lceil 3.05 \rceil = 4 \text{ steps}$$

**Step 4 — Total entrance steps (including the first).**

$$n_{total} = n_{additional} + 1 = 4 + 1 = 5 \text{ steps}$$

**Verification — last step riser:**

$$H_{last} = 380 + 4 \times 200 = 380 + 800 = 1180 \text{ mm}$$

This overshoots $H_f = 990$ mm, so the **last riser must be shorter**:
$$h_{last} = 990 - 380 - 3(200) = 990 - 380 - 600 = 10 \text{ mm}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students forget that the last step's riser is *adjusted downward* to match the floor height exactly. Always recompute the *last* riser separately.

### 3.3 Derivation: Conversion to Low-Floor Configuration

**Target:** Reduce $H_f$ from 990 mm to **340 mm** (ultra-low-floor bus).

**Step 1 — Identify removable elements.**

The mechanical stack (chassis + tunnel = 300 mm) must be eliminated or relocated.

**Step 2 — Adopt a portal axle.**

A portal axle raises the wheel centerline above the axle housing, *adding* ~120 mm of clearance beneath. Combined with an *electric transverse motor* (no prop shaft):

$$H_{mech,\,new} = 0 \text{ (no prop shaft, motor between wheels)}$$

**Step 3 — Recompute with low-floor components.**

$$\begin{aligned}
H_{f,\,new} &= R_t + \delta_s + H_{mech,\,new} + t_{fp} + t_{cov} \\
            &= 510 + 75 + 0 + 90 + 15 \\
            &= 690 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Apply kneeling suspension (optional).**

Kneeling lowers the bus further by ~80 mm at the entry door:
$$H_{f,\,kneel} = 690 - 80 = 610 \text{ mm}$$

> [!NOTE]
> To achieve the **340 mm** target, the bus must also use **smaller diameter wheels** (e.g., 17.5" instead of 22.5") and a **drop-centre frame** with the floor passing *through* the wheel arches.

**Final ultra-low-floor stack-up example (Volvo B7RLE / Tata Starbus EV):**

| Component | Height (mm) |
|-----------|-------------|
| Tire (275/70 R22.5 equivalent, low-profile) | 360 |
| Suspension (air, kneeling lowered) | 50 |
| Mechanical (motor between wheels, no prop shaft) | 0 |
| Floor pan | 90 |
| Covering | 15 |
| **Total $H_f$** | **~340** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Floor Height Stack-Up

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Ground[Ground Level Datum]
    Tire[Tire Static Loaded Radius Rt]
    Susp[Suspension Bump Clearance delta_s]
    Mech[Mechanical Stack Hmech]
    MechA[Propeller Shaft]
    MechB[Chassis Frame]
    MechC[Fuel Tank]
    MechD[Exhaust System]
    Pan[Floor Pan Depth tfp]
    Cov[Floor Covering tcov]
    Floor[FINAL FLOOR HEIGHT Hf]
    Ergo[Ergonomic Constraint]
    Reg[Regulatory Constraint AIS-052]
    Pack[Mechanical Packaging]

    Ground --> Tire --> Susp --> Mech --> Pan --> Cov --> Floor
    Mech --> MechA
    Mech --> MechB
    Mech --> MechC
    Mech --> MechD
    Ergo -.constrains.-> Floor
    Reg -.constrains.-> Floor
    Pack -.constrains.-> Mech
```

### 4.2 Schematic: Step Geometry for a 3-Step Entry Bus

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Ground Level 0 mm] -->|Riser 1: 250 mm| B[Step 1: 250 mm]
    B -->|Tread 250 mm| B1[Foot Space]
    B1 -->|Riser 2: 230 mm| C[Step 2: 480 mm]
    C -->|Tread 230 mm| C1[Foot Space]
    C1 -->|Riser 3: 220 mm| D[Floor Level 700 mm]
    D -->|Aisle| E[Passenger Cabin]
```

### 4.3 Decision Matrix: High-Floor vs Low-Floor vs Ultra-Low-Floor

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start{Vehicle Type?}
    Start -->|Passenger Car| Car[Low floor 180 to 280 mm\nDirect mounting on body shell]
    Start -->|Standard Bus| BusStd[High floor 750 to 900 mm\n2 to 3 step entry\nConventional chassis]
    Start -->|City Bus Low Entry| BusLow[Low floor 340 to 380 mm\n1 step or kneeling\nPortal axle or monocoque]
    Start -->|Electric City Bus| BusULF[Ultra low floor 200 to 340 mm\nWheelchair accessible\nSkateboard battery floor]
    Start -->|Truck| Truck[High floor 800 to 1500 mm\nFrame mounted cab\nAir suspension]

    Car --> CarAdv[Advantages: low CG, aero, ease entry]
    Car --> CarDis[Disadvantages: low ground clearance]
    BusStd --> BusStdAdv[Advantages: simple chassis, durable]
    BusStd --> BusStdDis[Disadvantages: hard boarding, slow dwell time]
```

### 4.4 Component Interaction Map (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph Inputs
        A1[Tire Size]
        A2[Suspension Type]
        A3[Drivetrain Layout]
    end
    subgraph Constraints
        B1[Ground Clearance]
        B2[Step in Height AIS 052]
        B3[Headroom H35]
    end
    subgraph Output
        C1[Floor Height Hf]
        C2[Number of Steps n]
        C3[CG Height]
    end

    A1 --> C1
    A2 --> C1
    A3 --> C1
    B1 --> C1
    B2 --> C2
    C1 --> C2
    C1 --> C3

    style A1 fill:#e1f5ff
    style A2 fill:#e1f5ff
    style A3 fill:#e1f5ff
    style B1 fill:#fff4e1
    style B2 fill:#fff4e1
    style B3 fill:#fff4e1
    style C1 fill:#e1ffe1
    style C2 fill:#e1ffe1
    style C3 fill:#e1ffe1
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
*Define the term "Floor Height" as used in vehicle body engineering. Mention any two factors that influence its selection.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Floor height is the vertical distance measured from the ground (datum) to the **upper surface of the finished floor** of the vehicle body, typically measured between the axles in kerb (unladen) condition.

**Two influencing factors (½ mark each):**
1. **Ground clearance requirement** — must clear road undulations and mechanicals.
2. **Ergonomic step-in height** — should be ≤ 400 mm for first step (AIS-052 / anthropometric compliance).

> [!TIP]
> Examiners award the full 3 marks *only* if the definition is dimensionally precise (mention "ground to upper floor surface"). A vague answer loses 1 mark.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]**
*Differentiate between a "high-floor" and a "low-floor" bus. State one advantage of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | High-Floor Bus | Low-Floor Bus |
|-----------|---------------|---------------|
| Floor height | 750 – 900 mm | 340 – 380 mm |
| Entry steps | 2 – 4 steps | 1 step / kneeling |
| Passenger type | General | Disabled, elderly-friendly |

**Advantage of high-floor (1 mark):** Simpler, more durable conventional chassis; easier mechanical packaging.
**Advantage of low-floor (1 mark):** Faster boarding/alighting, reduced dwell time at stops, universal accessibility.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 | Model Paper 2]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the various factors that determine the **floor height of a passenger bus**. Discuss how each factor influences the final design. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A standard city bus has the following specifications: Tire radius = 500 mm, suspension travel clearance = 70 mm, chassis frame height = 150 mm, propeller shaft tunnel height = 200 mm, floor pan thickness = 80 mm, and floor covering = 20 mm. **Calculate**:
  - (i) The total floor height. **(3 marks)**
  - (ii) The number of entrance steps required if the first step is at 350 mm from ground and the maximum riser height is 250 mm. **(2 marks)**
  - (iii) The actual height of the last riser. **(2 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Factors Determining Floor Height (7 marks):**

1. **Tire size and radius (1 mark):** Larger tires (used for heavy vehicles) raise the floor; smaller low-profile tires (used in low-floor buses) reduce it.

2. **Suspension type and travel (1 mark):** Air suspension allows kneeling; conventional leaf springs require more clearance. Bump clearance must be added.

3. **Mechanical packaging underneath (2 marks):** Propeller shaft, differential, fuel tank, exhaust pipe, battery all need vertical space. Rear-engine or electric layouts eliminate the prop shaft and reduce floor height.

4. **Ergonomic / step-in requirements (1 mark):** Anthropometric data dictates first-step height (5th percentile female = 380 mm). Affects dwell time and accessibility.

5. **Regulatory standards (1 mark):** AIS-052 mandates max 400 mm first step, max 250 mm individual riser, and step tread depth.

6. **Vehicle type and use-case (1 mark):** Long-distance buses may tolerate high floor; city buses need low floor for frequent stops.

*Sketch — refer to SECTION 4.1 above for stack-up diagram (1 mark for neat sketch with all layers).*

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Total Floor Height (3 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
H_f &= R_t + \delta_s + H_{frame} + H_{tunnel} + t_{fp} + t_{cov} \\
    &= 500 + 70 + 150 + 200 + 80 + 20 \\
    &= 1020 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) Number of Entrance Steps (2 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
H_{remain} &= H_f - H_{step1} = 1020 - 350 = 670 \text{ mm} \\
n_{additional} &= \left\lceil \dfrac{670}{250} \right\rceil = \lceil 2.68 \rceil = 3 \text{ steps} \\
n_{total} &= 3 + 1 = 4 \text{ steps (including first)}
\end{aligned}$$

**(iii) Actual Last Riser Height (2 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
h_{last} &= H_f - H_{step1} - (n_{additional} - 1) \cdot h_{riser} \\
         &= 1020 - 350 - 2(250) \\
         &= 1020 - 350 - 500 \\
         &= 170 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Answers:** $H_f = 1020$ mm, $n_{total} = 4$ steps, $h_{last} = 170$ mm.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 | Model Paper 1]**

**(a)** Describe the **concept of low-floor buses**. With the help of a block diagram, explain how mechanical packaging is reorganized to achieve a low floor height of 340 mm. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A low-entry city bus is being converted from a high-floor design. The original floor height was 900 mm. The redesign adopts:
  - Smaller 17.5" tires (radius = 360 mm)
  - Portal axle (mechanical stack height = 50 mm, prop shaft removed)
  - Air suspension with kneeling (additional 80 mm reduction at entry)
  
  **Calculate**: (i) The new static floor height. (ii) The first-step height after kneeling. (iii) Comment on whether this design satisfies the **AIS-052 standard of ≤ 400 mm first step**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Concept of Low-Floor Buses (7 marks):**

A **low-floor bus** has a floor height in the range of **340 – 380 mm** from the ground, requiring only **one step** (or even no step with kneeling) for entry. This is essential for:
- Universal accessibility (wheelchair, pram users)
- Faster boarding and alighting
- Reduced dwell time at bus stops

**Block diagram of reorganization (3 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Conventional
        C1[Engine Front] --> C2[Prop Shaft Tunnel]
        C2 --> C3[Rear Axle]
        C3 --> C4[Frame Rails 250 mm high]
        C4 --> C5[Floor 900 mm]
    end
    subgraph Low Floor
        L1[Electric Motor Transverse Between Wheels] --> L2[No Prop Shaft]
        L2 --> L3[Portal Axle 50 mm]
        L3 --> L4[Drop Centre Frame]
        L4 --> L5[Floor 340 mm]
    end
```

Key reorganizations: (i) **Prop shaft eliminated** by transverse motor, (ii) **Portal axle** raises wheel center, (iii) **Smaller diameter wheels**, (iv) **Drop-centre frame** allows floor to pass through wheel arches, (v) **Air suspension with kneeling**.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) New Static Floor Height (3 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
H_{f,new} &= R_t + \delta_s + H_{mech} + t_{fp} + t_{cov} \\
          &= 360 + 50 + 50 + 80 + 20 \quad \text{(assuming standard pan + covering)} \\
          &= 560 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) First-Step Height After Kneeling (2 marks):**

$$H_{step1,\,kneel} = H_{f,new} - H_{kneel} = 560 - 80 = 480 \text{ mm}$$

**(iii) Compliance Check (2 marks):**

AIS-052 mandates **first step ≤ 400 mm**. 
$$H_{step1,\,kneel} = 480 \text{ mm} \;\;>\;\; 400 \text{ mm}$$

**Conclusion: NON-COMPLIANT.** The design does **not** satisfy AIS-052. The designer must further:
- Reduce suspension clearance to 30 mm, OR
- Use a thinner floor pan (60 mm), OR
- Adopt 16" tires (radius ≈ 320 mm), OR
- Use a **double-kneel** system (additional 60 mm drop).

Recompute with $R_t = 320$ mm and $\delta_s = 30$ mm:
$$H_{f,new} = 320 + 30 + 50 + 80 + 20 = 500 \text{ mm} \rightarrow H_{step1} = 420 \text{ mm (still over)}$$

With 16" tires + 30 mm clearance + double-kneel:
$$H_{step1} = 320 + 30 + 50 + 80 + 20 - 80 - 60 = 360 \text{ mm} \;\;<\;\; 400 \text{ mm} \;\;✓$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Confusing *ground clearance* with *floor height*. (Ground clearance is below the chassis/floor; floor height includes floor pan + covering.)
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Forgetting to **add the floor covering** (carpet/PVC) on top of the floor pan. Examiners deduct 1 mark for this.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** In step-count problems, using $\lfloor \cdot \rfloor$ instead of $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ (ceiling). Always round **UP** because the last riser must be *reduced*, not increased.
> - **Common Mistake #4:** Forgetting that the **first step height** includes the door sill and step board thickness.
> - **Common Mistake #5:** Not stating units (mm) — lose 0.5 mark per sub-question.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Floor Height (Module 2)**

- **Definition:** Vertical distance from **ground datum** to the **upper surface of the finished floor**.
- **Master equation:** $H_f = R_t + \delta_s + H_{mech} + t_{fp} + t_{cov}$
- **Typical values (must memorize):**
  - Car: 180 – 280 mm
  - Standard bus: 750 – 900 mm
  - Low-floor bus: 340 – 380 mm
  - Ultra-low-floor: 200 – 320 mm
  - Truck cab: 800 – 1500 mm
- **AIS-052 limits:** First step ≤ **400 mm**; individual riser ≤ **250 mm**; tread depth ≥ **300 mm**.
- **Number of steps formula:** $n = \lceil (H_f - H_{step1}) / h_{riser} \rceil + 1$
- **Last riser adjustment:** Always recompute the last riser separately; it will be *shorter* than the others.
- **Low-floor enablers:** Portal axles, transverse motors, smaller wheels, air suspension, kneeling, drop-centre frames, electric skateboard platforms.
- **Ergonomic 5th-percentile female popliteal height:** 380 mm (drives first-step design).
- **Never confuse:** Ground clearance ≠ Floor height. Ground clearance is below the chassis; floor height includes the entire stack.
- **Unit consistency:** Always use **mm** in KTU numerical answers; mark final answer with units.
- **Sketch must include:** Ground, tire, suspension, chassis, mechanical components, floor pan, and finished floor surface in a clean vertical stack.

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-minute mnemonic — "Tall Stacks Make Floors":**
> **T**ire → **S**uspension → **M**echanical (chassis, prop shaft) → **F**loor pan → **C**overing
> = **T-S-M-F-C** = **"Tall Stacks Make Floors"** (the more you stack, the higher the floor).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Engine Location
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT413 - VEHICLE BODY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Engine Location

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2: ENGINE LOCATION - CORE TECHNICAL OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ENGINE LOCATION (Definition)**
> In Vehicle Body Engineering, **Engine Location** refers to the specific spatial positioning and orientation of the powerplant (Internal Combustion Engine or Electric Motor) within the chassis architecture of an automobile. It is a primary design parameter that dictates the vehicle's **drivetrain configuration**, **weight distribution**, **packaging efficiency**, **traction characteristics**, and **crashworthiness** profile.

The engine location is conventionally classified using a **two-letter alphanumeric code**:
- **1st Letter** = Position of the engine (F = Front, M = Mid, R = Rear)
- **2nd Letter** = Position of the driven wheels (F = Front, R = Rear, 4 = All four)

For example, an **"FR Layout"** indicates a **Front engine, Rear-wheel drive** configuration.

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy & Intuitive Understanding

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy: "The Human Body as a Vehicle"**
> Imagine the human body as a car. The **heart (engine)** pumps energy (blood/fuel) to different parts. If the heart is in the **chest (front)**, blood flows easily to the head but has a longer journey to the legs. If the heart is in the **belly (mid)**, blood reaches all extremities equally. If the heart is in the **legs (rear)**, blood rushes to the head easily, but the legs may feel cold. Just like the heart's position affects blood circulation, the engine's position affects **power delivery, weight balance, and traction** in a car.

## 1.3 Standard Engine Layout Categories

| S.No. | Layout Code | Full Form | Common Application |
|-------|-------------|-----------|---------------------|
| 1 | **FF** | Front Engine, Front-Wheel Drive | Hatchbacks, Sedans (Maruti Swift, Honda City) |
| 2 | **FR** | Front Engine, Rear-Wheel Drive | Luxury sedans, Trucks (BMW 3 Series, Tata trucks) |
| 3 | **RR** | Rear Engine, Rear-Wheel Drive | Classic sports cars (Porsche 911, VW Beetle) |
| 4 | **MR** | Mid Engine, Rear-Wheel Drive | Supercars (Ferrari 488, Lamborghini Huracán) |
| 5 | **F4** | Front Engine, Four-Wheel Drive | SUVs, Off-roaders (Mahindra Thar, Jeep Wrangler) |
| 6 | **M4** | Mid Engine, Four-Wheel Drive | Hypercars (Bugatti Chiron, Koenigsegg) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Students must be able to **compare and contrast** the five primary engine layouts, identify their **applications in modern automobiles**, and analyze their **impact on vehicle dynamics and packaging**.

---

## 1.4 Key Design Considerations (KTU Board Examination Focus)

The following **five critical factors** determine the choice of engine location:

1. **Weight Distribution (% - Front:Rear):** Typically an ideal sedan aims for **50:50** or **60:40** for stability.
2. **Traction Efficiency:** Driven wheels must carry sufficient weight for grip (adhesion).
3. **Packaging & Space Optimization:** Maximizing cabin and cargo space.
4. **Crash Safety (Crumple Zones):** Front and rear energy-absorbing zones.
5. **Manufacturing & Service Cost:** Driveshaft, transmission tunnel, and complexity.

---

## 1.5 Visual Schematic Representation

> [!NOTE]
> The diagrams in SECTION_4 will provide detailed Mermaid-based schematic representations of all five engine layouts, illustrating the drivetrain architecture, weight transfer paths, and crash zone geometry.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Detailed Analysis of Each Engine Layout

### 2.1.1 FRONT ENGINE, FRONT-WHEEL DRIVE (FF Layout)

> [!NOTE]
> **Architectural Logic:** The engine and transmission are mounted transversely (or longitudinally) at the front, driving the front wheels directly.

**Advantages:**
- **Packaging Efficiency:** No propeller shaft or rear differential required → flat floor → larger cabin.
- **Traction Benefit:** Weight of the engine presses down on the driven wheels → excellent grip on slippery surfaces.
- **Manufacturing Cost:** Simpler drivetrain reduces cost by approximately **20–30%** compared to FR.
- **Fuel Economy:** Lower overall mass → better mileage (typically **15–25 kmpl** in compact cars).

**Disadvantages:**
- **Torque Steer:** Uneven traction causes the car to pull to one side under hard acceleration.
- **Weight Imbalance:** Front-heavy → understeer tendency → reduced steering feel at the limit.
- **Handling Limit:** Less suitable for high-performance driving due to nose-heavy dynamics.

**KTU Board Example:** Maruti Suzuki Swift, Hyundai i20, Honda City.

---

### 2.1.2 FRONT ENGINE, REAR-WHEEL DRIVE (FR Layout)

> [!NOTE]
> **Architectural Logic:** A longitudinal front engine sends power rearward via a propeller shaft to a rear differential.

**Advantages:**
- **Balanced Weight Distribution:** Close to **50:50** achievable → neutral handling.
- **Steering Purity:** Front wheels are free for steering only → superior feedback.
- **Performance Capability:** Ideal for high-power applications (BMW M-series, Mercedes AMG).
- **Load Transfer Dynamics:** Weight shifts rearward during acceleration → enhanced traction.

**Disadvantages:**
- **Propeller Shaft Tunnel:** Intrudes into cabin floor → reduces middle-rear passenger comfort.
- **Increased Mass:** Additional drivetrain components add **~50–80 kg**.
- **Traction in Slippery Conditions:** Lighter rear axle may cause oversteer.

**KTU Board Example:** BMW 3 Series, Toyota Fortuner (ladder-frame SUVs), Tata trucks.

---

### 2.1.3 REAR ENGINE, REAR-WHEEL DRIVE (RR Layout)

> [!NOTE]
> **Architectural Logic:** The engine is mounted at the rear axle, directly driving the rear wheels.

**Advantages:**
- **Rear Traction Maximized:** All engine mass is on the driven wheels.
- **Front Space Liberation:** Large crumple zone for crash safety.
- **Compact Footprint:** Short overhangs for sporty proportions.

**Disadvantages:**
- **Severe Oversteer Tendency:** Swing-axle geometry can cause lift-off oversteer (notable in early Porsche 911).
- **Cooling Challenges:** Engine at the rear requires complex air management.
- **Steering Feel:** Reduced feedback at the front axle.

**KTU Board Example:** Porsche 911 (modern versions use sophisticated suspensions to mitigate oversteer).

---

### 2.1.4 MID ENGINE, REAR-WHEEL DRIVE (MR Layout)

> [!NOTE]
> **Architectural Logic:** The engine is positioned between the axles (typically just ahead of the rear axle), driving the rear wheels.

**Advantages:**
- **Optimal Polar Moment of Inertia:** Mass concentrated near the center → extremely agile handling.
- **Near-Perfect Weight Distribution:** **40:60 (Front:Rear)** is achievable.
- **Aerodynamic Advantage:** Allows low, swept body design.
- **Track Performance:** Used in Formula 1 and Le Mans prototypes.

**Disadvantages:**
- **Zero Rear Seat Space:** Strict two-seater configuration.
- **Complex Cooling:** Mid-mounted engine requires dedicated heat exchangers.
- **Service Difficulty:** Engine access is severely limited.

**KTU Board Example:** Ferrari 488 GTB, Lamborghini Huracán, McLaren 720S.

---

### 2.1.5 FRONT ENGINE, FOUR-WHEEL DRIVE (F4 / AWD Layout)

> [!NOTE]
> **Architectural Logic:** A central transfer case distributes torque to all four wheels.

**Advantages:**
- **Maximum Traction:** All wheels driven → ideal for off-road and adverse weather.
- **Safety:** Reduces wheelspin during acceleration.
- **Versatility:** Used in both utility (Jeep Wrangler) and performance (Audi Quattro) applications.

**Disadvantages:**
- **Weight Penalty:** Adds **~100–150 kg** over a 2WD equivalent.
- **Mechanical Complexity:** Transfer case, additional driveshafts, and differentials.
- **Fuel Economy Penalty:** Typically **10–15% lower** than 2WD counterparts.

**KTU Board Example:** Mahindra Thar, Land Rover Defender, Audi A4 Quattro, Subaru Outback.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Engine Location & Weight Distribution)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Memorize the formulas below — they are tested in both **Part A (3 marks)** and **Part B (14 marks)** calculations.

| S.No. | Parameter | Formula | Units | Application |
|-------|-----------|---------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | **Weight on Front Axle (Static)** | $W_f = \dfrac{W \cdot b}{L}$ | Newtons (N) | Static load analysis |
| 2 | **Weight on Rear Axle (Static)** | $W_r = \dfrac{W \cdot a}{L}$ | Newtons (N) | Static load analysis |
| 3 | **Longitudinal Weight Distribution** | $WD = \dfrac{W_f}{W_r} \times 100$ | Percentage (%) | Front: Rear ratio |
| 4 | **Longitudinal CG Position (from Front Axle)** | $a = \dfrac{W_r \cdot L}{W}$ | metres (m) | Center of Gravity (x-axis) |
| 5 | **Height of CG (Approximate, for given layout)** | $h_{cg} \approx 0.55 \cdot H_{vehicle}$ | metres (m) | Approximation for sedans |
| 6 | **Static Stability Factor (SSF)** | $SSF = \dfrac{T}{2h_{cg}}$ | Dimensionless | Rollover resistance |
| 7 | **Weight Transfer during Braking** | $\Delta W_f = \dfrac{m \cdot a_x \cdot h_{cg}}{L}$ | Newtons (N) | Brake bias calculation |
| 8 | **Polar Moment of Inertia (qualitative)** | $I_{zz} \propto m \cdot k^2$ | kg·m² | Handling agility indicator |

**Where:**
- $W$ = Total vehicle weight (N)
- $a$ = Distance from front axle to CG (m)
- $b$ = Distance from rear axle to CG (m)
- $L$ = Wheelbase = $a + b$ (m)
- $T$ = Track width (m)
- $m$ = Vehicle mass (kg)
- $a_x$ = Longitudinal deceleration (m/s²)
- $h_{cg}$ = Height of Center of Gravity (m)
- $k$ = Radius of gyration (m)

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** The pipe character `$\vert$` is intentionally avoided in this table — we use standard LaTeX notation without vertical bars to maintain markdown table integrity.

---

## 2.3 Comparative Decision Matrix (KTU Board Favorite)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This matrix is a **guaranteed 7-mark question** in KTU ESE. Understand the trade-offs, do not just memorize.

| Criterion | FF | FR | RR | MR | F4 |
|-----------|----|----|----|----|----|
| **Weight Distribution** | 65:35 | 50:50 | 35:65 | 40:60 | 55:45 |
| **Cabin Space** | Excellent | Good | Fair | Poor | Good |
| **Manufacturing Cost** | Lowest | Medium | High | Highest | High |
| **Traction (Dry Road)** | Good | Good | Excellent | Excellent | Best |
| **Traction (Snow/Wet)** | Excellent | Fair | Excellent | Excellent | Best |
