$$F_K = \frac{W \cdot (L_{TR} - x_{CG})}{L_{TR}} = \frac{25{,}000 \times (8 - 5)}{8} = \frac{25{,}000 \times 3}{8} = 9{,}375 \text{ kg}$$

### Step 3: Calculate Rear Axle Load

$$F_R = W - F_K = 25{,}000 - 9{,}375 = 15{,}625 \text{ kg}$$

### Step 4: Calculate Kingpin Load Percentage

$$\text{Percentage} = \frac{F_K}{W} \times 100 = \frac{9{,}375}{25{,}000} \times 100 = 37.5\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **EXAMINER'S NOTE:** A kingpin load of 37.5% is **too high** — it will overload the tractor's front axle and cause poor steering. The trailer should be re-loaded to shift the CG rearward, OR the kingpin should be relocated forward.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]**
**Define a semi-trailer. How does it differ from a full trailer?**

**Model Answer:**

A **semi-trailer** is a trailer that has wheels (axles) only at the rear, with its front end resting on the towing tractor through a fifth wheel coupling and kingpin. The vertical load of the semi-trailer is shared between its own rear axles (~60–70%) and the tractor's rear axle through the kingpin (~30–40%).

A **full trailer** has axles at both front and rear, and is connected to the tractor through a rigid drawbar with a pintle hook. The full trailer's entire load is supported by its own axles; the tractor carries no vertical load from the trailer.

| Feature | Semi-Trailer | Full Trailer |
|---|---|---|
| Front axles | None (rests on tractor) | Yes (own front axle) |
| Coupling | Fifth wheel + kingpin | Pintle hook + lunette |
| Load on tractor | Yes (via kingpin) | Negligible |
| Maneuverability | Better (turns sharply) | Poorer (longer wheelbase) |

**(3 marks — full allocation: 1 mark definition + 1.5 marks comparison + 0.5 marks for key distinction)**

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Retest]**
**What is off-tracking? Why is it important in trailer design?**

**Model Answer:**

**Off-tracking** is the phenomenon in which the rear wheels of a vehicle (or trailer) follow a path of **smaller radius** than the front wheels during turning. The difference in radii is the off-tracking distance $\Delta$.

$$\Delta = R_f - \sqrt{R_f^2 - (L_t + L_f)^2}$$

**Importance in trailer design:**
1. Determines the **minimum turning corridor** required.
2. Excessive off-tracking causes the trailer to mount **curbs**, enter **adjacent lanes**, or strike **stationary objects**.
3. It limits the maximum **legal trailer length** for a given turn radius.
4. Critical for **intersection design** in road engineering.
5. Affects the **maneuverability** of the tractor-trailer combination in urban areas.

**(3 marks — 1 mark definition + 1 mark formula + 1 mark importance list)**

---

## PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, typically divided into two sub-parts: **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Module internal choice means you must answer EITHER "Question A" OR "Question B" fully (both sub-parts).

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Main]**

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches the different types of trailer coupling devices used in commercial vehicles. Compare their applications and load capacities. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A semi-trailer has a total loaded weight of 24,000 kg. The kingpin is located 1 m from the front of the trailer, and the center axle group is 8 m from the kingpin. The center of gravity of the loaded trailer is 5 m from the kingpin. Calculate:
   (i) The kingpin load on the tractor.
   (ii) The load on the trailer's rear axle group.
   (iii) The percentage of weight distribution. **(7 marks)**

#### MODEL SOLUTION (a):

The major trailer coupling devices are:

1. **Fifth Wheel Coupling (Semi-Trailers):**
   - U-shaped steel assembly mounted horizontally on the tractor chassis.
   - The trailer's kingpin drops into the slot and is locked by spring-loaded jaws.
   - Kingpin diameters: **50 mm** (medium duty) and **90 mm** (heavy duty, North American).
   - **Load capacity:** Up to **25–30 tonnes** kingpin load.
   - **Application:** Long-haul freight, container chassis, tankers.

2. **Pintle Hook and Lunette Ring (Full Trailers / Military):**
   - Pintle hook is a vertical hook on the tractor; lunette is a forged ring on the trailer's drawbar.
   - Allows significant **vertical articulation** (pitching) — useful on uneven terrain.
   - **Load capacity:** Up to **20–40 tonnes** GCW.
   - **Application:** Military, off-road, heavy construction.

3. **Tow Ball Coupling (Light Trailers):**
   - Spherical ball (typically **50 mm** diameter) on the towing vehicle.
   - Trailer has a socket coupling head that locks over the ball.
   - **Load capacity:** Up to **3,500 kg GVW** (India/Europe).
   - **Application:** Caravans, boat trailers, small utility trailers.

4. **Automatic Coupling:**
   - Mechanized hands-free engagement; common in buses and city distribution.
   - Uses underrun protection beam with integrated coupling.

> *(7 marks: 4 marks for descriptions + 2 marks for neat sketches (use schematic blocks) + 1 mark for comparison table)*

#### MODEL SOLUTION (b):

**Given:**
- $W = 24{,}000$ kg
- $L_{TR} = 8$ m (kingpin to rear axle group)
- $x_{CG} = 5$ m (kingpin to CG)

**(i) Kingpin Load:**

$$F_K = \frac{W \cdot (L_{TR} - x_{CG})}{L_{TR}} = \frac{24{,}000 \times (8 - 5)}{8} = \frac{24{,}000 \times 3}{8}$$

$$F_K = 9{,}000 \text{ kg}$$

**(ii) Rear Axle Load:**

$$F_R = W - F_K = 24{,}000 - 9{,}000 = 15{,}000 \text{ kg}$$

**(iii) Percentage Distribution:**

$$\text{Kingpin \%} = \frac{9{,}000}{24{,}000} \times 100 = 37.5\%$$

$$\text{Rear Axle \%} = \frac{15{,}000}{24{,}000} \times 100 = 62.5\%$$

> *(7 marks: 2 marks formula + 2 marks for (i) + 1.5 marks for (ii) + 1.5 marks for (iii))*

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Supplementary]**

**(a)** Define the following terms with suitable sketches:
   (i) Kingpin
   (ii) Fifth wheel
   (iii) Pintle hook
   (iv) Tow bar
   (v) Lunette ring
   (vi) Landing gear
   (vii) Converter dolly **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A tractor-semitrailer has a wheelbase of 4.5 m. The distance from the kingpin to the trailer's rear axle is 9 m. The tractor's front wheels follow a turning radius of 14 m. Calculate:
   (i) The radius of the trailer's rear axle path.
   (ii) The off-tracking distance.
   (iii) Is the configuration safe from jackknifing? Justify. **(7 marks)**

#### MODEL SOLUTION (a):

1. **Kingpin (1 mark):** A vertical steel locating pin (50 mm or 90 mm diameter) mounted on the underside of a semi-trailer at its front. It fits into the fifth wheel coupling of the tractor, providing the pivot for articulation and transferring vertical and horizontal forces.

2. **Fifth Wheel (1 mark):** A U-shaped coupling device mounted horizontally on the tractor chassis, with locking jaws that grip the trailer's kingpin. It allows the trailer to rotate (articulate) about the kingpin axis.

3. **Pintle Hook (1 mark):** A vertically-oriented hook on the rear of a tractor (or military vehicle) used to couple with a lunette ring on a full trailer. Allows vertical and rotational movement.

4. **Tow Bar / Drawbar (1 mark):** A rigid or semi-rigid bar extending from the front of a full trailer, ending in a lunette ring. Transfers towing force to the trailer.

5. **Lunette Ring (1 mark):** A circular forged eye (typically 76 mm internal diameter) at the end of a drawbar, which engages with a pintle hook.

6. **Landing Gear (1 mark):** Retractable support legs at the front of a semi-trailer, used to support the trailer's front end when it is detached from the tractor.

7. **Converter Dolly (1 mark):** A small auxiliary axle assembly with a fifth wheel, used to convert a semi-trailer into a full trailer for multi-trailer (road train) combinations.

> *(7 marks: 1 mark each, with 0.5 marks for definition and 0.5 marks for function/sketch indication)*

#### MODEL SOLUTION (b):

**Given:**
- $L_t = 4.5$ m (tractor wheelbase)
- $L_f = 9$ m (kingpin to trailer rear axle)
- $R_f = 14$ m (tractor front axle turning radius)

**(i) Radius of Trailer's Rear Axle Path:**

$$R_{tr} = \sqrt{R_f^2 - (L_t + L_f)^2} = \sqrt{14^2 - (4.5 + 9)^2}$$

$$R_{tr} = \sqrt{196 - 13.5^2} = \sqrt{196 - 182.25} = \sqrt{13.75}$$

$$R_{tr} = 3.71 \text{ m}$$

**(ii) Off-Tracking Distance:**

$$\Delta = R_f - R_{tr} = 14 - 3.71 = 10.29 \text{ m}$$

**(iii) Jackknifing Check:**

For safe turning (no jackknifing): $R_f > (L_t + L_f)$

$$R_f = 14 \text{ m}, \quad (L_t + L_f) = 13.5 \text{ m}$$

Since $14 > 13.5$, the configuration is **safe from jackknifing**, but only marginally. The safety margin is $14 - 13.5 = 0.5$ m, which is too small for practical operation.

> [!WARNING]
> **RECOMMENDATION:** The safety margin should be **at least 1.5–2.0 m** for practical turning maneuvers. A 0.5 m margin would cause the trailer to scrape the inside of the curve and risk jackknifing on uneven roads.

> *(7 marks: 2 marks for (i) + 1.5 marks for (ii) + 2 marks for (iii) + 1.5 marks for the safety margin justification)*

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **COMMON MISTAKES THAT COST MARKS IN KTU TRAILER QUESTIONS:**
>
> 1. **Mixing up the kingpin load direction:** The kingpin load is the force the **trailer exerts ON the tractor's fifth wheel**, not the other way. Students often invert this and lose 1 mark.
> 2. **Forgetting to subtract $x_{CG}$ from $L_{TR}$:** Many students write $F_K = \frac{W \cdot x_{CG}}{L_{TR}}$ instead of $\frac{W \cdot (L_{TR} - x_{CG})}{L_{TR}}$. This is a **fatal error** worth 2 marks.
> 3. **Confusing semi-trailer with full trailer formulas:** Semi-trailers have **kingpin load**; full trailers have **drawbar pull** (horizontal force only, no vertical load on tractor).
> 4. **Not drawing sketches for "Explain" questions:** KTU examiners expect a neat labelled diagram for full marks on 7-mark questions. Without it, expect a **2-mark deduction**.
> 5. **Off-tracking formula errors:** Students often use $L_t$ alone (tractor wheelbase) instead of $(L_t + L_f)$ (total effective length). This gives an answer off by a factor of 2–3.
> 6. **Not stating units:** Always write **"kg" or "kN" or "m"** explicitly. KTU evaluators deduct 0.5 marks for missing units.
> 7. **Ignoring the safety margin comment:** When asked "is it safe?", students just say yes/no without quantifying. Always provide the **numerical margin** and a **practical recommendation**.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST (Save this for the night before the exam):**

**Core Definitions:**
- [ ] **Trailer:** Non-powered towed vehicle for load carriage.
- [ ] **Semi-Trailer:** Front rests on tractor (fifth wheel + kingpin).
- [ ] **Full Trailer:** Both front and rear axles, connected by drawbar.
- [ ] **Off-Tracking:** Difference between front and rear wheel turning radii.
- [ ] **Jackknifing:** Trailer folding onto the tractor (instability).
- [ ] **Kingpin:** Vertical pin on trailer's underside.
- [ ] **Fifth Wheel:** U-shaped coupling on tractor.
- [ ] **Pintle Hook & Lunette:** Hook-and-ring coupling for full trailers.
- [ ] **Landing Gear:** Retractable support legs for parked semi-trailers.
- [ ] **Converter Dolly:** Axle assembly converting semi to full trailer.

**Critical Formulas:**
- [ ] Kingpin Load: $F_K = \dfrac{W \cdot (L_{TR} - x_{CG})}{L_{TR}}$
- [ ] Off-Tracking: $\Delta = R_f - \sqrt{R_f^2 - (L_t + L_f)^2}$
- [ ] Stability Index: $SSI = \dfrac{t}{2 h_{CG}}$
- [ ] Rear Axle Load: $F_R = W - F_K$

**Design Rules of Thumb:**
- [ ] Kingpin load should be **15–25%** of total trailer weight.
- [ ] Maximum legal trailer length in India: **12 m** (standard).
- [ ] Maximum GCW: **49 tonnes** (multi-axle road use).
- [ ] Safety margin for non-jackknifing: $R_f > L_t + L_f + 1.5$ m (minimum).

**KTU Board Exam Hot Topics (Most Repeated):**
- [ ] Kingpin load numerical (guaranteed every year).
- [ ] Off-tracking derivation and numerical.
- [ ] Comparison of coupling devices.
- [ ] Differences between semi-trailer and full trailer.
- [ ] Sketch and label of fifth wheel + kingpin assembly.

**One-Line Revision Phrases:**
- "Semi-trailer = front on tractor; Full trailer = front on its own axle."
- "Off-tracking = front wheel radius MINUS rear wheel radius."
- "Kingpin = trailer's pivot; Fifth wheel = tractor's lock."
- "Pintle hook = military + off-road; Tow ball = caravans + light trailers."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tipper Body and Tanker Body
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT413 - VEHICLE BODY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Tipper Body and Tanker Body

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Tipper Body (Tipping / Dump Body)

**Formal Academic Definition:**
A *Tipper Body* (also called a *tipping body* or *dump body*) is an open-top, load-carrying superstructure mounted on a rigid truck chassis, engineered to be hydraulically tilted about a rear hinge axis to discharge bulk granular or lumpy payload by gravity. The tilt is achieved by a front-mounted single-acting telescopic hydraulic hoist cylinder pivoted between the chassis sub-frame and the underside of the body floor.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEMUT413 – Module 2):** Constructional features, types, and tipping mechanisms of tipper bodies used for bulk transport in mining, quarrying, and civil-engineering applications.

**Conceptual Analogy (Plain English):**
Imagine a small wheelbarrow in your backyard. When you want to unload the sand, you simply lift the front handles and the tray tilts backwards, letting the sand slide out from the rear. A **tipper truck** is just a giant, motorized, hydraulically-powered version of that wheelbarrow — except the lifting is done by an oil pump and a piston instead of your arms, and the rear is fitted with a swinging door (tailgate) that opens to let the load pour out.

**Key Engineering Metrics:**

| Parameter | Typical Value |
|---|---|
| Tipping angle (full discharge) | **45° – 60°** |
| Hoist cylinder working pressure | **15 – 20 MPa** (≈ 150 – 200 bar) |
| Body material yield strength | **250 – 450 MPa** (HSLA steel) |
| Payload range | **5 – 40 tonnes** |
| Hoist cycle time | **15 – 30 seconds** |

---

### 1.2 Tanker Body

**Formal Academic Definition:**
A *Tanker Body* is a fully enclosed, pressure-or-atmospheric, fluid-tight vessel mounted on a truck or trailer chassis, designed to transport liquids (water, fuel, milk, chemicals), liquefied gases (LPG), or dry bulk powders in pressurized form. Construction follows codes such as **IS 4177**, **ASME Section VIII**, or **ADR** (for hazardous goods), and uses cylindrical or elliptical cross-section shells reinforced with anti-surge baffles.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Distinction from Tipper Body:** A tipper body is **open-top** and **gravity-discharged** by tilting, whereas a tanker body is **fully sealed** and **pressure-or-pump discharged** through bottom valves — its contents never leave the vessel during transport.

**Conceptual Analogy (Plain English):**
Think of a large steel water-bottle lying on its side and strapped to a truck. The "bottle" is welded from thick steel plates, has a sealed cap on top (manhole) for filling, and a tap (discharge valve) at the bottom for emptying. Inside, you can see wavy metal discs (baffles) — these stop the water from sloshing violently when the truck brakes or turns, the way a baffle inside a washing machine stops clothes from thrashing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference:** Tanker shells for road transport are normally designed to a test pressure of **0.3 MPa (≈ 3 bar)** for non-hazardous liquids, and up to **1.7 MPa (≈ 17 bar)** for LPG service.

---

### 1.3 Functional Comparison Table

| Feature | Tipper Body | Tanker Body |
|---|---|---|
| Discharge method | Gravity tilt (hydraulic hoist) | Pump / pressurised line |
| Containment | Open-top, no sealing | Fully sealed, leak-proof |
| Payload form | Granular, lumpy, aggregate | Liquid, slurry, gas |
| Critical component | Hoist cylinder + pivot hinge | Shell, baffles, manhole, valves |
| Standard design code | IS 9433 / OEM proprietary | IS 4177 / ASME Sec. VIII / ADR |
| Discharge angle | **50° – 60°** (geometric) | N/A (horizontal flow) |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Tipper Body — Operational Logic (Step-by-Step)

**Step 1 — Load Reception**
The loaded body sits on the chassis sub-frame. The hoist cylinder is fully retracted. The centre of gravity (C.G.) of the loaded body lies *behind* the rear pivot hinge, ensuring stability.

**Step 2 — Hoist Activation**
The driver engages the PTO (Power Take-Off) which drives a hydraulic gear pump. Pressurised oil (≈ **15 – 20 MPa**) is admitted into the base of the single-acting telescopic hoist cylinder.

**Step 3 — Body Lift**
The cylinder extends, pushing upward on a sub-frame-mounted trunnion. The body rotates about the rear hinge axis (typically located 200–400 mm behind the rear axle centre).

**Step 4 — Discharge**
At a tipping angle of **≈ 50°**, the material's angle of repose is exceeded (e.g., wet sand ≈ 45°, gravel ≈ 35°), and the payload flows out of the open tailgate.

**Step 5 — Body Return**
The hoist cylinder is allowed to retract (gravity + oil bled back to reservoir), and the body is locked back on the safety catch.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a rear hinge?** A rear-mounted hinge places the load's C.G. forward of the pivot during lifting, which keeps the body self-righting (it naturally wants to fall back down). It also makes the loaded vehicle stable during transport because the load sits between the axles.

---

### 2.2 Types of Tipper Bodies (Classified by Tipping Geometry)

| Type | Pivot Location | Cylinder Position | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Front-end tipper (single cyl.)** | Rear of body | Front centre, under floor | Light/medium trucks |
| **Front-end tipper (twin cyl.)** | Rear of body | Two cylinders, front-left & front-right | Heavy mining trucks |
| **Rear-end tipper** | Front of body | Rear, over tailgate | Trailer tippers |
| **Three-way tipper** | Side hinges | Side-mounted cylinder | Agri / forestry |
| **Bottom-dump (belly tipper)** | Centre bottom | Lateral swing gate | Aggregate spreaders |

---

### 2.3 Tanker Body — Operational Logic

**Step 1 — Shell Fabrication**
Rolled steel plates (typically **6–10 mm thick**) are rolled into cylindrical sections, longitudinally seam-welded, then circumferentially welded to form the shell.

**Step 2 — End-Cap Welding**
Ellipsoidal (2:1) or torispherical dished ends are welded to the shell, providing pressure-vessel-grade closure.

**Step 3 — Internal Baffle Installation**
Anti-surge baffles (typically **3 mm thick**, spaced at every 1.5 × tank diameter) are welded inside, with a cut-out at the bottom for full drainage.

**Step 4 — Externals Mounted**
Manhole (typically **500 mm Ø**), walkway, ladder, pressure-vacuum relief valve, bottom discharge valve, and emergency shut-off.

**Step 5 — Chassis Integration**
The shell is cradled in rolled-steel saddle supports, which allow thermal expansion.

---

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All equations below are *exam-portable* — memorise the structure, not the exact algebra. Use `\vert` style absolute values where required.

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\theta_{\text{tip}} = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{h_{\text{cyl}}}{L_{\text{eff}}}\right)$ | Geometric tipping angle from cylinder geometry | degrees |
| 2 | $F_{\text{cyl}} = \dfrac{W_{\text{load}} \cdot r_{\text{cg}}}{r_{\text{cyl}} \cdot \eta_{\text{mech}}}$ | Hoist cylinder force required | kN |
| 3 | $V_{\text{tank}} = \pi r^{2} L + \dfrac{4}{3}\pi a b^{2}$ | Cylindrical shell + ellipsoidal head volume (2:1) | m³ |
| 4 | $\tau_{\text{shell}} = \dfrac{p \cdot D}{2 \cdot S \cdot E - 0.6 p}$ | Hoop stress in cylindrical shell (ASME) | MPa |
| 5 | $\sigma_{\text{long}} = \dfrac{p \cdot D}{4 \cdot S \cdot E - 0.4 p}$ | Longitudinal stress in shell | MPa |
| 6 | $t_{\text{min}} = \dfrac{p \cdot R}{S \cdot E - 0.6 p}$ | Minimum shell thickness (pressure vessel code) | mm |
| 7 | $F_{\text{surge}} = \rho \cdot a \cdot V_{\text{partial}}$ | Surge force on baffle during braking | N |
| 8 | $I_{\text{baffle}} = \dfrac{b \cdot t^{3}}{12}$ | Moment of inertia of baffle plate | mm⁴ |
| 9 | $\eta_{\text{vol,slosh}} = 1 - \dfrac{\tanh(kh)}{kh}$ | Effective fill ratio under sloshing | dimensionless |
| 10 | $P_{\text{hoist}} = \dfrac{F_{\text{cyl}}}{A_{\text{piston}}}$ | Hydraulic pressure required in hoist | MPa |

**Notation Glossary:**
- $W_{\text{load}}$ — total payload weight (kN)
- $r_{\text{cg}}$ — horizontal distance of load C.G. from rear hinge (m)
- $r_{\text{cyl}}$ — perpendicular distance from hinge to cylinder trunnion (m)
- $\eta_{\text{mech}}$ — mechanical efficiency (≈ **0.85 – 0.90**)
- $p$ — internal design pressure (MPa)
- $D$ — internal shell diameter (mm)
- $S$ — allowable stress (MPa)
- $E$ — joint efficiency (≈ **0.85 – 1.00**)
- $a, b$ — semi-axes of ellipsoidal head (m)

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Tipper bodies** are the workhorses of *mining logistics* — a 40-tonne mining tipper at an iron-ore mine in Odisha completes ≈ 25 load-dump cycles per shift, with hoist system MTBF > 8,000 hours.
- **Tanker bodies** are governed by safety because they carry flammable liquids. A failure in the shell at high speed can cause a *BLEVE* (Boiling Liquid Expanding Vapour Explosion). Hence the rigorous pressure-vessel code compliance.

> [!NOTE]
> The design of a road tanker shell is essentially a *Class III pressure vessel* in disguise — the same thin-shell membrane equations govern it, but with dynamic road-load factors of **2.5 – 4.0×** instead of the static 1.0 used in fixed plant vessels.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Tipping Angle from Cylinder Geometry

Consider a tipper body pivoted at the rear hinge H. The hoist cylinder is anchored at the chassis sub-frame at point C, and pushes up on the body underside at point B. When the body has rotated by angle $\theta$, the effective length of the cylinder, $L_{\text{cyl}}$, is:

$$
L_{\text{cyl}}(\theta) = \sqrt{L_0^{2} + r^{2} - 2 L_0 r \cos(\alpha - \theta)}
$$

where $L_0$ is the initial (retracted) cylinder length, $r$ is the moment-arm of the cylinder from hinge H, and $\alpha$ is the initial angle of the cylinder with the horizontal.

**Step 1:** Differentiate $L_{\text{cyl}}$ with respect to $\theta$ to find cylinder extension rate:

$$
\frac{dL_{\text{cyl}}}{d\theta} = \frac{L_0 r \sin(\alpha - \theta)}{\sqrt{L_0^{2} + r^{2} - 2 L_0 r \cos(\alpha - \theta)}}
$$

**Step 2:** For a *single-stage telescopic cylinder*, the piston travels a maximum stroke $S_{\text{max}}$. The full tipping angle is reached when:

$$
L_{\text{cyl}}(\theta_{\text{tip}}) - L_0 = S_{\text{max}}
$$

**Step 3:** Solving this implicitly for $\theta_{\text{tip}}$ gives the *design tipping angle*. For a typical 10-tonne tipper with $L_0 = 0.5$ m, $r = 1.8$ m, $\alpha = 30°$, $S_{\text{max}} = 1.0$ m, the solution is:

$$
\theta_{\text{tip}} \approx 52^{\circ}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** A tipping angle of **45°** discharges free-flowing material; **60°** is required for sticky, cohesive loads like wet clay. Always over-design to **55°** for safety margin.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Hoist Cylinder Force (Tipper)

Free-body diagram: The loaded body is pivoted at H. Three forces act on the body:

1. **$W_{\text{load}}$** — total load weight (acting downward at C.G., horizontal distance $r_{\text{cg}}$ from H)
2. **$F_{\text{cyl}}$** — cylinder force (acting at angle $\beta$ to body floor, at distance $r_{\text{cyl}}$ from H)
3. **$R_H$** — hinge reaction at H (passes through H, hence zero moment)

Taking moments about the hinge H (positive = clockwise, lifting):

$$
F_{\text{cyl}} \cdot r_{\text{cyl}} \cdot \sin(\beta) = \dfrac{W_{\text{load}} \cdot r_{\text{cg}} \cdot \cos(\theta)}{1}
$$

Therefore the *instantaneous* cylinder force is:

$$
F_{\text{cyl}}(\theta) = \dfrac{W_{\text{load}} \cdot r_{\text{cg}} \cdot \cos(\theta)}{r_{\text{cyl}} \cdot \sin(\beta) \cdot \eta_{\text{mech}}}
$$

**Step-by-step evaluation for a 15-tonne tipper:**

- $W_{\text{load}} = 15 \times 9.81 = 147.15$ kN
- $r_{\text{cg}} = 2.0$ m (assumed constant, body rigid)
- $r_{\text{cyl}} = 1.6$ m
- $\beta = 65°$
- $\eta_{\text{mech}} = 0.88$

**At $\theta = 0°$ (lift-off):**

$$
F_{\text{cyl}}(0) = \dfrac{147.15 \times 2.0 \times \cos(0°)}{1.6 \times \sin(65°) \times 0.88}
$$

$$
F_{\text{cyl}}(0) = \dfrac{147.15 \times 2.0 \times 1.0}{1.6 \times 0.9063 \times 0.88}
$$

$$
F_{\text{cyl}}(0) = \dfrac{294.30}{1.2761} \approx 230.6 \text{ kN}
$$

**At $\theta = 30°$ (mid-lift):**

$$
F_{\text{cyl}}(30) = \dfrac{147.15 \times 2.0 \times \cos(30°)}{1.6 \times \sin(65°) \times 0.88}
$$

$$
F_{\text{cyl}}(30) = \dfrac{147.15 \times 2.0 \times 0.8660}{1.2761}
$$

$$
F_{\text{cyl}}(30) = \dfrac{254.83}{1.2761} \approx 199.7 \text{ kN}
$$

**Conclusion:** The maximum cylinder force occurs at lift-off ($\theta = 0°$), so the cylinder is *sized* for this worst case. For a working pressure of **18 MPa** (≈ 180 bar):

$$
A_{\text{piston}} = \dfrac{F_{\text{cyl,max}}}{P_{\text{hoist}}} = \dfrac{230.6 \times 10^{3}}{18 \times 10^{6}} \approx 0.01281 \text{ m}^{2} = 128.1 \text{ cm}^{2}
$$

Required piston bore ≈ **128 mm** → standard bore **140 mm** selected.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Hoop Stress in Tanker Shell

For a thin-walled cylindrical shell under internal pressure $p$, membrane theory gives:

$$
\sigma_{\text{hoop}} = \dfrac{p \cdot r}{t}
$$

where $r$ is the inner radius and $t$ is the wall thickness. With $D = 2r$:

$$
\sigma_{\text{hoop}} = \dfrac{p \cdot D}{2t}
$$

**Including weld joint efficiency $E$ and allowable stress $S$** (per ASME Sec. VIII Div. 1):

$$
\sigma_{\text{hoop,allow}} = S \cdot E
$$

For a *design safety factor* of **4.0** (typical for road tankers carrying fuel):

$$
t_{\text{min}} = \dfrac{p \cdot D}{2 \cdot S \cdot E - 0.6 p}
$$

> **Note:** The denominator term $0.6p$ is the ASME correction for thick-wall theory; for road tankers with $t/D < 0.1$, this is often negligible but kept for compliance.

**Worked Example: 20 kL Diesel Tanker**

- $V = 20$ m³ → for a *2:1 ellipsoidal* tanker, take $L = 4.0$ m
- Cylindrical volume $V_{\text{cyl}} = V - 2 V_{\text{head}}$
- With 2:1 ellipsoidal head, $V_{\text{head}} = \dfrac{\pi D^{3}}{24}$ (with $L = D$)
- Solving iteratively: $D \approx 2.1$ m, $L \approx 5.5$ m
- Design pressure $p = 0.4$ MPa (test pressure)
- Allowable stress $S = 137$ MPa (IS 2062 grade at design temp 65 °C)
- Joint efficiency $E = 0.85$ (double-welded, spot radiographed)

$$
t_{\text{min}} = \dfrac{0.4 \times 2100}{2 \times 137 \times 0.85 - 0.6 \times 0.4}
$$

$$
t_{\text{min}} = \dfrac{840}{232.9 - 0.24} = \dfrac{840}{232.66} \approx 3.61 \text{ mm}
$$

Adding **2 mm corrosion allowance** and rounding up to next standard plate:

$$
t_{\text{design}} = 3.61 + 2.0 = 5.61 \text{ mm} \rightarrow 6 \text{ mm}
$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Sloshing Force on Baffle Plates

For a partially-filled tanker ($\text{fill ratio} < 0.95$), the fluid sloshes during braking. The longitudinal force on a baffle is:

$$
F_{\text{slosh}} = m_{\text{eff}} \cdot a_{\text{decel}}
$$

where $m_{\text{eff}}$ is the effective sloshing mass and $a_{\text{decel}}$ is the vehicle deceleration (typical **0.4 g** for emergency braking on a loaded truck).

For a *baffled* tanker, the sloshing mass is reduced by an empirical factor $k_s \approx 0.3$:

$$
m_{\text{eff}} = k_s \cdot \rho \cdot V_{\text{partial}}
$$

For a 20 kL diesel tanker at 90 % fill ($\rho = 850$ kg/m³):

$$
m_{\text{eff}} = 0.3 \times 850 \times 18 = 4590 \text{ kg}
$$

$$
F_{\text{slosh}} = 4590 \times 0.4 \times 9.81 = 18{,}011 \text{ N} \approx 18.0 \text{ kN}
$$

This force is transferred through the baffle welds to the shell, so baffle-to-shell weld sizing must be checked for *shear + tension* using this load.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Design Decision Flow

| Design Aspect | Tipper Body | Tanker Body |
|---|---|---|
| Governing equation | Moment-balance at hinge | Membrane stress in shell |
| Worst-case load | Lift-off instant ($\theta = 0$) | Static pressure + dynamic surge |
| Material selection driver | Wear (load slides) | Corrosion (fluid attacks) |
| Safety factor | **2.5 – 3.0** | **3.5 – 4.0** (hazardous fluids) |
| FEA check focus | Hinge boss stress | Baffle-shell weld fatigue |
| Test standard | Tip trial, 1.25× rated load | Hydro-test at 1.3× design pressure |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Tipper Body — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[Chassis Sub-frame] --> A2[Rear Hinge Pin Assembly]
    A2 --> A3[Body Floor Plate 6-8mm HSLA]
    A3 --> A4[Side Walls Ribbed]
    A3 --> A5[Front Bulkhead]
    A3 --> A6[Tailgate with Side Hinges]
    A1 --> A7[Hoist Cylinder Trunnion]
    A8[Hydraulic Gear Pump] --> A9[Pressure Relief Valve 20 MPa]
    A9 --> A10[Directional Control Valve]
    A10 --> A11[Telescopic Hoist Cylinder 140mm Bore]
    A11 --> A7
    A8 --> A12[Reservoir Tank 80L]
    A12 --> A8
    A11 --> A12
    A6 --> A13[Tailgate Latch Automatic]
    A13 --> A6
```

**Reading the diagram:** Oil flows from the reservoir → pump → relief valve (limits to **20 MPa**) → DCV (driver-controlled) → hoist cylinder → cylinder extends → pushes body at trunnion → body rotates about rear hinge → tailgate auto-opens → load discharges → cylinder retracts, oil returns to tank.

---

### 4.2 Tanker Body — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    B1[Chassis Frame Rails] --> B2[Saddle Support Roll-welded]
    B2 --> B3[Tank Shell Cylindrical 6mm]
    B3 --> B4[Ellipsoidal Heads 2 is to 1]
    B3 --> B5[Anti-surge Baffles every 1.5D]
    B3 --> B6[Reinforcement Rings External]
    B3 --> B7[Manhole 500mm with Cover]
    B3 --> B8[Bottom Discharge Valve Pneumatic]
    B7 --> B9[Pressure-Vacuum Relief Valve]
    B3 --> B10[Internal Lining Epoxy optional]
    B2 --> B11[U-bolt Clamping to Chassis]
    B12[Walkway and Handrail] --> B3
    B13[Ladder Access] --> B3
    B14[Emergency Shut-off Cable] --> B8
```

**Reading the diagram:** The shell + heads form a pressure vessel. Baffles break up sloshing. Reinforcement rings (rolled channel sections) prevent shell buckling under external vacuum. The bottom discharge valve is pneumatically operated and can be *emergency-closed* by a cable running to the cab. The manhole is the only access for cleaning and inspection.

---

### 4.3 Tipper Tipping Sequence — Event Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Dr as Driver
    participant PTO as Power Take Off
    participant Pump as Hydraulic Pump
    participant DCV as Control Valve
    participant Cyl as Hoist Cylinder
    participant Body as Tipper Body
    participant TG as Tailgate

    Dr->>PTO: Engage PTO
    PTO->>Pump: Drive engaged
    Pump->>DCV: Pressurise oil
    Dr->>DCV: Open lift port
    DCV->>Cyl: Oil to base
    Cyl->>Body: Extend push up
    Body->>Body: Rotate about rear hinge
    Body->>TG: Automatic latch release at 10 deg
    TG->>TG: Swing open
    Body->>Body: Reach 50 deg full discharge
    Dr->>DCV: Centre hold position
    Cyl->>Pump: Hold pressure
    Dr->>DCV: Open retract port
    Cyl->>Body: Lower
    Body->>TG: Latch re-engages
    Dr->>PTO: Disengage
```

**Reading the diagram:** This sequence is the *operational DNA* of a tipper. Notice the **tailgate auto-latch** — most modern tippers release the tailgate latch at a small lift angle (≈ 10°) so the driver doesn't have to leave the cab. The latch re-engages when the body returns to the chassis, providing positive mechanical locking in the transport position.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*List any three constructional features of a tipper body and state the function of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per point):**

1. **Rear Hinge Pin Assembly (1 mark):** A forged-steel pin mounted on the chassis cross-member, around which the body rotates during tipping. It carries the entire body weight during transport and the tipping moment during lift.
2. **Hoist Cylinder Trunnion (1 mark):** A forged lug welded to the body underside, providing the pivot point for the hoist cylinder's rod end. Must be sized to handle the lift-off force.
3. **Tailgate with Top Hinge (1 mark):** Allows automatic opening as the body tips beyond ~10°, providing the discharge aperture. Bottom-hinged tailgates give controlled pour; top-hinged give full-spread discharge.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*State the function of anti-surge baffles in a road tanker. Why are they not provided in a tipper body?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Function of baffles (2 marks):** Anti-surge baffles break the longitudinal flow of liquid during vehicle acceleration, braking, and cornering, reducing the *sloshing mass*, lowering the C.G. shift, and preventing excessive dynamic loads on the tank shell and baffle welds.
- **Why not in tipper body (1 mark):** A tipper carries **solid, granular** material (sand, gravel, ore) which does not slosh or generate surge forces, hence baffles are unnecessary. The tipper only needs the open-top floor and tailgate for gravity discharge.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Module 2 Internal Choice Pattern:** *Answer any ONE full question from Module 2.* Each question is split into two 7-mark sub-parts.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **hydraulic tipping system** of a rear-pivot front-tipping tipper body. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A tipper has a body length of 4.2 m and is to be tipped to **55°** using a single telescopic hoist cylinder with a 1.0 m stroke. The body pivots at the rear. Determine (i) the maximum cylinder force at lift-off, and (ii) the required piston bore for a system working pressure of **18 MPa**. Take payload = 12 tonnes, C.G. of loaded body at 2.1 m from rear hinge, cylinder trunnion at 1.5 m from rear hinge, cylinder-to-floor angle at lift-off = **70°**, mechanical efficiency = **0.88**. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to A(a):**

1. **Component list (3 marks):**
   - PTO-driven hydraulic gear pump
   - Reservoir with suction strainer
   - Pressure relief valve set at 20 MPa
   - 4/3 manually-operated directional control valve (lift – hold – lower)
   - Single-acting telescopic hoist cylinder
   - Hose ruptures protection valve
   - Body safety catch (mechanical lock)

2. **Operation (2 marks):**
   - Driver engages PTO → pump draws oil from reservoir.
   - DCV shifted to *lift* position → oil flows to base of cylinder.
   - Cylinder extends, pushing the body up at the trunnion.
   - Body rotates about rear hinge; tailgate auto-opens at ≈ 10° lift.
   - At full tipping, DCV moved to *hold*; then *lower* position retracts cylinder.

3. **Safety features (2 marks):**
   - **Hose-burst valve** prevents uncontrolled body drop if a hose fails.
   - **Mechanical body catch** locks body to chassis during transport, removing reliance on hydraulics.
   - **Relief valve** caps maximum pressure, protecting cylinder seals.

---

**Model Solution to A(b):**

**Given:**
- $\theta_{\text{tip}} = 55°$ (geometric design)
- $S_{\text{max}} = 1.0$ m
- $W = 12 \times 9.81 = 117.72$ kN
- $r_{\text{cg}} = 2.1$ m
- $r_{\text{cyl}} = 1.5$ m
- $\beta = 70°$
- $\eta = 0.88$
- $P = 18$ MPa

**(i) Maximum cylinder force at lift-off ($\theta = 0°$):**

$$
F_{\text{cyl,max}} = \dfrac{W \cdot r_{\text{cg}} \cdot \cos(0°)}{r_{\text{cyl}} \cdot \sin(\beta) \cdot \eta}
$$

$$
F_{\text{cyl,max}} = \dfrac{117.72 \times 2.1 \times 1.0}{1.5 \times \sin(70°) \times 0.88}
$$

$$
F_{\text{cyl,max}} = \dfrac{247.21}{1.5 \times 0.9397 \times 0.88}
$$

$$
F_{\text{cyl,max}} = \dfrac{247.21}{1.2404} \approx 199.3 \text{ kN}
$$

**(ii) Required piston bore:**

$$
A_{\text{piston}} = \dfrac{F_{\text{cyl,max}}}{P} = \dfrac{199.3 \times 10^{3}}{18 \times 10^{6}} = 0.01107 \text{ m}^{2} = 110.7 \text{ cm}^{2}
$$

$$
D_{\text{piston}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 A_{\text{piston}}}{\pi}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \times 110.7}{\pi}} = \sqrt{140.97} \approx 11.87 \text{ cm} = 118.7 \text{ mm}
$$

**Result:**
- (i) $F_{\text{cyl,max}} \approx 199.3$ kN
- (ii) $D_{\text{piston}} \approx 118.7$ mm → select **standard 125 mm** bore.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**(a)** Describe with a sketch the **constructional features of a road tanker body** for transporting diesel fuel. List the safety fittings provided. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A road tanker is to carry **18 kL of diesel** ($\rho = 850$ kg/m³) at a design pressure of **0.4 MPa**. The shell is **IS 2062** steel with allowable stress **137 MPa** and joint efficiency **0.85**. Determine: (i) suitable shell diameter, assuming length = 2.5 × diameter, with 2:1 ellipsoidal heads, and (ii) minimum shell thickness including 2 mm corrosion allowance. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to B(a):**

**Constructional features (4 marks):**
1. **Cylindrical shell** of 6–8 mm rolled steel, longitudinally seam-welded and circumferentially butt-welded.
2. **Ellipsoidal (2:1) dished ends** welded to shell, providing smooth pressure transition.
3. **Anti-surge baffles** at every 1.5 D, with bottom cut-out for drainage.
4. **External reinforcement rings** (rolled channel sections) welded to shell to prevent external buckling under vacuum.
5. **Saddle supports** welded to underside, cradling the shell on the chassis.
6. **Manhole** (500 mm dia.) with bolted cover, vent, and dip-stick.
7. **Walkway and ladder** for top access.

**Safety fittings (3 marks):**
1. **Pressure-vacuum relief valve** on manhole — vents at +0.07 MPa, admits air at -0.02 MPa.
2. **Bottom discharge valve** with pneumatic actuator and *emergency shut-off cable* to cab.
3. **Flame arrestors** in vent lines.
4. **Static earthing** chain or reel.
5. **Rear bumper with side under-run protection** as per AIS-052.

---

**Model Solution to B(b):**

**Given:** $V = 18$ m³, $p = 0.4$ MPa, $S = 137$ MPa, $E = 0.85$, $L = 2.5 D$.

**(i) Suitable shell diameter:**

Volume equation (cylindrical part + 2 ellipsoidal heads):

$$
V = \dfrac{\pi D^{2}}{4} \cdot L + 2 \cdot \dfrac{\pi D^{3}}{24}
$$

Substituting $L = 2.5 D$:

$$
18 = \dfrac{\pi D^{2}}{4} \cdot 2.5 D + \dfrac{\pi D^{3}}{12}
$$

$$
18 = \dfrac{2.5 \pi D^{3}}{4} + \dfrac{\pi D^{3}}{12} = 0.625 \pi D^{3} + 0.0833 \pi D^{3} = 0.7083 \pi D^{3}
$$

$$
18 = 2.225 D^{3} \quad \Rightarrow \quad D^{3} = \dfrac{18}{2.225} = 8.089
$$

$$
D = \sqrt[3]{8.089} \approx 2.008 \text{ m} \approx 2.0 \text{ m}
$$

So $D = 2000$ mm, $L = 2.5 \times 2.0 = 5.0$ m.

**(ii) Minimum shell thickness:**

$$
t_{\text{min}} = \dfrac{p \cdot D}{2 S E - 0.6 p} = \dfrac{0.4 \times 2000}{2 \times 137 \times 0.85 - 0.6 \times 0.4}
$$

$$
t_{\text{min}} = \dfrac{800}{232.9 - 0.24} = \dfrac{800}{232.66} \approx 3.44 \text{ mm}
$$

Adding 2 mm corrosion allowance:

$$
t_{\text{design}} = 3.44 + 2.0 = 5.44 \text{ mm} \rightarrow \text{select } 6 \text{ mm plate}
$$

**Result:**
- (i) $D = 2.0$ m, $L = 5.0$ m
- (ii) $t_{\text{design}} = 6$ mm (standard plate size)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the corrosion allowance** in tanker thickness — a single 1-mark deduction is common.
> 2. **Not including mechanical efficiency $\eta$** in cylinder force equation — the examiner expects the *real* force, not the ideal one.
> 3. **Confusing tipping angle with material discharge angle** — design tip angle is **> 50°** but the *material's angle of repose* determines whether it actually slides out.
> 4. **Omitting the $0.6p$ term** in ASME formula — though often negligible, KTU expects it written explicitly for full credit.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Tipper Body** is open-top, gravity-discharged by hydraulic tilt around a **rear hinge**.
- [x] **Tanker Body** is sealed, pressure-or-pump discharged, designed as a **pressure vessel** to ASME / IS 4177.
- [x] **Hoist cylinder force** is *maximum at lift-off* ($\theta = 0°$), not at the full tipping angle.
- [x] **Tipping angle** required: **45°** for free-flowing, **55–60°** for cohesive loads.
- [x] **Working pressure** of tipper hoist system: **15 – 20 MPa**; safety relief set at **+10 %** above.
- [x] **Tank design pressure**: 0.3 MPa for non-hazardous, up to 1.7 MPa for LPG.
- [x] **Baffles** reduce sloshing mass by empirical factor **$k_s \approx 0.3$**.
- [x] **Baffle spacing**: every **1.5 × D**; thickness ≥ **3 mm**; cut-out at bottom.
- [x] **ASME hoop stress** formula: $\sigma = pD / (2t)$ — memorise this.
- [x] **Corrosion allowance**: **2 mm** standard for road tankers.
- [x] **Joint efficiency** $E$ for double-welded butt joint (spot radiographed) = **0.85**; fully radiographed = **1.00**.
- [x] **Standard materials**: IS 2062 (mild steel), SA 516 (pressure vessel steel), SA 240 (stainless for chemicals).
- [x] **Safety fittings** on a road tanker: P-V relief valve, bottom valve with emergency shut-off, flame arrestor, earthing reel.
- [x] **Tipper safety fittings**: hose-burst valve, mechanical body catch, pressure relief valve.
- [x] **Test pressure for tankers**: **1.3 ×** design pressure (hydrostatic).
- [x] **Test load for tippers**: **1.25 ×** rated payload (static tip trial at OEM yard).
- [x] **Difference in design philosophy**: Tipper = *structural fatigue dominant*; Tanker = *pressure + corrosion + fatigue* dominant.
- [x] **Tipper design codes**: IS 9433, OEM proprietary; **Tanker design codes**: IS 4177, ASME Sec. VIII, ADR.
- [x] **Exhausted gas recirculation (EGR) compatibility**: Tanker shells must allow for chassis frame flex of **± 15 mm** longitudinal.
- [x] **Chassis sub-frame for tipper**: built as separate bolted sub-assembly, not the main ladder frame.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic for KTU Viva:**
> **"TILT-PRESSURE"**
> **T** — Tipper = Tilt to dump
> **I** — IS 9433 (tipper), IS 4177 (tanker)
> **L** — Lift-off is the worst-case hoist load
> **T** — Tailgate auto-release at ≈ 10°
> **P** — Pressure-vessel codes govern tankers
> **R** — Relief valve at +10 % working pressure
> **E** — Ellipsoidal (2:1) heads
> **S** — Surge baffles every 1.5 D
> **S** — Safety catch on tipper; P-V valve on tanker
> **U** — U-bolt clamping allows thermal expansion
> **R** — Reinforcement rings prevent vacuum buckling
> **E** — Emergency shut-off cable on tanker discharge
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flow Visualization Techniques, Airflow Management Test
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT413 - VEHICLE BODY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Flow Visualization Techniques, Airflow Management Test

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Flow Visualization — The Formal Definition

In the context of **Vehicle Body Engineering** (KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 3), **Flow Visualization** is defined as the family of experimental aerodynamic techniques used to make otherwise invisible fluid streamlines, boundary layers, vortices, separation regions, and turbulent wakes *directly observable* around a vehicle body during controlled wind-tunnel or on-road testing. The objective is to identify, photographically or digitally record, and qualitatively (and sometimes quantitatively) interpret the spatial structure of the airflow over a car, truck, or two-wheeler.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Flow visualization is the *bridge between CFD prediction and physical validation*. No aerodynamic package is finally signed off in industry without at least one of these techniques being applied to a clay model, a 1:1 prototype, or a 25% wind-tunnel model.

## 1.2 Airflow Management Test — The Formal Definition

An **Airflow Management Test (AMT)** is a structured, instrumented evaluation program performed inside an automotive wind tunnel (or via CFD as a precursor) to quantify and qualify the behaviour of *functional* airflows entering, traversing, and leaving the vehicle. Unlike pure drag/lift testing that focuses on the body shell, AMT focuses on **mass-flow budgets** — engine cooling air, brake cooling air, HVAC cabin air, transmission cooling, exhaust extraction, windscreen defrost, wiper clearing, side-glass demist, and water/dirt management.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The Human Body:**
> Think of a car as a human swimmer. The swimmer must (a) reduce water resistance (aerodynamic drag), (b) keep the head above water (downforce / lift balance), and (c) breathe properly — *take in* fresh air (cooling/HVAC intake), *process* it (radiator, AC evaporator), and *expel* used air (underbody extraction, exhaust). Flow Visualization shows the swimmer's stroke pattern; Airflow Management Tests measure how much clean water the lungs are actually getting.

## 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in the KTU 2024 Scheme

The 2024 Scheme emphasises **Outcome-Based Education (OBE)** and design-thinking. COs for this module typically read: *"The student will be able to evaluate vehicle aerodynamic performance and conduct/interpret airflow experiments."* The assessment, therefore, does not stop at theory — students are expected to know which technique fits which engineering question, the standard wind-tunnel correction methods, and the Reynolds-number scaling rules.

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Reality:** A 14-mark question in KTU ESE on this topic will almost always pair a *technique description* (7 marks) with a *quantitative correction* or *test-procedure flowchart* (7 marks). Memorise both halves.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Flow Visualization Techniques

The KTU 2024 syllabus recognises four super-classes. Each class is tied to a specific engineering question the designer is trying to answer.

### 2.1.1 Surface Flow Visualization

Techniques that **paint the body surface itself** to reveal local skin-friction lines (streamlines on the wall), separation lines, and reattachment lines.

- **Tufts (Yarn Tapes):** Nylon or wool tufts glued to the surface. Their direction at test speed reveals local flow direction; their fluttering frequency correlates with local turbulence intensity. Used extensively on the A-pillars, C-pillars, rear-window, and underbody.
- **Oil-Film (Oil-Dot / Oil-Streak) Method:** A pigmented oil (e.g. **diesel oil + titanium dioxide** or **lampblack + kerosene**) brushed onto the model surface. At test speed, viscous shear drags the oil into streaks that map the wall streamlines. **Limitation:** only one instantaneous direction is captured; not suitable for unsteady separation.
- **Sublimation Method (Naphthalene / Camphor):** A volatile solid (typically naphthalene) coated on the model; mass transfer to the air stream erodes it fastest where the local mass-transfer coefficient (and hence skin friction) is highest. Leaves a "shadowgraph" of high-shear zones.
- **Temperature-Sensitive Paints (TSP):** Surface coated with phosphorescent or liquid-crystal paint. Under uniform illumination, luminescence decays faster in high-heat-transfer (high-shear) regions. Yields a 2-D map of `h(x,y)` — useful for **transition detection**.
- **Pressure-Sensitive Paints (PSP):** Oxygen-quenched luminescent paint; local O₂ partial pressure (≈ local static pressure) modulates emission intensity. Yields a 2-D `C_p` map across the entire body in a single test.

### 2.1.2 Tracer / Particle Methods

Techniques that **seed the air itself** so the flow becomes visible.

- **Smoke / Fog Generators:** Mineral oil droplets (~1 µm) injected upstream of the model. Captures 3-D vortex structures, wake roll-up, and base-flow reversal. Common in low-speed full-scale tunnels.
- **Vapour Screen:** Stage fog illuminated by a thin light sheet; ideal for showing *cross-sectional* velocity vectors.
- **Helium Bubbles / Soap Bubbles:** For low-speed water tunnels. Neutrally buoyant bubbles trace streamlines faithfully.
- **Laser-Induced Fluorescence (LIF):** Fluorescein or acetone vapour in a laser sheet; quantum yield maps scalar mixing — used in HVAC cabin-flow studies.
- **Particle Image Velocimetry (PIV):** The industrial gold standard. Oil droplets (~1 µm) illuminated by a pulsed Nd:YAG laser sheet. A double pulse yields two images; cross-correlation of sub-windows produces a full **2-D velocity vector field (u, v)** with sub-pixel accuracy. Modern *stereo-PIV* and *tomographic PIV* give 3-D-3-C data.

### 2.1.3 Optical (Density-Gradient) Methods

Techniques that exploit the **refractive index gradient** of the air itself — no tracers, no surface prep. Ideal for **shock-wave studies** and **thermal plume** visualisation.

- **Schlieren System:** Two-mirror (or two-lens) Z-configuration. A knife-edge at the focal plane blocks part of the deflected light; density gradients appear as bright/dark fringes. Quantitatively, the image intensity is proportional to the **density gradient perpendicular to the knife edge**.
- **Shadowgraph:** A point source + screen; density gradients cause ray deflection and second-derivative-of-density contrast. **More sensitive to bulk** than Schlieren but lower contrast.
- **Interferometry (Mach–Zehnder / Michelson):** Interference fringes shift in proportion to integrated density change. Quantitative; used in research, rarely in production AMT.

### 2.1.4 Computational Visualisation (Mentioned for completeness)

- **Streamline, Pathline, Streakline plots from CFD**; iso-surfaces of Q-criterion, λ₂, or vorticity magnitude. These are *post-processing* visualisations of velocity fields, not physical flow-visualisation, but the KTU syllabus expects the student to know the difference between a real test and a CFD post-process.

## 2.2 Airflow Management Test — Detailed Subsystems

A production AMT is typically organised into **six test families**, each with its own instrumentation. Understanding *what* is measured is as important as the test method itself.

### 2.2.1 Cooling System AMT (Engine + Radiator)

- **Measurement:** Coolant ΔT, air-side pressure drop, mass-flow rate, and fin effectiveness.
- **Instruments:** Multi-hole pressure probes (7-hole) traversing the grille opening, K-type thermocouple rakes, **anemometry arrays** at the radiator face.
- **Acceptance Criteria:** (i) uniform velocity distribution across radiator core (Uniformity Index $\eta_u \geq 0.85$); (ii) no hot-air re-circulation from engine bay to grille.
- **Hot-Cold Test:** Vehicle heat-soaked in a solar chamber, then run at top speed; *transient* radiator performance is logged.

### 2.2.2 Brake Cooling AMT

- Brake discs are instrumented with **thermocouples** at the rubbing surface. Run a series of controlled stops (e.g. 30 stops from 100 km/h). Post-run thermal mapping uses **infra-red thermography**.

### 2.2.3 HVAC / Cabin Comfort AMT

- A *thermal manikin* (sweating manikin, e.g. Newton-type) sits in the cabin. Air-supply temperature, velocity, and humidity are mapped at **breathing-zone** and **driver-foot** measurement points. ISO 7730 / ASHRAE 55 PMV-PPD indices are reported.

### 2.2.4 Underhood / Engine-Bay AMT

- The bonnet is removed; **7-hole pressure probes** and **PIV** map the air routing. Goal: ensure no hot re-circulation onto the exhaust manifold, no oil-foaming on the cam cover.

### 2.2.5 Water / Dirt Management AMT

- **Soiling Test:** Aerosolised chalk dust sprayed on the car at highway speed; post-run image analysis quantifies dirt deposition on side glass, rear lamp, body sides.
- **Water-Management Test:** Dye-coloured water sprayed on A-pillars, sunroof, door mirrors; flow paths to cabin-air intake and wiper park are traced.

### 2.2.6 Wind-Noise / Aeroacoustics AMT

- **Microphone arrays** (beamforming — e.g. HEAD-acoustics, Bruel & Kjaer) localise noise sources near A-pillars, mirror mounts, and roof rails. **SPL vs. frequency** is plotted from 200 Hz to 10 kHz.

## 2.3 The Wind-Tunnel Itself — Sub-system Map

| Sub-system | Function | Key Specification |
|---|---|---|
| **Contraction** | Accelerates flow, reduces turbulence | Contraction ratio ≥ 9:1 |
| **Test Section** | Houses the model | **Open** (no walls, good for 1:1) or **Closed** (better control) |
| **Nozzle** | Final acceleration | Bell-shaped; area ratio fixes M |
| **Diffuser** | Decelerates, recovers pressure | Divergence angle ≤ 5° |
| **Fan / Drive** | Provides momentum | Power up to 4 MW (full-scale) |
| **Boundary-Layer Suction** | Removes wall BL | Slotted wall + plenum |
| **Moving-Belt Ground Plane** | Simulates road motion | Belt speed = freestream $V_\infty$ |
| **Boundary-Layer Suction on Floor** | Matches atmospheric BL | Adjustable suction slot |
| **Force Balance** | Measures $F_D, F_L, F_S, M$ | Internal or external; 6-component |
| **Pressure Scanning System** | Maps $C_p$ | 256- to 1024-channel electronic scanners |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / Boundary Condition | Typical KTU Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho V L}{\mu} = \dfrac{V L}{\nu}$ | Reynolds number; $L$ = characteristic length (wheelbase for cars) | Scale-rule determination |
| 2 | $C_D = \dfrac{2 F_D}{\rho V_\infty^{2} \, A}$ | Drag coefficient; $A$ = frontal area | Closed-throat balancing |
| 3 | $C_L = \dfrac{2 F_L}{\rho V_\infty^{2} \, A}$ | Lift coefficient | Downforce verification |
| 4 | $C_S = \dfrac{2 F_S}{\rho V_\infty^{2} \, A}$ | Side-force coefficient | Crosswind stability |
| 5 | $C_M = \dfrac{2 M}{\rho V_\infty^{2} \, A \, L}$ | Yaw moment coefficient | Yaw stability |
| 6 | $C_p = \dfrac{p - p_\infty}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_\infty^{2}}$ | Pressure coefficient | Surface flow diagnostics |
| 7 | $q_\infty = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_\infty^{2}$ | Dynamic pressure (Pa) | Force / $C_p$ baseline |
| 8 | $\varepsilon_{bl} = 1 - \left(\dfrac{V_{model}}{V_\infty}\right)^{2}$ | Blockage correction (solid-blockage) | Wind-tunnel correction |
| 9 | $\varepsilon_{bl}^{wake} = \dfrac{1}{4} \dfrac{V_{model}}{V_\infty} \dfrac{C_D A}{C_{t}^{3/2}}$ | Wake-blockage (subsonic) | Open-throat correction |
| 10 | $C_{D,\psi} \approx C_{D,0} + k \psi^{2}$ | Drag variation with yaw angle $\psi$ (radians), $k \approx 0.0005$–$0.001$ per rad² | Yaw-sweep AMT |
| 11 | $Yaw \, Gain = \dfrac{C_{S,\psi}}{C_{D,0}}$ | Crosswind sensitivity index (per radian) | Crosswind safety KPI |
| 12 | $Mass \, Flow = \rho \int u \, dA$ | Continuity integral | Cooling-system AMT |
| 13 | $Uniformity \, Index = 1 - \dfrac{1}{2}\dfrac{\sum \vert u_i - \bar{u}\vert}{\bar{u} \, n}$ | Distribution uniformity (0–1, 1 = perfectly uniform) | Radiator flow quality |
| 14 | $\eta_{u} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{2\bar{u}}\left(u_{max}-u_{min}\right)$ | Simplified uniformity index (acceptable form) | Same as above |
| 15 | $C_f = \dfrac{\tau_w}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_\infty^{2}}$ | Skin-friction coefficient | Oil-film, TSP mapping |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary conditions to memorise for KTU exams:**
> 1. $C_p$ on the stagnation point of a bluff body → $+1.0$ (maximum).
> 2. $C_p$ on the base (rear of car) → typically $-0.2$ to $-0.4$.
> 3. Reynolds-number independence for a car is roughly achieved at $Re \geq 3 \times 10^{6}$ (based on wheelbase) — **above this, $C_D$ becomes scale-independent for shapes with no significant laminar separation**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** Without flow visualisation, the coolant fan would not know where to extract hot air; without AMT, the brake discs would overheat on the first mountain pass. The techniques in this module save *millions of dollars* in warranty costs and are central to the **homologation** (type-approval) of road vehicles.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Solid Blockage Correction

When a model is placed in a closed wind-tunnel test section, the air must **speed up around the model** because the same mass-flow is being squeezed through a smaller effective area. This means the *test-section velocity* $V_t$ is **higher** than the *free-air velocity* the car would see on the road $V_\infty$. The drag we measure in the tunnel, $F_{D,meas}$, is therefore too high. We correct it.

**Step 1 — Define the geometric blockage ratio.**

$$
\varepsilon = \frac{A_{model}}{A_{tunnel}}
$$

where $A_{model}$ is the maximum frontal area of the car (or scaled model) and $A_{tunnel}$ is the cross-sectional area of the empty test section.

**Step 2 — Mass conservation around the model.**

The air that cannot pass *through* the model must pass *around* it. Let $V_m$ be the local velocity at the model's shoulder. By continuity:

$$
A_{tunnel} \, V_t = \left(A_{tunnel} - A_{model}\right) V_m
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $V_m$.**

$$
V_m = \frac{V_t}{1 - \varepsilon}
$$

**Step 4 — Relate measured dynamic pressure to free-stream dynamic pressure.**

The dynamic pressure the model *actually* experiences is based on $V_m$, not $V_t$. The free-stream dynamic pressure is based on $V_\infty$ (what the car would see with no tunnel walls). Equating the two *free-stream* conditions:

$$
\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_\infty^{2} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_m^{2} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho \frac{V_t^{2}}{(1 - \varepsilon)^{2}}
$$

Therefore the **equivalent free-stream velocity** in the tunnel is:

$$
V_\infty = \frac{V_t}{1 - \varepsilon}
$$

**Step 5 — Correct the measured drag.**

The corrected drag coefficient uses $V_\infty$, but the area $A$ is still the model frontal area:

$$
C_{D,corrected} = \frac{2 F_{D,meas}}{\rho \, V_\infty^{2} \, A}
$$

Substituting the corrected velocity:

$$
C_{D,corrected} = \frac{2 F_{D,meas} \, (1 - \varepsilon)^{2}}{\rho \, V_t^{2} \, A}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Numerical Example:** A 25%-scale model with frontal area $0.55 \text{ m}^2$ is tested in a $6 \text{ m}^2$ test section. $\varepsilon = 0.55/6 = 0.0917$. If the measured $C_D$ (uncorrected) is $0.330$, then $C_{D,corrected} = 0.330 \times (1 - 0.0917)^{2} = 0.330 \times 0.8268 = 0.2729$. That is a **17% over-estimate** if uncorrected.

## 3.2 Derivation — Drag in Yaw (Crosswind)

When a car drives in a **crosswind** at yaw angle $\psi$ (degrees), drag increases. The KTU 2024 syllabus expects the student to derive the leading-order correction.

**Step 1 — Project the freestream onto car axes.**

The car sees a velocity component $V_\infty \cos\psi$ along its longitudinal axis and $V_\infty \sin\psi$ along the lateral axis. The effective dynamic pressure *along* the car is:

$$
q_{long} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho \left(V_\infty \cos\psi\right)^{2} = q_\infty \cos^{2}\psi
$$

**Step 2 — Add sideforce-induced drag (cross-coupling).**

The sideforce $F_S$ does no work *directly* on the car along its path, but it creates a *yaw moment* and induces additional pressure drag because the body is now operating in asymmetric flow. To first order in $\psi^{2}$ (small angles, $\psi < 15^{\circ}$):

$$
C_{D}(\psi) = C_{D}(0) + k \psi^{2}
$$

where $\psi$ is in radians. Typical production-sedan $k \approx 5 \times 10^{-4}$ per rad².

**Step 3 — Sideforce coefficient in yaw.**

Similarly:

$$
C_{S}(\psi) \approx C_{S,1}\, \psi
$$

where $C_{S,1}$ is the sideforce derivative, typically $0.7$–$1.2$ per radian for a sedan.

**Step 4 — Define crosswind sensitivity index (used by Euro NCAP).**

$$
S_{cw} = \frac{C_{S,1}}{C_{D,0}} \quad [\text{per radian}]
$$

A *low* $S_{cw}$ means the car resists side gusts well.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering reality check:** At $\psi = 10^{\circ}$ (≈ 0.175 rad), with $k = 5 \times 10^{-4}$, the drag rises by $k\psi^{2} = 1.53 \times 10^{-5}$ — small. But a truck-tractor at $\psi = 20^{\circ}$ sees $C_D$ rise by ~5–8%, and **a van at $\psi = 30^{\circ}$ can double its side-force** compared to straight-line. This is why vans are notoriously vulnerable to crosswind gusts.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Technique

| Engineering Question | Best Technique | Why | Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| Where is the boundary layer separating? | Oil-film + tufts | Wall streamlines go from attached → separated → reattached; visible streaks break | Qualitative streamline map |
| What is the local $C_p$ field? | PSP (Pressure-Sensitive Paint) | Non-intrusive, full-field | Quantitative $C_p(x,y)$ map |
| What is the 3-D vortex structure in the wake? | Smoke + PIV | Smoke shows structure; PIV gives velocity | Vector / streamline field |
| Is there a shock on a fast-ev vehicle (e.g. F1)? | Schlieren | Density-gradient sensitive | Density-gradient image |
| Where is transition on the body? | TSP (Temperature-Sensitive Paint) | Heat-transfer ∝ shear | 2-D transition map |
| Is the radiator getting uniform flow? | 7-hole probe rake at face | Quantitative mass-flow measurement | Velocity contours |
| Where is brake-disc heat building up? | IR thermography | Non-contact temperature | Thermal image |
| What is cabin-air delivery temperature? | Sweating manikin | Industry standard | PMV / PPD index |
| Where is the wind-noise source? | Microphone-array beamforming | Spatial resolution of acoustic sources | Sound-power map |
| Is the A-pillar causing water ingress? | Dye spray + video | Traces flow paths | Visual flow path |

## 3.4 Worked Numerical — PIV Resolution

A 25%-scale car model is tested in a low-speed tunnel at $V_\infty = 40 \text{ m/s}$. A PIV system has a 4 MP camera (2048 × 2048 pixels) and a 100 mm lens placed 1.2 m from the laser sheet. The field of view on the plane is 200 mm × 200 mm.

**Step 1 — Spatial resolution.**

Pixel size on the image plane:

$$
\Delta x = \frac{200 \text{ mm}}{2048} = 0.0977 \text{ mm/px}
$$

**Step 2 — Interrogation-window size.**

A typical 32 × 32 pixel window is used for cross-correlation:

$$
W = 32 \times 0.0977 = 3.125 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Vector spacing.**

With 50% overlap, vector grid spacing is $W/2 = 1.56 \text{ mm}$. Vectors per plane = $(128)^{2} = 16{,}384$.

**Step 4 — Minimum resolvable velocity.**

If the inter-pulse time is $\Delta t = 20 \text{ μs}$ and pixel resolution is 0.1 px, the minimum displacement is $0.1 \times 0.0977 = 0.00977 \text{ mm}$. Therefore:

$$
u_{min} = \frac{0.00977 \text{ mm}}{20 \text{ μs}} = 0.49 \text{ m/s}
$$

Below this velocity, the PIV will alias and produce noisy vectors. **A PIV user's rule of thumb is to design $\Delta t$ so that particle displacement is 4–8 pixels**, not 0.1.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Flow Visualization Technique Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Aerodynamic Question?] --> q1{Visualise flow\non surface?}
    q1 -- Yes --> surf{Surface or\nTracers?}
    q1 -- No --> q2{Visualise\nflow field in air?}
    surf -- Surface --> tuft[TUFT method]
    surf -- Surface --> oil[OIL FILM method]
    surf -- Surface --> sub[SUBLIMATION method]
    surf -- Surface --> tsp[TSP - Temp Paint]
    surf -- Surface --> psp[PSP - Pressure Paint]
    q2 -- Tracers --> tracers{Tracer type?}
    tracers -- Smoke --> smoke[SMOKE generator]
    tracers -- Particles --> piv[PARTICLE IMAGE\nVELOCIMETRY]
    tracers -- Dye --> lif[LASER INDUCED\nFLUORESCENCE]
    q2 -- Density Gradients --> optic{Optical method?}
    optic -- Schlieren --> schl[SCHLIEREN system]
    optic -- Shadowgraph --> shad[SHADOWGRAPH]
    optic -- Interferometry --> intf[INTERFEROMETER]
    tuft --> result[STREAMLINE MAP]
    oil --> result
    sub --> result
    tsp --> result
    psp --> result
    smoke --> result
    piv --> result
    lif --> result
    schl --> result
    shad --> result
    intf --> result
```

## 4.2 Wind Tunnel Block-Level Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ATMOS[Atmospheric Air] --> SET[Setting Chamber\nwith Honeycomb\nand Screens]
    SET --> CON[Contraction Cone\nratio 9 to 1]
    CON --> TS[Test Section\nwith Model]
    TS --> DIFF[Diffuser]
    DIFF --> FAN[Axial Fan\nup to 4 MW]
    FAN --> CORN[Corner Vanes]
    CORN --> SET
    TS --> BL[Boundary Layer\nSuction Slots]
    BL --> CORN
    TS --> MBP[Moving Belt\nGround Plane]
    MBP --> TS
    TS --> FB[6 Component\nForce Balance]
    FB --> DAQ[Data Acquisition\nSystem]
    DAQ --> CMP[Computer for\nCD CL CM CP]
    PIV_OPT[PIV Optical\nAccess Window] --> TS
```

## 4.3 Airflow Management Test Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    VIN[Vehicle Under Test\n1 to 1 or Scaled] --> WT[Wind Tunnel]
    WT --> COOL[Cooling System AMT]
    WT --> BRAKE[Brake Cooling AMT]
    WT --> HVAC[HVAC Cabin AMT]
    WT --> HOOD[Underhood AMT]
    WT --> WATER[Water Dirt AMT]
    WT --> WN[Wind Noise AMT]
    WT --> FORCE[Force Moment AMT\nCD CL CM]
    COOL --> D1[7 hole probes\nThermocouples]
    COOL --> D2[Mass Flow\nUniformity Index]
    BRAKE --> D3[IR Thermography\nThermocouples]
    HVAC --> D4[Manikin\nPMV PPD]
    HOOD --> D5[PIV\n7 hole probe]
    WATER --> D6[Soiling Spray\nDye Tracking]
    WN --> D7[Microphone Array\nBeamforming]
    FORCE --> D8[Force Balance\nPressure Scanner]
    D1 --> REP[Test Report]
    D2 --> REP
    D3 --> REP
    D4 --> REP
    D5 --> REP
    D6 --> REP
    D7 --> REP
    D8 --> REP
    REP --> SIG[Sign off for\nHomologation]
```

## 4.4 Boundary-Layer and Ground-Simulation Concept

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    INF[Incoming Atmospheric\nBoundary Layer] --> TS[Test Section Floor]
    TS --> SUC[Boundary Layer\nSuction Slot]
    SUC --> NEW[New Thin BL\non Test Floor]
    NEW --> BELT[Moving Belt\nSame Speed as Vinf]
    BELT --> CAR[Car Model]
    CAR --> OBS[Observed Flow\nMatches Road]
    INF -. Without Suction and Belt .-> TS2[Test Section Floor]
    TS2 --> BL2[Thick Slow BL\non Floor]
    BL2 --> CAR2[Car Model]
    CAR2 --> WRONG[Observed Flow\nDOES NOT Match Road]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram interpretation:** Without the **moving-belt ground plane** and the **boundary-layer suction slot**, the floor of the test section acts as a *stationary* wall. The car then "sees" a *thick* boundary layer (since the floor is at rest), which is **not** what a car experiences on a moving road. The moving belt matches the floor's surface speed to the freestream $V_\infty$, eliminating this artefact.

## 4.5 PIV Optical Setup Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    LASER[Nd YAG Laser\nDouble Pulse 532 nm] --> SHEET[Light Sheet\nOptics]
    SHEET --> REGION[Region of Interest\nin Test Section]
    REGION --> CAM[CCD or CMOS Camera\n4 to 16 MP]
    CAM --> SYNCH[Synchroniser]
    LASER --> SYNCH
    SYNCH --> TRIG[Trigger at\nTest Speed]
    TRIG --> LASER
    TRIG --> CAM
    CAM --> PROC[Cross Correlation\nProcessor]
    PROC --> VEC[Velocity Vector Field]
    VEC --> CFL[CFL or Tecplot\nPost Processing]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 marks each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between the Schlieren and Shadowgraph methods of flow visualisation. State two situations in which each is preferred in vehicle aerodynamic testing.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Schlieren | Shadowgraph |
|---|---|---|
| Optical basis | Knife-edge at focal plane blocks part of the deflected light from a parallel light beam | Point light source casts direct shadow of density-gradient field onto a screen |
| Sensitivity | Linear in $\partial \rho / \partial y$ (first spatial derivative) | Quadratic in $\partial^{2}\rho / \partial y^{2}$ (second spatial derivative) |
| Image contrast | High; sharp fringes | Lower; broad bright/dark patches |
| Quantitative? | Yes, after calibration | Mostly qualitative |

**Preferred in vehicle testing (1 + 1 = 2 marks):**
- **Schlieren** is preferred for detecting *localised* flow phenomena such as the leading-edge vortex on a wing-mirror, the small shock on a Formula-1 front wing endplate, or the density gradients above a hot radiator outlet. Its high spatial resolution and tunability make it ideal for fine features.
- **Shadowgraph** is preferred for *bulk* thermal plume visualisation — e.g. the convective plume above the engine bonnet of a stationary car in a solar soak test. It is also mechanically simple, so it is often the first choice in **on-road** flow-visualisation rigs where alignment is difficult.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is a "moving-belt ground plane" in an automotive wind tunnel, and why is it essential for accurate aerodynamic testing?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **moving-belt ground plane** (also called a **5-belt system** or **single-belt underbody**) is a continuous belt that travels at the same linear speed as the freestream airflow $V_\infty$ in the test section. It is placed immediately under the car model so that the surface in contact with the underbody flow is moving *with* the freestream rather than being stationary. *(1 mark)*

It is essential because in *real road* operation, the road surface *moves with* the car at the car's speed; equivalently, *relative to the car, the road is stationary, and the air moves at $V_\infty$*. In a wind tunnel with a fixed floor, the boundary layer on the floor grows very thick and slow, which the car's underbody *does not see* in reality. This leads to **artificially high** lift coefficients, **distorted** diffuser flows, and **incorrect** wake structures. The moving belt eliminates this artefact by matching the no-slip condition at the road. *(2 marks)*

---

## Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 marks each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`**
**(a)** With neat sketches, describe the **oil-film method** and the **sublimation method** of surface flow visualisation. State their relative advantages and limitations. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A 1:4 scale model of a passenger car (frontal area $0.60 \text{ m}^2$) is tested in a closed-throat wind tunnel of cross-section $6.0 \text{ m}^{2}$. The measured drag force at a tunnel test-section velocity of $45 \text{ m/s}$ is $38.5 \text{ N}$. Air density $\rho = 1.20 \text{ kg/m}^{3}$. Calculate the **corrected drag coefficient** after applying the solid-blockage correction. Comment on whether the blockage ratio is acceptable per KTU / industry practice. *(7 marks)*

---

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — 7 marks

**Oil-Film Method (≈ 3.5 marks):**

- A pigmented oil (e.g. **lampblack suspended in kerosene** or **TiO₂ in diesel oil**) is brushed uniformly onto the model's painted surface. The model is then run at the desired test speed for a fixed time (typically 30 s – 2 min). *(1 mark)*
- The viscous shear stress of the airflow drags the oil in the local flow direction. The pigments settle along the **wall streamlines**, leaving dark streaks on a light background. *(1 mark)*
- After the test, photographs are taken with the model stationary. The streaks are interpreted as wall-streamline patterns: *attached flow* gives smooth parallel lines; *separated flow* shows divergent / converging / recirculating streaks; *reattachment* shows a clear **saddle point** or **focus**. *(1 mark)*

**Sublimation Method (≈ 2.5 marks):**

- Naphthalene (or camphor) crystals are dissolved in a volatile carrier and brushed onto the surface; alternatively, the model is dipped in a saturated solution. As the solvent evaporates, a thin white layer of solid naphthalene remains. *(1 mark)*
- When the model is run, the naphthalene **sublimes** (solid → gas) at a rate proportional to the local **mass-transfer coefficient** $h_m$, which in turn is proportional to the local **skin-friction coefficient** $C_f$. *(0.5 mark)*
- After the test, the surface shows **dark patches where sublimation was high (high shear)** and **light patches where it was low (low shear / separation)**. The result is essentially a 2-D map of $C_f$. *(1 mark)*

**Comparative Table (≈ 1 mark):**

| Aspect | Oil-Film | Sublimation |
|---|---|---|
| What it shows | Wall streamlines (direction) | Shear magnitude (and indirectly, transition) |
| Quantitative? | No | Semi-quantitative |
| Captures unsteady flow? | No (single snapshot) | No (integrated over run) |
| Works in adverse pressure gradient? | Yes | Yes, but sublimation rate saturates |
| Best for | Separation / reattachment pattern | Transition detection, $C_f$ ranking |
| Cost | Very low | Low (requires ventilation — toxic fumes) |

---

#### Part (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Compute blockage ratio.**

$$
\varepsilon = \frac{A_{model}}{A_{tunnel}} = \frac{0.60}{6.00} = 0.100
$$

That is **10% blockage**. *(1 mark)*

**Step 2 — Write the corrected drag-coefficient formula.**

The corrected $C_D$ uses the *equivalent free-stream velocity*, not the test-section velocity:

$$
V_\infty = \frac{V_t}{1 - \varepsilon}
$$

$$
C_{D,corr} = \frac{2 F_{D,meas}}{\rho V_\infty^{2} A_{model}} = \frac{2 F_{D,meas} \,(1 - \varepsilon)^{2}}{\rho V_t^{2} A_{model}}
$$

*(1 mark)*

**Step 3 — Substitute values.**

$$
V_\infty = \frac{45}{1 - 0.100} = \frac{45}{0.900} = 50.00 \text{ m/s}
$$

*(1 mark)*

$$
C_{D,corr} = \frac{2 \times 38.5 \times (0.900)^{2}}{1.20 \times (50.00)^{2} \times 0.60}
$$

$$
C_{D,corr} = \frac{77.0 \times 0.81}{1.20 \times 2500 \times 0.60} = \frac{62.37}{1800} = 0.0347
$$

*(2 marks for arithmetic)*

**Step 4 — Comment on acceptability.**

Per industry practice (and KTU model-answer rubric), a solid-blockage ratio of **≤ 5% is considered ideal**, **5–10% is acceptable with correction**, and **> 10% is unacceptable** without extensive corrections (including wake-blockage and wall-interference). At 10%, the correction is borderline acceptable; **further test runs with a smaller model (e.g. 1:5 scale) are recommended** to reduce $\varepsilon$ to ≤ 5%. *(2 marks)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — where students lose marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the square on $(1-\varepsilon)$.** Many students write $C_{D,corr} = C_{D,meas}(1 - \varepsilon)$ instead of $(1-\varepsilon)^{2}$. Cost: **1 mark** per occurrence.
> 2. **Confusing $A_{model}$ with $A_{tunnel}$.** Use the *model* frontal area, not the tunnel area, in the $C_D$ denominator. Cost: **1 mark**.
> 3. **Quoting $C_D$ without units** is not penalised in KTU, but quoting $C_D$ as $0.0347$ *with a value of 0.347* is the most common arithmetic slip (forgetting the factor of 2 in the numerator, or squaring 50 wrongly). Always re-check the denominator.

---

### **Question B (Alternative Choice) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**
**(a)** Describe the **Particle Image Velocimetry (PIV)** technique in detail. With the aid of a labelled block diagram, explain the role of the laser, the camera, the synchroniser, and the cross-correlation processor. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A passenger car with a frontal area of $2.20 \text{ m}^{2}$ and a drag coefficient of $0.30$ is driven at $90 \text{ km/h}$ in still air. Calculate (i) the drag force, (ii) the power required to overcome drag, and (iii) the *corrected* drag force at the equivalent test-section velocity of $36 \text{ m/s}$ in a wind tunnel of cross-section $9.0 \text{ m}^{2}$ if the blockage correction is applied. Take $\rho = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^{3}$. *(7 marks)*

---

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — 7 marks

**PIV Description (5 marks) + Diagram (2 marks):**

PIV is a **non-intrusive, full-field velocity measurement** technique that captures a 2-D (or 3-D, in stereo/tomographic PIV) instantaneous velocity vector field in a planar slice of the flow. *(1 mark)*

**Procedure:**

1. **Seeding:** The flow is seeded with **tracer particles** — typically **1–3 µm olive-oil or DEHS droplets** that faithfully follow the flow. *(0.5 mark)*
2. **Illumination:** A **double-pulsed Nd:YAG laser** (typically 532 nm green, ~50–200 mJ/pulse, 5–10 ns pulse width) is expanded through a cylindrical lens into a **thin light sheet** (~1 mm thick) illuminating the measurement plane. *(0.5 mark)*
3. **Imaging:** A **CCD or CMOS camera** (resolution typically 1–16 MP), positioned perpendicular to the laser sheet and equipped with a bandpass filter to reject ambient light, records **two successive images** of the illuminated particles separated by a small inter-pulse time $\Delta t$ (typically 1 µs – 1 ms). *(0.5 mark)*
4. **Synchronisation:** A **synchroniser (timer/processor)** triggers the two laser pulses and the two camera frame exposures with sub-microsecond accuracy, ensuring the two images are taken at known time separation $\Delta t$. *(0.5 mark)*
5. **Cross-Correlation:** Each image is divided into small **interrogation windows** (e.g. 16 × 16 or 32 × 32 pixels). For each window, a **2-D cross-correlation** is performed between the *first* and *second* image. The peak of the correlation function gives the **mean particle displacement** $\Delta x, \Delta y$ within that window during $\Delta t$. *(0.5 mark)*
6. **Velocity Computation:** The velocity vector in each window is:
$$
\vec{u} = \frac{\vec{\Delta s}}{\Delta t}
$$
With sub-pixel accuracy (via Gaussian peak-fitting or sub-pixel interpolation), the velocity precision is typically **±0.1 pixel / $\Delta t$**, often **< 1% of the freestream velocity**. *(0.5 mark)*
7. **Post-Processing:** Vector validation (median filter, peak-ratio validation), outlier removal, and visualisation as **vector arrows, streamline contours, or vorticity maps**. Modern PIV can deliver **10,000+ vectors per frame at kHz rates** (Time-Resolved PIV). *(0.5 mark)*

**Labelled block diagram (2 marks):** Draw the synchroniser at the centre, with arrows going *out* to the laser and the camera; arrows going *in* from camera to the cross-correlation processor; and a final arrow from the processor to the velocity vector display. (See Section 4.5 of these notes for the Mermaid version.)

**Advantages:** Non-intrusive; full-field; resolves unsteady vortices; quantitative.
**Limitations:** Cost; requires optical access; only 2-D plane per setup (stereo-PIV needed for 3-D).

---

#### Part (b) — 7 marks

**Given:**
- $A = 2.20 \text{ m}^{2}$
- $C_D = 0.30$
- On-road speed $V_{road} = 90 \text{ km/h} = 25 \text{ m/s}$
- Test-section speed $V_t = 36 \text{ m/s}$
- $A_{tunnel} = 9.0 \text{ m}^{2}$
- $\rho = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^{3}$

**Step 1 — Blockage ratio.**

$$
\varepsilon = \frac{2.20}{9.00} = 0.2444
$$

**This is well above 10%** — the blockage is severe. We still apply the correction because the problem instructs it, but in practice this test is *invalid* without a smaller model. *(1 mark)*

**Part (i) — On-road drag force:**

$$
F_{D,road} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^{2} A C_D
$$

$$
F_{D,road} = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 1.225 \times (25)^{2} \times 2.20 \times 0.30
$$

$$
F_{D,road} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 625 \times 2.20 \times 0.30
$$

$$
F_{D,road} = 252.97 \text{ N} \approx 253 \text{ N}
$$

*(2 marks)*

**Part (ii) — Power to overcome drag:**

$$
