P = F_{D,road} \times V = 252.97 \times 25 = 6324 \text{ W} \approx 6.32 \text{ kW}
$$

*(1 mark)*

**Part (iii) — Corrected drag in tunnel at $V_t = 36 \text{ m/s}$:**

Equivalent free-stream velocity:

$$
V_\infty = \frac{V_t}{1 - \varepsilon} = \frac{36}{1 - 0.2444} = \frac{36}{0.7556} = 47.65 \text{ m/s}
$$

*(1 mark)*

Corrected drag force (using the *measured* drag that would have been obtained in the tunnel):

The drag that the balance *reads* in the tunnel at $V_t = 36$ m/s is based on the *test-section* dynamic pressure, but the *corrected* drag is computed using $V_\infty$:

$$
F_{D,corr} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V_\infty^{2} A C_D = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 1.225 \times (47.65)^{2} \times 2.20 \times 0.30
$$

$$
F_{D,corr} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 2270.5 \times 2.20 \times 0.30
$$

$$
F_{D,corr} = 918.5 \text{ N} \approx 919 \text{ N}
$$

*(1 mark)*

**Final ratio — uncorrected vs corrected:**

Uncorrected drag at $V_t = 36$ m/s would be:

$$
F_{D,uncorr} = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 1.225 \times 36^{2} \times 2.20 \times 0.30 = 524.4 \text{ N}
$$

Correction factor = $F_{D,corr} / F_{D,uncorr} = (1-\varepsilon)^{-2} = (0.7556)^{-2} = 1.751$. So the corrected drag is **75% higher** than the apparent reading — again confirming that the test is invalid. *(1 mark)*

---

## 5.X KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 ways students LOSE marks on this topic in KTU 2024 ESE:**
> 1. **Mixing up surface flow and tracer flow techniques** — the examiner *will* deduct for describing "tufts" when the question is about "PIV" or vice versa. Memorise the *decision-tree in Section 4.1* and use it to structure your answer.
> 2. **Forgetting the $(1-\varepsilon)^{2}$ factor** in the blockage correction. This is the single most common 2-mark error.
> 3. **Conflating drag, lift, side-force and moment coefficient formulas** — make sure you use the right area $A$ (frontal) and the right reference length $L$ (wheelbase for $C_M$).
> 4. **Omitting units** in numerical problems — KTU awards *partial* marks only if the unit is correct. A force in N (not m/s, not kg) earns full credit.
> 5. **Skipping the diagram** — KTU paper valuation explicitly allocates 2 marks for a *labelled sketch* in any 7-mark sub-question on visualisation techniques. A 5-line ASCII / Mermaid-style sketch is enough; you do not need CAD-quality art.

---

## 5.Y Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Flow visualisation** = making the invisible visible. Four super-classes: **surface** (tufts, oil-film, sublimation, TSP, PSP), **tracer** (smoke, PIV, LIF), **optical density-gradient** (Schlieren, Shadowgraph, Interferometry), and **computational** (CFD post-processing).
- **Airflow management tests** focus on *mass-flow budgets* (cooling, HVAC, brake, water/dirt, wind-noise) — not on $C_D, C_L$ alone.
- The **wind-tunnel corrections** every student must know by heart: (i) **solid-blockage** $\varepsilon = A_m/A_t$, (ii) **wake-blockage** (open-throat), (iii) **boundary-layer growth on the floor** (mitigated by **suction slot + moving belt**).
- The **blockage-corrected drag formula** is:
$$C_{D,corr} = \dfrac{2 F_{D,meas}\,(1-\varepsilon)^{2}}{\rho V_t^{2} A_{model}}$$
- The **yaw-drag relation** to first order is $C_D(\psi) = C_{D,0} + k\psi^{2}$ (with $\psi$ in radians).
- **Stagnation point** of a bluff body has $C_p = +1.0$; **base** of a car has $C_p \approx -0.2$ to $-0.4$.
- A **5-belt moving ground plane** simulates road motion; **boundary-layer suction** simulates atmospheric BL; both are *essential* for road-realistic results.
- **PIV** is the industrial gold standard for 2-D / 3-D velocity fields: 1–3 µm oil droplets + double-pulsed Nd:YAG laser + cross-correlation.
- **Schlieren** is linear in $\partial\rho/\partial y$; **Shadowgraph** is quadratic in $\partial^{2}\rho/\partial y^{2}$. Schlieren is sharper; Shadowgraph is mechanically simpler.
- **Industry-acceptable blockage ratio** is **≤ 5% ideal**, **5–10% with correction**, **> 10% invalid** without major corrections.
- **Reynolds-number independence** for car shapes is typically achieved at $Re \geq 3 \times 10^{6}$ based on wheelbase.
- **Uniformity Index** $\eta_u \geq 0.85$ is the typical acceptance criterion for a radiator's incoming airflow.
- **Euro NCAP crosswind sensitivity index** $S_{cw} = C_{S,1}/C_{D,0}$ — lower is safer.
- **KTU hot keywords** that *must* appear in your answer for full marks: *blockage correction*, *moving-belt ground plane*, *boundary-layer suction*, *pressure coefficient*, *Reynolds-number independence*, *cross-correlation*, *knife edge*, *tumbling tufts vs. aligned tufts*, *Yaw sweep*, *PMV/PPD index*, *beamforming*, *frontof-radiator uniformity*.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-minute revision mantra (10 seconds):** *Visualise — Block — Correct — Measure.* (1) **Visualise** the flow with the right technique. (2) **Block**-test-set-up must be small enough to keep $\varepsilon \leq 0.05$. (3) **Correct** for blockage and wall interference. (4) **Measure** the right physical quantity — drag, lift, $C_p$, mass flow, temperature, sound — with the right sensor.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Measurement of Various Forces and Moments by Using Wind Tunnel
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT413 - VEHICLE BODY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Measurement of Various Forces and Moments by Using Wind Tunnel

# Module 3 — Measurement of Various Forces and Moments by Using Wind Tunnel

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In Vehicle Body Engineering, **wind tunnel measurement of forces and moments** refers to the experimental determination of the **six aerodynamic components** (three forces and three moments) acting on a vehicle body when subjected to a controlled, simulated airflow. The six components are measured simultaneously using a multi-component **force balance** (also called a *strain gauge balance* or *load cell*) mechanically coupled to the vehicle model, which is suspended in the test section of the wind tunnel.

The three forces are:
- **Drag (D / Fx)** — along the longitudinal axis of the vehicle.
- **Lift (L / Fz)** — along the vertical axis.
- **Side Force (S / Fy)** — along the lateral axis.

The three moments are:
- **Rolling Moment (Rl / Mx)** — about the longitudinal axis.
- **Pitching Moment (Pm / My)** — about the lateral axis.
- **Yawing Moment (Ym / Mz)** — about the vertical axis.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Exact):**
> *A wind tunnel balance is a precision electromechanical instrument that resolves the total aerodynamic load on a test article into its six Cartesian force and moment components, expressed in the body-fixed wind-axis reference frame, by measuring strain induced in precisely calibrated elastic flexures.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine standing at the edge of a swimming pool and pushing a small toy boat through the water. The boat pushes back on your hand — that push along the direction of motion is *drag*. If the boat tilts upward (water rushing under the hull lifts it), that vertical push is *lift*. Now if you try to twist the boat left or right, the water resists — that twisting sensation is the *yawing moment*. A wind tunnel is simply a giant, controlled "swimming pool for air," and a **force balance** is the ultra-sensitive "hand" inside the tunnel that *feels* the air pushing on the vehicle and translates that push into precise electrical signals.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters in Vehicle Body Engineering:**
> - **Fuel economy** is dominated by drag (≈ 50–60% of fuel consumption at highway speeds).
> - **High-speed stability** is governed by lift, pitching moment, and yawing moment.
> - **Crosswind safety** depends on side force and yawing moment.
> - **NVH (Noise, Vibration, Harshness)** is influenced by wake and pressure distribution.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Reference Constants

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard Unit | Typical Vehicle Magnitude |
|--------|----------|---------------|--------------------------|
| $C_D$ | Drag Coefficient | dimensionless | **0.25 – 0.40** |
| $C_L$ | Lift Coefficient | dimensionless | **0.10 – 0.50** (negative preferred) |
| $C_S$ | Side-Force Coefficient | dimensionless | **0.10 – 0.40** |
| $C_{Rl}$ | Rolling-Moment Coefficient | dimensionless | **0.01 – 0.10** |
| $C_{Pm}$ | Pitching-Moment Coefficient | dimensionless | **0.05 – 0.20** |
| $C_{Ym}$ | Yawing-Moment Coefficient | dimensionless | **0.01 – 0.15** |
| $\rho$ | Air Density at STP | **1.225 kg/m³** | — |
| $V_\infty$ | Free-stream Velocity | m/s | 30 – 60 m/s typical |
| $A$ | Frontal Reference Area | m² | 1.8 – 2.5 m² |
| $L$ | Wheelbase Reference Length | m | 2.4 – 3.0 m |
| $q_\infty$ | Dynamic Pressure | Pa = N/m² | $\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_\infty^2$ |

> [!NOTE]
> All KTU examination questions involving vehicle aerodynamic coefficients use **$\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³** unless otherwise specified, and assume the **wind-axis system** with origin at the center of the test-section floor projection of the wheelbase/2.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Six-Component Force Balance — Operating Principle

A modern **six-component internal strain-gauge balance** is the gold standard for vehicle aerodynamic testing. It works on the following principle:

1. The vehicle model (typically a **1:1 full-scale** or **1:4 scale** clay/rapid-prototype body) is rigidly attached to the balance via a sting, strut, or floor-mount system.
2. The balance body contains an array of **calibrated elastic flexures** machined from a single block of high-grade steel or aluminum.
3. As airflow exerts force on the model, the flexures deform by **micrometers**.
4. **Strain gauges** (Wheatstone bridge circuits) bonded to the flexures convert this micro-deformation into **millivolt-level electrical signals**.
5. A **data acquisition system (DAS)** amplifies, filters, and converts the signals using a pre-calibrated **linear matrix** of calibration coefficients $K_{ij}$ to resolve the six force/moment components.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Measurement

1. **Model Installation** — The vehicle model is attached so that the **wind axis origin** coincides with the **balance moment center**. Misalignment > 0.1 mm causes significant cross-talk errors.
2. **Tare/Zero Calibration** — With the tunnel off, the DAS records the zero offsets of all six channels.
3. **Tunnel Start-up** — Wind velocity is ramped gradually to avoid shock-loading the balance.
4. **Steady-State Sampling** — At each test point, data is sampled at ≥ 1 kHz for ≥ 10 s and **time-averaged**.
5. **Pressure/Temperature Correction** — Measured dynamic pressure $q_\infty$ and temperature $T$ are corrected to standard day conditions using:
   
   $$q_\infty = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,V_\infty^2, \quad \rho = \frac{p}{R\,T}$$

6. **Coefficient Calculation** — Each measured force/moment is non-dimensionalized using $q_\infty$ and the reference area/length.
7. **Blockage Correction** — For models occupying > 5% of test section, **Maskell's blockage correction** is applied:

   $$\varepsilon_{block} = \frac{C_D}{1 - k_1\, C_D\,A_S/A_T}$$

   where $A_S$ is model frontal area, $A_T$ is tunnel cross-section area.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Use in KTU Problems |
|---|---------|---------|----------------------------|
| 1 | $F_D = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,V_\infty^2\,A\,C_D$ | **Drag Force** | Compute drag at given speed |
| 2 | $F_L = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,V_\infty^2\,A\,C_L$ | **Lift Force** | High-speed stability analysis |
| 3 | $F_S = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,V_\infty^2\,A\,C_S$ | **Side Force** | Crosswind analysis |
| 4 | $M_P = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,V_\infty^2\,A\,L\,C_{Pm}$ | **Pitching Moment** | Acceleration/braking stability |
| 5 | $M_Y = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,V_\infty^2\,A\,L\,C_{Ym}$ | **Yawing Moment** | Crosswind/yaw stability |
| 6 | $M_R = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,V_\infty^2\,A\,L\,C_{Rl}$ | **Rolling Moment** | Cornering aerodynamics |
| 7 | $C_D = \dfrac{F_D}{q_\infty\,A} = \dfrac{2F_D}{\rho\,V_\infty^2\,A}$ | **Drag Coefficient** | Convert measured force to $C_D$ |
| 8 | $\text{Re} = \dfrac{\rho\,V_\infty\,L_c}{\mu}$ | **Reynolds Number** | Match full-scale flow conditions |
| 9 | $M = V_\infty / a$ | **Mach Number** | Confirm incompressible flow ($M < 0.3$) |
| 10 | $P_{shaft} = F_D \cdot V_\infty$ | **Aerodynamic Power Loss** | Energy/fuel-economy problems |
| 11 | $C_{M,xCG} = C_{M,xref} + \dfrac{x_{ref}-x_{CG}}{L}\,C_L$ | **Moment Center Transfer** | Shift moment reference to CG |
| 12 | $\dfrac{C_D}{C_{L}^{3/2}} = \text{Aero. Efficiency Index}$ | **Aerodynamic Efficiency** | Vehicle optimization |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory Trick for KTU:**
> *All six aerodynamic components share the same "skeleton":* $q_\infty \times \text{(Area or Area×Length)} \times \text{Coefficient}$. *Memorize $q_\infty$ and the rest is just multipliers.*

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Formula 1 (Drag)** is the direct input to BS-VI/Euro 7 fuel-economy and **CO₂ emission** homologation testing.
- **Formula 4 (Pitching Moment)** governs the **dive-and-squat** behavior of passenger cars and the **high-speed porpoising** of trucks.
- **Formula 5 (Yawing Moment)** is the cornerstone of **crosswind sensitivity** analysis (e.g., T-junction gusts on highways).
- **Formula 8 (Reynolds Number)** is used to validate wind-tunnel model tests against full-scale conditions; if $\text{Re}_{model} \neq \text{Re}_{full}$, results are not directly transferable.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Measured Force to Dimensionless Coefficient

**Given:** A 1:4 scale model of a passenger car is tested in a wind tunnel.
- Test-section air density: $\rho = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- Free-stream velocity: $V_\infty = 40 \text{ m/s}$
- Measured drag force: $F_D = 12.6 \text{ N}$
- Frontal area of model: $A_m = 0.125 \text{ m}^2$

**Find:** (a) Dynamic pressure $q_\infty$ (b) Drag coefficient $C_D$ (c) Drag force at full scale (prototype) at $V = 160 \text{ km/h}$.

#### Step (a): Dynamic Pressure

The dynamic pressure is the kinetic energy per unit volume of the moving air:

$$q_\infty = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,V_\infty^2$$

Substituting the values:

$$q_\infty = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 1.225 \times (40)^2$$

$$q_\infty = 0.6125 \times 1600$$

$$\boxed{q_\infty = 980 \text{ Pa} = 980 \text{ N/m}^2}$$

> **Logic:** $V_\infty^2$ gives 1600; multiply by 1.225 to get 1960; halve to get 980 Pa.

#### Step (b): Drag Coefficient

By definition:

$$C_D = \frac{F_D}{q_\infty \, A_m}$$

$$C_D = \frac{12.6}{980 \times 0.125}$$

$$C_D = \frac{12.6}{122.5}$$

$$\boxed{C_D = 0.1029 \approx 0.103}$$

> **Logic:** The model has low $C_D$ because it is an idealized prototype; production cars typically have $C_D \approx 0.28$–$0.32$.

#### Step (c): Full-Scale Drag Force at 160 km/h

**Convert speed to SI units:**

$$V_p = 160 \text{ km/h} = \frac{160 \times 1000}{3600} = 44.44 \text{ m/s}$$

**Prototype frontal area (scale ratio squared):**

$$A_p = A_m \times 4^2 = 0.125 \times 16 = 2.0 \text{ m}^2$$

**Prototype dynamic pressure:**

$$q_{\infty,p} = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 1.225 \times (44.44)^2$$

$$q_{\infty,p} = 0.6125 \times 1975.0$$

$$q_{\infty,p} \approx 1209.7 \text{ Pa}$$

**Full-scale drag force** (assuming Reynolds-number independence of $C_D$, valid for $\text{Re} > 10^6$):

$$F_{D,p} = q_{\infty,p} \times A_p \times C_D$$

$$F_{D,p} = 1209.7 \times 2.0 \times 0.103$$

$$F_{D,p} = 249.2 \text{ N}$$

**Aerodynamic power loss:**

$$P_{shaft} = F_{D,p} \times V_p = 249.2 \times 44.44 \approx 11{,}074 \text{ W} \approx 11.1 \text{ kW}$$

$$\boxed{F_{D,p} \approx 249.2 \text{ N}, \quad P \approx 11.1 \text{ kW}}$$

> **Logic Check:** At 160 km/h, ≈ 11 kW of engine power is consumed just to overcome drag — roughly 25% of total engine output of a typical 1.5 L sedan.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Moment Center Transfer (Pitching Moment about CG)

**Problem:** The pitching moment is measured about the rear-axle reference point: $M_{Pm,rear} = 320$ N·m, with $C_{Pm,rear} = 0.12$ at $V_\infty = 30$ m/s. Find the pitching moment about the center of gravity, which is **1.2 m ahead** of the rear axle. Wheelbase $L = 2.4$ m, frontal area $A = 2.0$ m².

**Formula:**

$$C_{Pm,CG} = C_{Pm,rear} + \left(\frac{x_{rear} - x_{CG}}{L}\right) \cdot C_L$$

Assuming the lift coefficient at this condition is $C_L = 0.20$ (measured):

$$x_{rear} - x_{CG} = 0 - (-1.2) = +1.2 \text{ m}$$

$$C_{Pm,CG} = 0.12 + \left(\frac{1.2}{2.4}\right)(0.20)$$

$$C_{Pm,CG} = 0.12 + (0.5)(0.20)$$

$$C_{Pm,CG} = 0.12 + 0.10$$

$$\boxed{C_{Pm,CG} = 0.22}$$

**Convert to physical moment:**

$$q_\infty = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 1.225 \times 30^2 = 0.6125 \times 900 = 551.25 \text{ Pa}$$

$$M_{Pm,CG} = q_\infty \cdot A \cdot L \cdot C_{Pm,CG}$$

$$M_{Pm,CG} = 551.25 \times 2.0 \times 2.4 \times 0.22$$

$$\boxed{M_{Pm,CG} \approx 582.2 \text{ N·m}}$$

> **Logic:** Moving the reference point forward (toward CG) increases the pitching moment if lift is positive (the lift force creates a larger arm about the new forward point). This is the *physical* reason cars pitch forward on acceleration.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — How to Choose the Right Formula in the KTU Exam

| Exam Cue in Question | Use This Formula | Common Student Mistake |
|----------------------|------------------|------------------------|
| "At a speed of X km/h, find drag" | $F_D = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 A C_D$ | Forgetting to convert km/h to m/s |
| "Find $C_D$ from measured force" | $C_D = F_D / (q_\infty A)$ | Forgetting to compute $q_\infty$ first |
| "About the CG" / "about a point ahead/behind" | Moment center transfer formula | Treating $C_M$ as constant across references |
| "Coefficient" mentioned | Always $\text{force}/(q_\infty A)$ or $\text{moment}/(q_\infty A L)$ | Mixing force and moment formulas |
| "Aerodynamic power" | $P = F_D \cdot V_\infty$ | Using $V$ instead of $V_\infty$ or forgetting the dot product |
| "Same $C_D$, different speed" | $F_D \propto V^2$ | Assuming linear scaling |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Six-Component Force Balance Data Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Aerodynamic Load on Vehicle Model] --> A2[Sting Strut Mounting]
    A2 --> A3[Six-Component Force Balance]
    A3 --> A4[Strain Gauge Wheatstone Bridges]
    A4 --> A5[Amplifier and Filter Unit]
    A5 --> A6[Analog to Digital Converter]
    A6 --> A7[Data Acquisition System DAS]
    A7 --> A8[Calibration Matrix Multiplier Kij]
    A8 --> A9[Resolved Six Forces and Moments Fx Fy Fz Mx My Mz]
    A9 --> A10[Dynamic Pressure q infinity from Pitot Probe]
    A10 --> A11[Non Dimensionalization by qA or qAL]
    A11 --> A12[Final Coefficients CD CL CS CRl CPm CYm]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Complete Wind Tunnel Test Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Define Test Objective] --> P2[Select Model Scale and Reference Geometry]
    P2 --> P3[Install Model on Balance and Align Wind Axis]
    P3 --> P4[Tare and Zero the Six Channels]
    P4 --> P5[Set Tunnel Velocity to V infinity]
    P5 --> P6[Sweep Yaw Angle from minus 10 deg to plus 10 deg]
    P6 --> P7[Record Steady State Time Averaged Signals]
    P7 --> P8[Apply Pressure Temperature and Blockage Correction]
    P8 --> P9[Compute Non Dimensional Coefficients]
    P9 --> P10[Validate Repeatability and Uncertainty]
    P10 --> P11[Report Six Coefficients vs Yaw and Speed]
```

### 4.3 Functional Architecture — Coordinate Systems in Vehicle Aerodynamics

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph WIND_AXIS [Wind Axis System Fixed to Free Stream]
        WX[X axis along V infinity Drag direction]
        WY[Y axis lateral to flow Side Force]
        WZ[Z axis vertical Lift direction]
    end
    subgraph BODY_AXIS [Body Axis System Fixed to Vehicle]
        BX[X axis along vehicle longitudinal]
        BY[Y axis along vehicle lateral]
        BZ[Z axis along vehicle vertical]
    end
    subgraph BETA[Slip or Yaw Angle Beta]
        VEC[Free stream velocity vector V infinity]
        ANGLE[Angle between WX and BX]
    end
    WIND_AXIS --- BODY_AXIS
    BODY_AXIS --- BETA
    BETA --- WIND_AXIS
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram — Blockage Correction Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M[Measured Drag Force F D measured] --> C1[Compute Model Frontal Area A S]
    C1 --> C2[Compute Tunnel Cross Section A T]
    C2 --> C3[Blockage Ratio sigma = A S by A T]
    M --> C4[Initial Drag Coefficient C D raw]
    C3 --> C5[Apply Maskell or Munk Correction]
    C4 --> C5
    C5 --> C6[Corrected C D true]
    C6 --> C7[Final Reportable Drag Coefficient]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Each block represents a physical *function*, not a component. The arrows show the **flow of information** (data or control signals) during a single test run. The wind tunnel itself is implicit as the "controlled environment" that holds the model and balance at a fixed $V_\infty$ and yaw angle $\beta$.
