# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Body-in-White (BIW) and list any four materials used in its construction. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**Model Answer (3 Marks)**:

**Definition (2 Marks):**
> The Body-in-White (BIW) is the stage in automobile manufacturing where the car body sheet metal components have been welded together — typically using resistance spot welding — but **before** the application of paint (painting process) and **before** the assembly of moving parts (doors, hood, fenders, glass) and the powertrain (engine, transmission).

**Four Materials (1 Mark — ¼ each):**
1. Mild Steel (low-carbon steel) — floor pan, inner panels
2. High-Strength Steel (HSS) — cross members, reinforcements
3. Aluminum Alloys — hood, engine cradle
4. Polypropylene (PP) / SMC — bumpers, fenders

**Mark Allocation Breakdown:**
| Component | Marks |
|-----------|-------|
| BIW Definition | 2 |
| Any 4 materials | 1 |

---

### Question 2: List any three properties required of a steel sheet used in automotive body construction. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**Model Answer (3 Marks — 1 each):**

1. **Formability** — The steel sheet must be able to undergo plastic deformation (deep drawing, bending, stretching) during stamping operations without cracking. Measured by **Lankford coefficient (r-value)** and **strain-hardening exponent (n-value)**.

2. **Weldability** — The sheet must be amenable to **resistance spot welding (RSW)**, the most common BIW joining method. Good weldability means no brittle weld nugget formation and consistent weld strength.

3. **Corrosion Resistance** — The sheet should resist environmental degradation. Achieved through metallic coatings (galvanizing, galvannealing) and the paint system (ED coat + primer + basecoat + clearcoat).

**Alternate acceptable answers** (any three): Strength-to-weight ratio, crash energy absorption, paintability, recyclability, dimensional accuracy.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) – KTU ESE Pattern with Internal Choice

### Question A: (Choice 1) – 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q. (a)** Explain in detail the classification of steel sheets used in automotive body construction. Compare the mechanical properties of Mild Steel, HSS, AHSS, and UHSS. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (1 Mark):**
Steel sheets are classified based on tensile strength, microstructure, and formability. The four main categories used in BIW are Mild Steel, HSS, AHSS, and UHSS.

**Mild Steel (MS) / Low-Carbon Steel (1.5 Marks):**
- Carbon content: **0.05–0.25%**
- Yield strength: **140–250 MPa**, UTS: **270–410 MPa**
- Elongation: **25–40%** (excellent ductility)
- Microstructure: Predominantly ferrite with some pearlite
- Applications: Floor pan, inner panels, non-structural reinforcement
- Forming: Easy, supports deep drawing
- Variants: IF (Interstitial Free), BH (Bake Hardenable), CMn (Carbon-Manganese)

**High-Strength Steel (HSS) (1.5 Marks):**
- Yield strength: **210–550 MPa**, UTS: **340–780 MPa**
- Strengthening mechanisms: Solid-solution, grain refinement, precipitation
- Applications: Cross members, longitudinal rails, reinforcements
- Forming: Moderate complexity, conventional cold stamping

**Advanced High-Strength Steel (AHSS) (1.5 Marks):**
- Yield strength: **420–950 MPa**, UTS: **780–1500 MPa**
- Multi-phase microstructure: Ferrite + Martensite + Bainite + Retained Austenite
- **DP (Dual Phase)**: Soft ferrite + hard martensite — work-hardening behaviour
- **TRIP (Transformation-Induced Plasticity)**: Retained austenite transforms to martensite during deformation, giving high elongation
- **CP (Complex Phase)**: Fine bainite + martensite — high fatigue strength
- Applications: B-pillar, side impact beams, door rings

**Ultra-High-Strength Steel (UHSS) (1.5 Marks):**
- UTS: **> 1500 MPa**, YS: **950–1100 MPa**
- Microstructure: Predominantly martensitic
- Formed by **hot stamping (press hardening)** of boron steel (e.g., 22MnB5)
- Applications: A-pillar, bumper beam, rocker reinforcement
- Special welding required (laser welding, tailored blanks)

---

**Q. (b)** Describe the complete painting process flow in an automotive plant. List the four major paint layers with their typical thickness and function. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Pre-treatment Stage (1.5 Marks):**
The BIW undergoes the following pre-treatment operations:
1. **Degreasing** — Alkaline spray to remove stamping oils and dirt
2. **Water Rinse** — Removal of degreasing solution
3. **Phosphating** — Zinc-phosphate conversion coating (1–3 µm) for corrosion protection and paint adhesion
4. **Passivation** — Chromate-free rinse (zirconium/titanium based) for enhanced phosphate stability

**ED Coat (Electrodeposition) Stage (1.5 Marks):**
- BIW is fully immersed in a paint tank
- BIW acts as the **cathode** in modern cathodic ED systems
- Voltage: **200–350 V DC**, Bath temperature: **28–32 °C**
- Coating thickness: **18–25 µm** (uniform coverage even in cavities)
- Cured in oven at **160–180 °C for 20–30 minutes**

**Primer Surfacer Stage (1 Mark):**
- Applied by **robotic electrostatic spray**
- Thickness: **30–35 µm**
- Functions: Stone-chip resistance, smooth surface, UV barrier, colour uniformity

**Basecoat Stage (1 Mark):**
- Provides colour (solid, metallic, pearlescent)
- Thickness: **15–25 µm**
- Water-based in modern plants for VOC reduction

**Clearcoat Stage (1 Mark):**
- Transparent, glossy topcoat
- Thickness: **35–45 µm**
- Two-component polyurethane (2K PU) — scratch, UV, and chemical resistant
- Cured at 140 °C for 20 minutes

**Total Film Thickness:** ~100–120 µm

**Conclusion (1 Mark):**
The modern multi-layer paint system delivers **10+ years of corrosion protection**, OEM-quality finish, and meets environmental regulations (low VOC).

---

### Question B: (Choice 2 – Internal Choice Alternative) – 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Q. (a)** Explain the various **types of materials** used in vehicle body construction. Discuss the role of steel, aluminum, plastics, and composites with examples. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (1 Mark):**
Modern vehicles use a **Multi-Material Mix Design (MMMD)** strategy, where the right material is placed in the right location to optimize weight, cost, safety, and performance. The four major material families are **steel, aluminum, plastics, and composites**.

**Steel (2 Marks):**
- Accounts for **55–60% of BIW mass** in a typical car
- Grades used: Mild Steel, HSS, AHSS, UHSS
- Examples: Mild steel in floor pan, DP780 in B-pillar, 22MnB5 (UHSS) in bumper beam (hot-stamped)
- Advantages: Low cost, high strength, excellent formability, weldable, 100% recyclable
- Disadvantages: Higher density (7850 kg/m³), susceptible to corrosion without coating

**Aluminum (1.5 Marks):**
- 2× lighter than steel (density **2700 kg/m³**)
- Common alloys: **AA 5754** (hood outer, fender), **AA 6111** (panels), **AA 6061** (extrusions)
- Used in: Hood, doors, engine cradle, suspension arms (Audi A8, Ford F-150)
- Forming: Requires specific lubricants and die materials; **not directly weldable to steel** — joining methods include self-piercing rivets (SPR), adhesive bonding, and flow-drill screws
- Cost: 2–3× higher than steel; recycling infrastructure improving

**Plastics / Polymers (1.5 Marks):**
- Used in **30–35% of vehicle components by volume**
- Examples:
  - **PP (Polypropylene)**: Bumpers, interior trim (low cost, impact resistant)
  - **SMC (Sheet Moulding Compound)**: Body panels (fenders, hood), Class-A surface finish
  - **ABS**: Instrument panels, grilles
  - **PUR (Polyurethane) foam**: Seats, headliners
- Advantages: Light weight, design freedom, corrosion-proof, colour moulded-in
- Disadvantages: Difficult to recycle, lower strength, UV degradation

**Composites (1 Mark):**
- **CFRP (Carbon Fibre Reinforced Polymer)**: Used in BMW i3, i8 roof module, McLaren supercars
- **GFRP (Glass Fibre Reinforced Polymer)**: Used in Lotus Elise, panel leaf springs (Corvette)
- **Natural Fibre Composites**: Door panels, parcel shelves (eco-friendly)
- Highest strength-to-weight ratio, but high cost and long cure times

---

**Q. (b)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **phosphating** and **ED coating** stages of automotive painting. State the chemical reactions involved and the process parameters. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Phosphating Stage (3.5 Marks):**

Phosphating is a **conversion coating process** where the steel surface reacts with a zinc-phosphate solution to form a crystalline layer of zinc phosphate.

**Process Steps:**
1. Degreasing (alkaline spray)
2. Water rinse
3. Surface activation (titanium phosphate colloid)
4. Phosphating (immersion or spray)
5. Water rinse
6. Passivation (chrome-free rinse)

**Chemical Reaction (Simplified):**
$$3Zn(H_2PO_4)_2 + 4Fe \rightarrow Zn_3(PO_4)_2 \cdot 4H_2O + 2FeHPO_4 + H_2 \uparrow$$

**Process Parameters:**
| Parameter | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| Bath temperature | 35–55 °C |
| pH | 2.5–3.5 |
| Coating weight | 1.5–2.5 g/m² |
| Coating thickness | 1–3 µm |
| Crystal structure | Hopeite (Zn₃(PO₄)₂·4H₂O) |

**Functions:** Improves paint adhesion, corrosion resistance, and provides a base for ED coat.

**ED Coating Stage (3.5 Marks):**

The BIW is dipped into a paint tank filled with deionized water and paint emulsion. An electric current is passed; the paint particles migrate to the body (cathode) and deposit as a film.

**Process Steps:**
1. Pre-rinse
2. ED tank immersion
3. DC voltage application
4. UF (Ultrafiltration) rinse (1, 2, 3 stages)
5. Deionized water rinse
6. ED curing oven

**Reaction at Cathode (BIW):**
$$2H_2O + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2 \uparrow + 2OH^-$$

The OH⁻ ions neutralize the cationic resin, depositing it uniformly on the body surface.

**Process Parameters:**
| Parameter | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| Voltage | 200–350 V DC |
| Current density | 10–15 A/m² |
| Bath temperature | 28–32 °C |
| pH | 5.5–6.5 |
| Conductivity | 1000–2000 µS/cm |
| Coating thickness | 18–25 µm |
| Curing temperature | 160–180 °C |
| Curing time | 20–30 minutes |

**Functions:** Uniform corrosion protection, primer for topcoat system, complete cavity coverage.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing HSS / AHSS / UHSS categories** — Students often incorrectly state tensile strength ranges. Memorize: HSS = 340–780 MPa, AHSS = 780–1500 MPa, UHSS = > 1500 MPa. Mixing these up costs 2–3 marks.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to specify units** — "Tensile strength is 780" is **wrong**; correct answer is "780 MPa". Examiners deduct **½ to 1 mark** for missing units.
>
> 3. **Confusing "ED Coat" with "Electrostatic painting"** — ED Coat uses immersion and an electric current (electrochemical), while spray painting uses electrostatic attraction between charged droplets and grounded body. They are **different processes** with different coating thickness and uniformity.
>
> 4. **Omitting the phosphating stage** — Many students start their paint process description directly from ED Coat, skipping pre-treatment. Phosphating is a **mandatory step** in the KTU syllabus.
>
> 5. **Using vague terms like "paint is applied"** — Examiners expect specific terminology: "cathodic electrodeposition at 280 V DC, 30 °C, producing 20 µm dry film thickness". Specificity = marks.
>
> 6. **Not stating examples of applications** — When asked about steel grades, always mention an automotive component (e.g., "DP780 is used in the B-pillar") to demonstrate applied knowledge.
>
> 7. **Spelling errors**: "Phospating" (should be "Phosphating"), "Martenzite" (should be "Martensite"), "Galvanealing" (should be "Galvannealing"). KTU examiners do deduct **½ mark** for glaring spelling errors in technical terms.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exams:**

### Steel Sheet Fundamentals
- ☐ Steel accounts for **55–60% of BIW mass** in modern cars
- ☐ Steel sheet thickness for body panels: **0.5–3.0 mm** (most common: 0.6–0.8 mm)
- ☐ Steel density: **7850 kg/m³**; Aluminum density: **2700 kg/m³** (about 1/3 of steel)
- ☐ Mild Steel = **0.05–0.25% C**, UTS 270–410 MPa
- ☐ HSS = UTS **340–780 MPa**
- ☐ AHSS = UTS **780–1500 MPa** (DP, TRIP, CP, Martensitic)
- ☐ UHSS = UTS **> 1500 MPa** (e.g., 22MnB5, hot-stamped at 900 °C)
- ☐ DP steel has ferrite + martensite → high work-hardening rate
- ☐ TRIP steel uses retained austenite → high elongation + strength
- ☐ 22MnB5 = most common UHSS for hot stamping of bumper beams

### Painting Process
- ☐ Total paint film thickness: **~100–120 µm** (ED: 20 + Primer: 30 + Base: 20 + Clear: 40)
- ☐ Phosphating: **1–3 µm** zinc phosphate crystalline layer
- ☐ ED Coat: Cathodic, **200–350 V DC**, **18–25 µm**, cure at **160–180 °C**
- ☐ Primer: **30–35 µm**, stone-chip + UV protection
- ☐ Basecoat: **15–25 µm**, colour layer
- ☐ Clearcoat: **35–45 µm**, 2K polyurethane, scratch + UV resistance
- ☐ Paint shop energy consumption: **30–40%** of total plant energy

### Formability Metrics
- ☐ **r-value (Lankford coefficient)**: Drawability — higher r-value = better drawability
- ☐ **n-value (strain hardening exponent)**: Stretch formability — higher n-value = better stretch

### Corrosion Protection
- ☐ Galvanized steel: Zinc coating for sacrificial protection
- ☐ Galvannealed steel: Zinc-iron alloy coating (better weldability + paintability)
- ☐ Zinc density: **7140 kg/m³**
- ☐ OEM corrosion warranty: typically **10–12 years / unlimited km**

### Other Body Materials
- ☐ Aluminum alloys: AA 5754 (hood), AA 6111 (panels)
- ☐ Plastics: PP (bumpers), SMC (Class-A body panels), ABS (interior)
- ☐ Composites: CFRP (BMW i3), GFRP (Lotus), Natural fibres (eco)

### Joining Methods
- ☐ **Resistance Spot Welding (RSW)**: Most common BIW joining method
- ☐ **Self-Piercing Rivets (SPR)**: For aluminum and mixed-material joints
- ☐ **Laser Welding**: For UHSS, tailored blanks
- ☐ **Adhesive Bonding**: For dissimilar materials, NVH reduction
- ☐ **MIG/MAG Welding**: For thicker steel components (chassis)

### KTU Must-Remember Numbers
- ☐ Density of steel: **7850 kg/m³**
- ☐ Density of aluminum: **2700 kg/m³**
- ☐ Density of zinc: **7140 kg/m³**
- ☐ Modulus of elasticity of steel: **200 GPa**
- ☐ Hot stamping temperature: **900–950 °C**
- ☐ ED coat voltage: **200–350 V DC**
- ☐ Paint oven temperature: **140–180 °C**
- ☐ NCAP SEA threshold: **5000 J/kg**

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Strategy**: For 14-mark questions, always structure your answer with: (1) Definition, (2) Classification/Categories, (3) Properties with units, (4) Applications, (5) Diagram, (6) Conclusion. This format aligns with KTU's CO (Course Outcome) mapping and ensures full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Timber
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT413 - VEHICLE BODY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Timber

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Timber** refers to wood that has been processed and prepared for use in structural and non-structural applications, including vehicle body construction. In the context of **Vehicle Body Engineering**, timber denotes selected hardwoods and softwoods used historically and in niche modern applications as framing, paneling, decorative trim, and load-bearing skeletal members of automotive bodies.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PEMUT413 – Module 4):**
> *Timber is a lignocellulosic, hygroscopic, anisotropic biological composite material derived from the stem (trunk) and branches of trees, characterized by grain orientation along the longitudinal axis of growth. In automotive engineering, it is classified as a **traditional natural composite** possessing unique mechanical properties that made it the dominant body material until the 1930s.*

## 1.2 The Three Anatomical Planes of Timber

When a tree trunk is cut, three principal reference surfaces reveal the structural anatomy:

| Cut Type | Direction of Cut | Reveals | Visual Pattern |
|----------|------------------|---------|----------------|
| **Cross / Transverse Section** | Perpendicular to grain | Growth rings, pith, heartwood, sapwood | Concentric circles |
| **Radial Section** | Along the radius (through pith) | Straight parallel grain lines | Linear streaks |
| **Tangential Section** | Tangent to growth rings | Curved, flame-like pattern | Parabolic arches |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy: Timber as a Bundle of Microscopic Drinking Straws**
>
> Imagine a timber log as a tightly packed bundle of billions of microscopic drinking straws (these are the **wood cells / tracheids / fibres**), all running parallel along the length of the log. If you push along the straws (along the grain) — the log is **very strong** (like trying to crush a straw end-on). But if you push sideways (across the grain) — it crushes easily (like crushing a straw from the side). This directional dependence of strength is called **anisotropy**, and it is the single most important property governing how timber behaves in a vehicle body. A skilled body-builder, like a coachbuilder of the 1920s, knew this instinctively — he placed the wood so the grain always ran along the direction of expected load.

## 1.4 Classification of Timber for Vehicle Use

### A. Botanical Classification
- **Hardwoods (Angiosperms):** Dicotyledonous trees with broad leaves. Example used in coachbuilding — **Teak, Oak, Mahogany, Ash, Walnut, Maple**.
- **Softwoods (Gymnosperms):** Coniferous trees with needle leaves. Example — **Pine, Spruce, Cedar, Fir**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Misconception:** The terms *hardwood* and *softwood* do **NOT** strictly indicate hardness. *Balsa* is technically a hardwood but is one of the softest woods; *Yew* is a softwood but is harder than many hardwoods.

### B. Engineering Classification for Vehicle Body
| Class | Description | Vehicle Body Use |
|-------|-------------|--------------------|
| **First Class Timber** | Defect-free, close grain, durable | Structural frames, chassis members |
| **Second Class Timber** | Minor defects, slightly lower strength | Internal panels, non-load trim |
| **Third Class Timber** | Visible defects, low durability | Temporary formwork, packing crates |

## 1.5 Why Timber Was — and Still Is — Used in Vehicle Bodies

> [!NOTE]
> **Five Engineering Advantages of Timber:**
> 1. **High strength-to-weight ratio** — comparable to mild steel per unit weight.
> 2. **Excellent vibration damping** — superior to metals, leading to quieter bodies.
> 3. **Easy machinability** — can be shaped, bent (steam-bent), carved with hand tools.
> 4. **Crash energy absorption** — cellular structure crushes progressively.
> 5. **Aesthetic appeal** — natural grain finishes for luxury interiors (Rolls-Royce, Bentley tradition).

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Macro-Structure of a Timber Log

A cross-section of a mature log reveals these concentric zones, each with engineering significance:

```
Outermost  →  Bark (protection)
              ↓
              Cambium (growth layer)
              ↓
              Sapwood (living, transports sap, lighter)
              ↓
              Heartwood (dead, dense, darker, structural)
              ↓
              Pith (central core, weak, avoid in body work)
              ↓
              Medullary rays (radial transport)
```

- **Heartwood vs Sapwood:** For vehicle frames, **heartwood** is preferred due to its higher density, natural durability, and resistance to decay.
- **Annual Rings:** Each ring = one year of growth. **Springwood (early wood)** is less dense; **Summerwood (late wood)** is denser. The proportion of summerwood determines strength.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quick Identification Rule for Body Builders:**
> *A timber sample with >50% summerwood (denser, darker bands) is suitable for load-bearing structural members. Wood with <30% summerwood should be used only for decorative trim.*

## 2.2 Mechanical Properties of Timber

Timber exhibits **anisotropic** behavior — properties differ along three principal axes:
- **Longitudinal (L):** Along the grain (parallel to fibres).
- **Radial (R):** From pith outward.
- **Tangential (T):** Perpendicular to growth rings.

### 2.2.1 Strength Comparison Across Directions

$$
\sigma_{L} \;>\; \sigma_{R} \;>\; \sigma_{T}
$$

Typical values for a structural hardwood (e.g., Teak):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Tensile Strength (L)} &\approx 80 - 120 \text{ MPa} \\
\text{Tensile Strength (T)} &\approx 4 - 6 \text{ MPa} \\
\text{Compressive Strength (L)} &\approx 50 - 70 \text{ MPa} \\
\text{Compressive Strength (T)} &\approx 6 - 10 \text{ MPa} \\
\text{Modulus of Elasticity (L)} &\approx 10 - 15 \text{ GPa} \\
\text{Shear Strength (L-T)} &\approx 8 - 12 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 2.3 Moisture Content — The Most Critical Property

**Moisture Content (MC)** is defined as the mass of water divided by the mass of oven-dry wood:

$$
MC \;=\; \frac{M_{green} - M_{oven\,dry}}{M_{oven\,dry}} \times 100 \;\;[\%]
$$

- **Green timber:** MC = 30% to 200% (depending on species).
- **Fibre Saturation Point (FSP):** MC ≈ **25% to 30%** — below this, wood begins to shrink and most physical properties change.
- **Equilibrium Moisture Content (EMC):** The MC at which wood neither gains nor loses moisture to the surrounding air. For vehicle cabins, target EMC ≈ **12% at 20°C, 65% RH**.

> [!WARNING]
> **If timber is used in a vehicle body without proper seasoning to ~12% MC, it will:**
> - Shrink and warp in dry climates (causing panels to crack).
> - Swell in humid climates (causing joints to fail).
> - Develop checks and splits along the grain.
> - Foster fungal decay at MC > 20%.

## 2.4 Seasoning of Timber

Seasoning is the controlled reduction of moisture content. Two principal methods:

| Method | Process | Time | Quality of Result |
|--------|---------|------|-------------------|
| **Air Seasoning** | Stacked under covered shed, natural air drying | 2–24 months | Good, slight surface defects |
| **Kiln Seasoning** | Controlled chamber with heat & humidity | 4–10 days | Excellent, uniform MC |

For vehicle body timber, **kiln seasoning** to **MC = 10–12%** is the industry standard.

## 2.5 Preservation of Timber

Timber used in vehicles may be exposed to moisture (floor pans, door sills). Preservation methods:

1. **Surface application:** Brushing, spraying with creosote, paint, varnish.
2. **Soaking:** Immersion in preservative (e.g., boric acid solution).
3. **Pressure treatment:** Forcing preservative deep into cells using vacuum-pressure cycles.
4. **Charring:** Surface carbonization — traditional method for termite resistance.

Common preservatives: **Creosote oil, CCA (Copper-Chrome-Arsenic), Borates, Pentachlorophenol**.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Memorize the following table — these are the high-yield formulae for Module 4 Timber.**

| # | Formula / Property | Expression | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Moisture Content** | $MC = \dfrac{M_{green}-M_{dry}}{M_{dry}} \times 100$ | % | Above FSP ≈ 25–30% |
| 2 | **Specific Gravity** | $SG = \dfrac{\rho_{wood}}{\rho_{water}}$ | — | Use oven-dry mass/volume at MC=12% |
| 3 | **Density** | $\rho = \dfrac{M}{V}$ | kg/m³ | Hardwoods: 600–900; Softwoods: 350–550 |
| 4 | **Shrinkage (volumetric)** | $S_v = \dfrac{V_{green}-V_{dry}}{V_{green}} \times 100$ | % | Typical: 9–15% |
| 5 | **Shrinkage (tangential)** | $S_T = \dfrac{T_{green}-T_{dry}}{T_{green}} \times 100$ | % | Typical: 4–8% |
| 6 | **Shrinkage (radial)** | $S_R = \dfrac{R_{green}-R_{dry}}{R_{green}} \times 100$ | % | Typical: 2–5% |
| 7 | **Strength parallel to grain (compression)** | $\sigma_{c,L} = \dfrac{P}{A}$ | MPa | Used for structural frame |
| 8 | **Modulus of Elasticity** | $E = \dfrac{\sigma}{\varepsilon}$ | GPa | Anisotropic: $E_L \gg E_T$ |
| 9 | **Hardness (Janka scale)** | Force to embed 11.28 mm steel ball to half depth | kN | Used to compare species |
| 10 | **Buckling load (long column)** | $P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 E I}{L_e^{\,2}}$ | N | Euler formula for slender timber posts |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> - The **Janka hardness** determines whether a wood species is suitable for flooring or load-bearing floorboards.
> - **Anisotropic shrinkage ratios** ($S_T \approx 2 S_R$) explain why wood cups *towards the pith* when drying — a fundamental behaviour exploited by coachbuilders to pre-shape curved panels.
> - **Euler buckling** is directly applied when sizing slender wooden roof bows in classic car body frames.

## 2.7 Common Defects in Timber Used in Bodies

| Defect | Description | Effect on Body |
|--------|-------------|----------------|
| **Knots** | Embedded branch bases | Stress concentrators — reduce strength up to 60% |
| **Shakes** | Separation along grain (cup shake, heart shake, star shake) | Reduce load-bearing capacity |
| **Checks & Splits** | Longitudinal cracks from uneven drying | Allow moisture ingress — corrosion of adjacent metal |
| **Warp** | Bow, crook, twist, cup | Misalignment of body panels |
| **Decay / Rot** | Fungal attack at high MC | Total structural failure |
| **Insect Damage** | Termite / borer tunnels | Loss of cross-section |

## 2.8 Selection Criteria for Vehicle Body Timber

A competent body engineer selects timber based on:

1. **Strength-to-weight ratio** (high).
2. **Dimensional stability** (low shrinkage).
3. **Workability** (easy steam bending for compound curves).
4. **Resistance to decay & insects**.
5. **Availability & cost**.
6. **Aesthetic grain pattern** (for visible interior trim).

> [!NOTE]
> **Historical Coachbuilder's Favourite Species:**
> - *Ash* — for chassis frames (high shock resistance).
> - *Hickory* — for wheel spokes (toughness).
> - *Mahogany / Walnut* — for body panels (workability, beauty).
> - *Teak* — for marine/convertible body parts (water resistance).

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Examples

## 3.1 Derivations — From First Principles

### 3.1.1 Derivation of the Moisture Content Formula

By definition, moisture content is the ratio of the mass of water present in the wood to the mass of completely dry wood, expressed as a percentage.

**Step 1:** Let $M_{green}$ be the mass of the freshly cut (green) timber sample.

**Step 2:** Place the sample in a laboratory oven at $103 \pm 2 \, °C$ until constant mass is achieved (typically 24–48 hours). Record this as $M_{dry}$.

**Step 3:** The mass of water removed equals the difference:

$$
M_{water} \;=\; M_{green} - M_{dry}
$$

**Step 4:** The moisture content is then:

$$
MC \;=\; \frac{M_{water}}{M_{dry}} \times 100 \;=\; \frac{M_{green} - M_{dry}}{M_{dry}} \times 100
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why use oven-dry mass as the denominator (not green mass)?**
> Using $M_{dry}$ in the denominator keeps MC continuous and physically meaningful even at very high moisture contents (>100%). It is the universal KTU / IS / ASTM convention.

### 3.1.2 Derivation of Dimensional Shrinkage

When timber dries below the fibre saturation point (FSP ≈ 25–30%), cell walls lose bound water and the wood shrinks.

**Step 1:** Let $L_{green}$ be the original (green) dimension in a chosen direction.

**Step 2:** After drying to MC = 12% (equilibrium), the new dimension is $L_{dry}$.

**Step 3:** The shrinkage ratio in that direction is:

$$
S \;=\; \frac{L_{green} - L_{dry}}{L_{green}} \times 100 \quad [\%]
$$

**Step 4:** For total volumetric shrinkage:

$$
S_v \;=\; \frac{V_{green} - V_{dry}}{V_{green}} \times 100 \quad [\%]
$$

**Step 5:** Empirically, for most species:

$$
S_v \;\approx\; S_L + S_R + S_T
$$

where $S_L \approx 0.1 - 0.3\%$, $S_R \approx 2 - 5\%$, and $S_T \approx 4 - 8\%$.

The tangential-to-radial shrinkage ratio is approximately:

$$
\frac{S_T}{S_R} \;\approx\; 2 \quad \text{(for most hardwoods)}
$$

This ratio explains the characteristic *cupping* of flat-sawn boards during drying.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Numerical Problems

### 3.2.1 Problem 1 — Moisture Content Calculation

**Problem Statement:**
A wooden chassis frame sample has a green mass of 480 g. After oven drying at 103 °C, its mass stabilizes at 360 g. Calculate (i) the moisture content, (ii) the mass of water removed, and (iii) comment on its suitability for vehicle body use without further seasoning.

**Given:**
- $M_{green} = 480 \text{ g}$
- $M_{dry} = 360 \text{ g}$

**Solution:**

**Part (i) — Moisture Content:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
MC &= \frac{M_{green} - M_{dry}}{M_{dry}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{480 - 360}{360} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{120}{360} \times 100 \\
&= 33.33 \%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (ii) — Mass of water removed:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{water} &= M_{green} - M_{dry} \\
&= 480 - 360 \\
&= 120 \text{ g}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (iii) — Engineering comment:**

Since $MC = 33.33\% > $ FSP (≈ 25–30%) **and** $> $ target EMC (12%), this timber is unsuitable for vehicle body application. It must be **kiln-seasoned** to reduce MC to 10–12% before use. Using it green will cause:
- Shrinkage of 3–7% in tangential direction.
- Joint loosening, panel warping, and possible decay.

**Answer:** $MC = 33.33\%$, $M_{water} = 120$ g; the timber needs kiln-seasoning.

---

### 3.2.2 Problem 2 — Buckling of a Wooden Roof Bow

**Problem Statement:**
A traditional wooden roof bow (slender curved beam) used in a classic car body has the following dimensions and properties:

- Length (effective): $L_e = 1.2$ m
- Cross-section: $50 \text{ mm} \times 25 \text{ mm}$ (rectangular)
- Modulus of Elasticity (along grain): $E = 12$ GPa
- Both ends are pin-connected.

Determine the Euler critical buckling load.

**Given:**
- $L_e = 1.2$ m
- $b = 50$ mm $= 0.05$ m
- $h = 25$ mm $= 0.025$ m
- $E = 12 \text{ GPa} = 12 \times 10^9 \text{ Pa}$
- End condition: pin-pin (effective length factor $K = 1$)

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Calculate the least moment of inertia. Since buckling occurs about the weakest axis, take the minimum $I_{min}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{min} &= \frac{b \cdot h^3}{12} \quad \text{(about axis parallel to longer side)}\\
&= \frac{0.05 \times (0.025)^3}{12} \\
&= \frac{0.05 \times 1.5625 \times 10^{-5}}{12} \\
&= \frac{7.8125 \times 10^{-7}}{12} \\
&= 6.51 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m}^4
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the Euler buckling formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{cr} &= \frac{\pi^2 E I_{min}}{L_e^{\,2}} \\
&= \frac{\pi^2 \times 12 \times 10^9 \times 6.51 \times 10^{-8}}{(1.2)^2} \\
&= \frac{9.8696 \times 12 \times 6.51 \times 10}{1.44} \\
&= \frac{9.8696 \times 78.12}{1.44} \\
&= \frac{770.91}{1.44} \\
&\approx 535.4 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Convert to kgf for engineering intuition:

$$
P_{cr} \approx \frac{535.4}{9.81} \approx 54.6 \text{ kgf}
$$

**Engineering Comment:** A roof bow must resist compressive loads from the roof fabric, passengers, and dynamic loads. With a critical buckling load of only ~535 N, this slender bow requires additional bracing — a real reason why wooden body frames were always triangulated with diagonal members.

**Answer:** $P_{cr} \approx 535.4$ N.

---

### 3.2.3 Problem 3 — Volumetric Shrinkage Estimation

**Problem Statement:**
A block of freshly cut oak (green) measures $100 \times 100 \times 300$ mm. After kiln drying to MC = 12%, the tangential shrinkage is 6%, radial shrinkage is 3%, and longitudinal shrinkage is 0.2%. Calculate (i) the final dimensions, and (ii) the final volume.

**Given:**
- Green dimensions: $L_0 = 300$ mm (along grain), $T_0 = 100$ mm, $R_0 = 100$ mm.
- $S_T = 6\%$, $S_R = 3\%$, $S_L = 0.2\%$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Final tangential dimension:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= T_0 \left(1 - \frac{S_T}{100}\right) \\
&= 100 \times (1 - 0.06) \\
&= 94 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Final radial dimension:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= R_0 \left(1 - \frac{S_R}{100}\right) \\
&= 100 \times (1 - 0.03) \\
&= 97 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Final longitudinal dimension:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= L_0 \left(1 - \frac{S_L}{100}\right) \\
&= 300 \times (1 - 0.002) \\
&= 299.4 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Final volume:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{dry} &= T \times R \times L \\
&= 94 \times 97 \times 299.4 \\
&= 94 \times 29041.8 \\
&= 2,729,929 \text{ mm}^3 \\
&\approx 2.73 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Volumetric shrinkage:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_v &= \frac{V_{green} - V_{dry}}{V_{green}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{3{,}000{,}000 - 2{,}729{,}929}{3{,}000{,}000} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{270{,}071}{3{,}000{,}000} \times 100 \\
&\approx 9.0 \%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** Final dimensions $94 \times 97 \times 299.4$ mm; final volume ≈ $2.73 \times 10^6$ mm³; volumetric shrinkage ≈ 9.0%.
