# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — Short Answer (Definition) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define timber. List any four desirable properties of timber for use in vehicle body construction.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Timber is wood that has been suitably seasoned and treated, derived from trees, and used as a structural or non-structural engineering material. In vehicle body engineering, it refers to selected hardwood species (e.g., teak, ash, mahogany) shaped into structural frames, panels, and decorative members.

**Four desirable properties (2 marks — ½ mark each):**

1. **High strength-to-weight ratio** — comparable to mild steel per unit weight.
2. **Excellent vibration damping** — provides quieter, smoother rides.
3. **Easy workability** — can be steam-bent into compound curves and shaped with simple tools.
4. **Good crash energy absorption** — cellular structure collapses progressively.
5. *(Optional)* Aesthetic appeal — natural grain finishes for luxury interiors.

---

### Question 2 — Short Answer (Comparison) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: Differentiate between hardwood and softwood. Give two examples of each suitable for vehicle body use.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Hardwood | Softwood |
|-----------|----------|----------|
| **Botanical origin** | Angiosperms (broad-leaved, dicot) | Gymnosperms (coniferous, needle-leaved) |
| **Structure** | More complex, vessels + fibres | Simple, mainly tracheids |
| **Density (general)** | Higher (600–900 kg/m³) | Lower (350–550 kg/m³) |
| **Strength** | Generally higher | Generally lower |
| **Cost** | Higher | Lower |

**Examples (1 mark):**
- **Hardwoods:** Teak, Ash, Mahogany, Oak, Walnut, Maple.
- **Softwoods:** Pine, Spruce, Cedar, Fir.

> [!NOTE]
> The terms *hardwood* and *softwood* are botanical, not strictly indicative of actual hardness.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the macro-structure of timber with a neat diagram. Discuss the significance of **heartwood** and **sapwood** in vehicle body applications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define **moisture content**. A green timber sample has mass 540 g and an oven-dry mass of 380 g. Calculate the moisture content. Comment on the suitability of this timber for use in a vehicle body. The fibre saturation point (FSP) of the species is 28%. **(7 marks)**

### Model Answer — Question A

#### Part (a) — Macro-Structure of Timber (7 marks)

A cross-section of a timber log shows the following concentric zones from outside inward:

1. **Bark** — protective outer layer (no structural use).
2. **Cambium** — thin growth layer that produces new wood and bark cells.
3. **Sapwood** — outer living wood that transports sap from roots to leaves. It is lighter in colour and lower in density.
4. **Heartwood** — inner dead wood, denser, darker, structurally stronger, and more decay-resistant.
5. **Pith** — central core of soft tissue, weakest region (always avoided in body work).
6. **Medullary rays** — radial cells transporting nutrients laterally.

**Significance for vehicle body (4 marks):**

- **Heartwood is preferred** for structural members (chassis frames, door posts, roof bows) because of its:
  - Higher density → higher strength.
  - Natural deposition of extractives (tannins, oils) → greater natural durability.
  - Better resistance to fungal decay and insect attack.
- **Sapwood** is more permeable, decays faster, and shrinks more on drying. It is used only for:
  - Internal decorative trim where load-bearing is not required.
  - Plywood cores where it is laminated and sealed.

**Diagram (3 marks):** Refer to Section 4.1 — concentric ring diagram from Bark → Pith.

#### Part (b) — Moisture Content Numerical (7 marks)

**Definition (2 marks):**
Moisture Content (MC) is the mass of water present in wood expressed as a percentage of the oven-dry mass of wood:

$$
MC = \frac{M_{green} - M_{dry}}{M_{dry}} \times 100 \quad [\%]
$$

**Numerical solution (3 marks):**

Given $M_{green} = 540$ g, $M_{dry} = 380$ g.

$$
\begin{aligned}
MC &= \frac{540 - 380}{380} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{160}{380} \times 100 \\
&= 42.11 \%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Suitability comment (2 marks):**
Since $MC = 42.11\%$ is **much higher** than the FSP (28%) and far above the target EMC for vehicle bodies (12%), this timber is **completely unsuitable** for direct use. It must first be **kiln-seasoned** to MC ≈ 10–12%, after which it will be dimensionally stable and ready for vehicle body framing.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** What is **seasoning of timber**? Compare air seasoning and kiln seasoning. Why is kiln seasoning preferred for vehicle body timber? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A wooden roof bow of a classic car body has effective length 1.5 m and rectangular cross-section 60 mm × 30 mm. The modulus of elasticity along the grain is 10 GPa. The bow is pin-connected at both ends. Calculate the **Euler critical buckling load**. Take $\pi^2 \approx 9.87$. **(7 marks)**

### Model Answer — Question B

#### Part (a) — Seasoning of Timber (7 marks)

**Definition (2 marks):**
Seasoning is the controlled process of reducing the moisture content of timber to a level suitable for its intended use, in order to improve dimensional stability, strength, and durability.

**Comparison table (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Air Seasoning | Kiln Seasoning |
|-----------|---------------|----------------|
| Medium | Natural air in covered shed | Controlled hot-air chamber |
| Duration | 2–24 months | 4–10 days |
| Final MC | 15–20% | 10–12% |
| Uniformity | Variable (depends on weather) | Highly uniform |
| Defects | Some surface cracks, stains | Minimal defects |
| Cost | Low | Moderate to high |
| Skill required | Low | Requires operator |

**Why kiln seasoning is preferred for vehicle body timber (2 marks):**
1. Achieves **uniform low MC** (10–12%) essential for dimensional accuracy of body panels.
2. **Kills fungi and insects** during the high-temperature cycle, increasing durability.
3. Faster turnaround supports mass production schedules.
4. Produces timber with **minimum warping** — critical for tight body joints.

#### Part (b) — Euler Buckling Numerical (7 marks)

**Given:**
- Effective length $L_e = 1.5$ m
- Cross-section: $b = 60$ mm $= 0.06$ m, $h = 30$ mm $= 0.03$ m
- $E = 10$ GPa $= 10 \times 10^9$ Pa
- End conditions: pin–pin → $K = 1$
- $\pi^2 \approx 9.87$

**Step 1 — Minimum moment of inertia (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{min} &= \frac{b h^3}{12} \\
&= \frac{0.06 \times (0.03)^3}{12} \\
&= \frac{0.06 \times 2.7 \times 10^{-5}}{12} \\
&= \frac{1.62 \times 10^{-6}}{12} \\
&= 1.35 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^4
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Euler critical load (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{cr} &= \frac{\pi^2 E I_{min}}{L_e^{\,2}} \\
&= \frac{9.87 \times 10 \times 10^9 \times 1.35 \times 10^{-7}}{(1.5)^2} \\
&= \frac{9.87 \times 1.35 \times 10^3}{2.25} \\
&= \frac{13{,}324.5}{2.25} \\
&\approx 5922 \text{ N} \\
&\approx 5.92 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Engineering conclusion (2 marks):**
A roof bow with this cross-section can carry about 5.9 kN before buckling. For a passenger car roof supporting headliner, fabric, and a partial occupant load in a rollover, this is borderline. Designers of classic bodies therefore used **multiple bows spaced closely (300–400 mm apart)** to keep individual load on each bow safely below $P_{cr}/n$ with a safety factor $n \geq 3$.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks — Timber Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing hardwood/softwood with hardness.** They are *botanical* terms, not hardness measures. Examiners deduct 1 mark here routinely.
> 2. **Forgetting to use oven-dry mass** in the denominator of the MC formula. Using $M_{green}$ instead of $M_{dry}$ will give a wrong answer and cost full marks in numericals.
> 3. **Mixing up tangential, radial, and longitudinal directions** in shrinkage. Always state which direction the shrinkage refers to. $S_T \approx 2 S_R$ is a memorised fact examiners love to test.
> 4. **Skipping units and significant figures** in numericals — KTU examiners deduct ½ mark for sloppy unit handling.
> 5. **Ignoring the FSP** when commenting on suitability — without comparing MC to FSP (≈ 25–30%) the comment is incomplete and loses 1 mark.
> 6. **In Euler buckling**, students often forget to take the *minimum* moment of inertia, leading to a buckling load about the wrong axis.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Timber (Module 4)**

- **Timber** = seasoned, treated wood used in vehicle bodies; historical & niche modern use.
- **Anatomy of a log:** Bark → Cambium → Sapwood → Heartwood → Pith (with medullary rays).
- **Heartwood > Sapwood** for structural use (denser, more durable).
- **Hardwood** = Angiosperm (broadleaf), e.g., Teak, Ash, Mahogany.
- **Softwood** = Gymnosperm (conifer), e.g., Pine, Spruce, Cedar.
- **Anisotropy:** $\sigma_L \gg \sigma_R > \sigma_T$ — load must be aligned with grain.
- **Moisture Content:** $MC = \dfrac{M_{green}-M_{dry}}{M_{dry}} \times 100\%$
- **Fibre Saturation Point (FSP):** ≈ 25–30% MC — shrinkage begins below this.
- **Target MC for vehicle bodies:** 10–12% (kiln seasoned).
- **Seasoning:** Air (slow, 2–24 months) vs Kiln (fast, 4–10 days, uniform).
- **Preservation:** Surface coating, soaking, pressure impregnation (CCA, creosote, borates).
- **Shrinkage:** Tangential ≈ 4–8%, Radial ≈ 2–5%, Longitudinal ≈ 0.1–0.3%.
- **Defects:** Knots, shakes, warps, checks, splits, decay, insect damage.
- **Euler Buckling:** $P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 E I_{min}}{L_e^{\,2}}$ — pin-pin: $L_e = L$.
- **Mechanical properties typical (hardwood):** $E_L \approx 10$–15 GPa, $\sigma_{c,L} \approx 50$–70 MPa, $\sigma_{t,L} \approx 80$–120 MPa.
- **Selection rule:** > 50% summerwood ratio for structural members.
- **Modern relevance:** Wooden dashboards, cabin trim, classic-car restorations, luxury interiors (Rolls-Royce, Bentley).
- **Environmental advantage:** Renewable, biodegradable, lower embodied energy than steel or aluminium.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Plastics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT413 - VEHICLE BODY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Plastics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Plastics

A **plastic** is a synthetic or semi-synthetic polymeric material that can be molded or shaped, usually under heat and pressure, into solid objects of any desired form. The word "plastic" is derived from the Greek word *plastikos*, meaning "capable of being shaped or molded." In the context of **vehicle body engineering (PEMUT413)**, plastics and reinforced plastics form the second-largest group of materials (after ferrous metals) used in modern automotive body construction, primarily because of their high **strength-to-weight ratio**, **corrosion resistance**, and **design flexibility**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PEMUT413 – Module 4):**
> Plastics are organic polymeric materials composed of long-chain molecules (monomers linked into polymers) that can be shaped under heat and pressure. They may contain additives such as fillers, plasticizers, stabilizers, colorants, and reinforcements (fibers) to enhance specific mechanical, thermal, or chemical properties.

### 1.2 Polymer – The Building Block

A **polymer** is a large molecule (macromolecule) composed of many repeating subunits called **monomers**, joined by covalent chemical bonds. The molecular structure and the type of bonding (linear, branched, or cross-linked) decide the behavior of the plastic when heated.

The two main polymerization routes are:

1. **Addition (Chain-Growth) Polymerization** – Monomers add together without the loss of any atom. Example: Polyethylene (PE), Polypropylene (PP), Polyvinyl Chloride (PVC), Polystyrene (PS).
2. **Condensation (Step-Growth) Polymerization** – Monomers join with the elimination of a small by-product molecule (commonly water, HCl, or methanol). Example: Phenol-Formaldehyde, Polyesters, Polyamides (Nylon).

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy – Plastics as "Clay of the Modern World"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy:**
> Imagine a **steel chain** (rigid, fixed shape, very strong in tension) versus a **ball-and-socket toy construction set** (each ball can rotate and link to several others in any direction). A polymer is like a long chain of such balls. When the chains are loosely tangled (like a bowl of cooked spaghetti) and slide over one another, the material is soft and flexible — this is **thermoplastic**. When the chains are tied together at random points (like a net), the material becomes rigid and cannot be re-softened — this is **thermosetting plastic**.

In a vehicle body, plastics act like a **lightweight skin** that can be molded into highly aerodynamic, complex shapes that would be impossible or extremely expensive to achieve with sheet steel. Think of a modern car bumper: it is a single molded plastic piece that integrates energy-absorbing foam, mounting brackets, sensor housings, and a paintable outer skin — something that would otherwise need 8–10 stamped steel parts welded together.

### 1.4 Standard Material Metrics Used in Plastics

| Metric | Standard Symbol | Typical Range (Automotive Plastics) | Engineering Importance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Density** | $\rho$ | 0.9 – 2.0 $\text{g/cm}^3$ | Lightweighting (vs. 7.85 $\text{g/cm}^3$ for steel) |
| **Tensile Strength** | $\sigma_t$ | 20 – 350 $\text{MPa}$ | Crash-load resistance |
| **Young's Modulus** | $E$ | 1 – 30 $\text{GPa}$ | Stiffness & dent resistance |
| **Glass Transition Temp.** | $T_g$ | 80 – 200 $^\circ\text{C}$ | Upper service temperature |
| **Specific Strength** | $\sigma_t / \rho$ | 15 – 250 $\text{kNm/kg}$ | Strength per unit mass |
| **Elongation at Break** | $\varepsilon_b$ | 1 % – 600 % | Ductility & energy absorption |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why plastics for vehicle bodies?**
> 1. **Mass Reduction** – 1 kg of plastic replaces ~2–4 kg of steel → directly cuts fuel consumption and CO₂ emissions.
> 2. **Corrosion Resistance** – Plastics do not rust; they extend vehicle service life.
> 3. **Design Freedom** – One molded part replaces an assembly of many stamped-and-welded steel pieces.
> 4. **Safety (Energy Absorption)** – Modern foamed plastics can absorb 4–5 times more crash energy per kg than steel.
> 5. **Recyclability** – Most thermoplastics (PP, ABS, PA) can be re-melted and reprocessed.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Plastics (The Master Logic Tree)

Plastics are broadly divided into **three** behavioral families. This classification is the *single most important* question in the KTU board exam for this topic.

### Family 1: Thermoplastics (Re-softenable)
- Long, linear or branched polymer chains with **weak secondary (van der Waals) bonds** between them.
- On heating, the secondary bonds weaken and chains slide → material softens → can be remolded.
- On cooling, it hardens again. The process is **reversible**.
- Generally **recyclable**.
- **Examples used in cars:** PP (bumpers, dashboards), ABS (interior trim, instrument panels), PA/Nylon (under-hood gears, radiator tanks), PVC (cable insulation, roof linings), PE (fuel tanks), PC (headlamp lenses), PMMA (tail-lamp lenses), PET (seat fabrics, wiper arms).

### Family 2: Thermosetting Plastics (Permanently Hardened)
- Polymer chains are **cross-linked** in three dimensions by strong covalent bonds, forming a rigid network.
- Once cured, heating does NOT soften them — instead, they decompose (char or burn).
- The curing reaction is **irreversible**.
- Generally **not recyclable** by re-melting.
- Higher heat resistance and creep resistance than thermoplastics.
- **Examples used in cars:** Phenol-formaldehyde (brake pads, clutch facings), Melamine-formaldehyde (dashboard laminates), Epoxy (adhesives, composites), Unsaturated Polyester (fiberglass body panels), Polyurethane (PU foam in seats & bumpers), SMC/BMC body panels.

### Family 3: Elastomers (Rubbers)
- Polymer chains are lightly cross-linked (sparse network) so that the material can stretch to many times its original length and return to shape.
- Elastic behavior governed by the **entropic elasticity** of coiled chains.
- **Examples:** Natural Rubber (tyres, weatherstrip), EPDM (window seals), SBR (tyres), Silicone Rubber (gaskets, hoses), Nitrile Rubber (fuel hoses).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory Trick for Board Exams:**
> **"T-T-T"** → **T**hermoplastics are **T**emporary networks (chains slide when hot), **T**hermosets are **T**ied-up (permanent cross-links), **T**hermoplastic **E**lastomers are the modern hybrid that behaves like rubber but processes like plastic.

## 2.2 Reinforced Plastics (Composites for Body Panels)

When a **fibrous reinforcement** is embedded in a **plastic matrix**, the resulting composite gains dramatically improved strength and stiffness. This is the foundation of modern FRP (Fibre-Reinforced Plastic) body panels used in cars, buses, trucks, and racing vehicles.

| Reinforcement | Common Code | Tensile Strength | Young's Modulus | Vehicle Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Glass Fibre** | GRP / GFRP | 2,000 – 4,500 $\text{MPa}$ | 70 – 85 $\text{GPa}$ | Bus bodies, truck cabs, hoods |
| **Carbon Fibre** | CRP / CFRP | 3,000 – 7,000 $\text{MPa}$ | 230 – 600 $\text{GPa}$ | Racing bodies, EV battery enclosures |
| **Aramid (Kevlar)** | ARP / AFRP | 2,500 – 3,600 $\text{MPa}$ | 70 – 130 $\text{GPa}$ | Bullet-proof panels, fenders |
| **Natural Fibre (Flax, Hemp)** | NFRP | 250 – 800 $\text{MPa}$ | 10 – 80 $\text{GPa}$ | Door trims, parcel shelves |

### 2.3 Specific Strength — The Key Composite Performance Equation

The reason composites dominate body engineering is best captured by the **specific strength** ratio. For a fibre-reinforced lamina (the basic building block of a laminate) loaded along the fibre direction, by the **Rule of Mixtures**:

$$\sigma_{c} = \sigma_{f} \cdot V_{f} + \sigma_{m} \cdot (1 - V_{f})$$

where $\sigma_c$ is the composite strength, $\sigma_f$ is the fibre strength, $\sigma_m$ is the matrix strength, and $V_f$ is the **fibre volume fraction** (typically 0.3 to 0.6 in real automotive composites).

Similarly, by the same rule:

$$E_{c} = E_{f} \cdot V_{f} + E_{m} \cdot (1 - V_{f})$$

The corresponding **specific strength** is then:

$$\text{SS} = \frac{\sigma_{c}}{\rho_{c}}$$

with

$$\rho_{c} = \rho_{f} \cdot V_{f} + \rho_{m} \cdot (1 - V_{f})$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** Specific strength is the master metric for any weight-critical structure. Carbon-fibre composites with $V_f \approx 0.6$ achieve a specific strength of ~250 $\text{kNm/kg}$, which is roughly **5–7 times that of mild steel** and **3–4 times that of aluminium alloy** — the reason supercars, F1 chassis, and now the latest EV battery enclosures adopt CFRP.

## 2.4 Glass Transition Temperature – The "Softening Line"

Every amorphous plastic has a **glass transition temperature** $T_g$. Below $T_g$, the polymer chains are frozen in place and the material behaves as a rigid glass. Above $T_g$, chains gain segmental mobility and the material becomes rubbery.

$$T_{g,\text{mix}} = w_{1} \cdot T_{g,1} + w_{2} \cdot T_{g,2} + \cdots + w_{n} \cdot T_{g,n}$$

where $w_i$ is the mass fraction of the $i$-th polymer/component and $T_{g,i}$ is its glass transition temperature. This **Fox equation** is used to estimate the $T_g$ of a polymer blend or a plasticized system.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Symbols | Typical Application in PEMUT413 |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sigma_{c} = \sigma_{f} V_{f} + \sigma_{m} (1 - V_{f})$ | Rule of mixtures for strength | Predicting FRP panel strength |
| 2 | $E_{c} = E_{f} V_{f} + E_{m} (1 - V_{f})$ | Rule of mixtures for modulus | Predicting panel stiffness |
| 3 | $\rho_{c} = \rho_{f} V_{f} + \rho_{m} (1 - V_{f})$ | Rule of mixtures for density | Mass estimation of a body panel |
| 4 | $\text{SS} = \sigma_{c} / \rho_{c}$ | Specific strength | Material selection charts (Ashby plot) |
| 5 | $\text{SA} = E / \rho^{3}$ | Specific stiffness (Ashby) | Lightweight panel design |
| 6 | $\dfrac{1}{T_{g,\text{mix}}} = \sum \dfrac{w_{i}}{T_{g,i}}$ | Fox equation for $T_g$ | Blends & copolymer $T_g$ estimation |
| 7 | $\Delta L = \alpha \cdot L_0 \cdot \Delta T$ | Thermal expansion | Mould design, gap tolerances |
| 8 | $\eta = \dfrac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}}$ | Dynamic viscosity | Injection-moulding flow simulation |
| 9 | $t_{\text{cycle}} = t_{\text{fill}} + t_{\text{cool}}$ | Moulding cycle time | Production planning |
| 10 | $V_{\text{shot}} = n \cdot V_{\text{part}} \cdot (1 + s)$ | Shot volume | Injection moulding machine selection |

> [!NOTE]
> In the KTU 2024 scheme, examiners expect students to **derive** the rule of mixtures in a 14-mark question, so do not just memorise — **understand the physics**: load is shared between fibre and matrix **in parallel** (iso-strain), so stresses add weighted by volume fraction.

## 2.6 Why the "Why" and "How" Matter

- **Why are thermosets used for body panels (SMC, BMC)?** Because the cross-linked network gives the panel dimensional stability at the **paint-bake oven temperature** (~180–200 °C) that thermoplastics cannot withstand.
- **Why is PP dominant in bumpers?** It has the lowest density (~0.905 g/cm³) among commodity plastics, good impact strength (especially with EPDM modifiers), and excellent chemical resistance to road salts and fuels.
- **Why are carbon fibres aligned unidirectionally in body panels?** Because off-axis strength of a UD lamina drops by an order of magnitude — orientation of fibres governs the entire mechanical behaviour.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Rule of Mixtures for Composite Strength (along fibre direction)

This derivation is the **most frequently asked 7-mark analytical sub-part** in KTU Module 4 of PEMUT413. We work it out from first principles, line by line.

### Assumption: Iso-strain (Voigt) Model

In a unidirectional fibre-reinforced composite loaded along the fibre axis, both fibre and matrix experience the **same strain** because they are perfectly bonded at the interface and deform together.

$$\varepsilon_{c} = \varepsilon_{f} = \varepsilon_{m} = \varepsilon$$

**Step 1: Express the stress in each constituent using Hooke's law.**

$$\sigma_{f} = E_{f} \cdot \varepsilon \qquad \text{and} \qquad \sigma_{m} = E_{m} \cdot \varepsilon$$

**Step 2: Apply force equilibrium on a representative volume element (RVE).**

The total load carried by the composite cross-section is the sum of the load carried by the fibres and the load carried by the matrix, because they act **in parallel** mechanically.

$$F_{c} = F_{f} + F_{m}$$

**Step 3: Replace forces by stresses times areas.**

$$(\sigma_{c}) \cdot A_{c} = (\sigma_{f}) \cdot A_{f} + (\sigma_{m}) \cdot A_{m}$$

**Step 4: Divide throughout by the total area $A_c$.**

$$\sigma_{c} = \sigma_{f} \cdot \dfrac{A_{f}}{A_{c}} + \sigma_{m} \cdot \dfrac{A_{m}}{A_{c}}$$

**Step 5: Recognise the area ratios as the volume fractions** (for a unit cell, the area ratio = volume ratio).

$$\dfrac{A_{f}}{A_{c}} = V_{f} \qquad \text{and} \qquad \dfrac{A_{m}}{A_{c}} = 1 - V_{f}$$

**Step 6: Substitute to obtain the rule of mixtures for strength.**

$$\sigma_{c} = \sigma_{f} \cdot V_{f} + \sigma_{m} \cdot (1 - V_{f})$$

**Step 7: Rearrange the equation** to highlight the contribution of the fibre versus the matrix.

$$\sigma_{c} = \sigma_{m} + V_{f} \cdot (\sigma_{f} - \sigma_{m})$$

This is the **canonical form** of the rule of mixtures and is the form board examiners expect students to write at the end of the derivation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Validity:** This equation is the *upper bound* (Voigt bound) of composite strength — accurate only when the load is **perfectly aligned with the fibre direction**. For off-axis loading, the **inverse rule of mixtures (Reuss bound)** is used:

$$\dfrac{1}{\sigma_{c}} = \dfrac{V_{f}}{\sigma_{f}} + \dfrac{1 - V_{f}}{\sigma_{m}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Specific Strength of an FRP Body Panel

### Worked Numerical Example (14-Mark KTU Style)

A car bonnet (hood) is made of a glass-fibre-reinforced polyester (GRP) composite.
Given data:
- Fibre: E-glass, $V_f = 0.40$, $\sigma_f = 2{,}500$ $\text{MPa}$, $\rho_f = 2.55$ $\text{g/cm}^3$, $E_f = 72$ $\text{GPa}$
- Matrix: Polyester, $\sigma_m = 55$ $\text{MPa}$, $\rho_m = 1.20$ $\text{g/cm}^3$, $E_m = 3.5$ $\text{GPa}$

**Step A: Composite strength along the fibre direction (Rule of Mixtures).**

$$\sigma_{c} = \sigma_{f} \cdot V_{f} + \sigma_{m} \cdot (1 - V_{f})$$

$$\sigma_{c} = (2500)(0.40) + (55)(1 - 0.40)$$

$$\sigma_{c} = 1000 + 33 = 1033 \; \text{MPa}$$

**Step B: Composite Young's Modulus along the fibre direction.**

$$E_{c} = E_{f} \cdot V_{f} + E_{m} \cdot (1 - V_{f})$$

$$E_{c} = (72)(0.40) + (3.5)(0.60) = 28.8 + 2.1 = 30.9 \; \text{GPa}$$

**Step C: Composite density.**

$$\rho_{c} = \rho_{f} \cdot V_{f} + \rho_{m} \cdot (1 - V_{f})$$

$$\rho_{c} = (2.55)(0.40) + (1.20)(0.60) = 1.020 + 0.720 = 1.740 \; \text{g/cm}^3$$

**Step D: Specific strength.**

$$\text{SS} = \dfrac{\sigma_{c}}{\rho_{c}} = \dfrac{1033 \; \text{MPa}}{1.740 \; \text{g/cm}^3}$$

$$\text{SS} \approx 593.7 \; \text{MPa} \cdot \text{cm}^3 / \text{g} = 593.7 \; \text{kN} \cdot \text{m} / \text{kg}$$

**Step E: Compare with mild steel** (typical $\sigma_t = 350$ MPa, $\rho = 7.85$ g/cm³):

$$\text{SS}_{\text{steel}} = \dfrac{350}{7.85} \approx 44.6 \; \text{kNm/kg}$$

**Step F: Mass advantage ratio for the bonnet.**

$$\text{Mass Advantage} = \dfrac{\text{SS}_{\text{GRP}}}{\text{SS}_{\text{steel}}} = \dfrac{593.7}{44.6} \approx 13.3$$

Hence, **for the same load-bearing capacity, the GRP bonnet weighs roughly 1/13.3 ≈ 7.5 % of the equivalent steel bonnet** (subject to buckling and stiffness checks).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** GRP bonnet is about **13× more efficient** than mild steel on a strength-to-weight basis. In a 14-mark KTU question, the final paragraph should explicitly state this comparative advantage — examiners award the closing 2 marks for the engineering interpretation, not the algebra.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Properties vs Plastic Family (Master Comparison Table for Board Exams)

| Property | Thermoplastic | Thermosetting Plastic | Elastomer |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Molecular Structure** | Linear / branched chains | 3-D cross-linked network | Lightly cross-linked coiled chains |
| **Behaviour on Heating** | Softens → re-solidifies (reversible) | Decomposes (irreversible) | Softens but recovers shape |
| **Recyclability** | High (re-meltable) | Low (cannot re-melt) | Limited |
| **Tensile Strength (typical)** | 20 – 100 MPa | 50 – 200 MPa | 5 – 30 MPa |
| **Elongation at Break** | 10 % – 600 % | 0 % – 5 % (brittle) | 100 % – 800 % |
| **Young's Modulus** | 1 – 4 GPa | 3 – 12 GPa | 0.001 – 0.1 GPa |
| **Glass Transition $T_g$** | Often above use temp | Above decomposition | Below use temp |
| **Melt Viscosity at Processing** | Low (easy flow) | High (must cure in mould) | N/A (rubber process) |
| **Typical Process** | Injection moulding, blow moulding, extrusion | Compression moulding, RTM, RIM | Compression + vulcanisation |
| **Vehicle Body Application** | Bumpers (PP), tanks (HDPE), lenses (PC, PMMA) | SMC panels, PU foam seats, epoxy adhesives | Tyres (SBR), seals (EPDM), hoses |
| **Cost (relative)** | Low – Medium | Medium – High | Low – Medium |
| **Repairability** | Easy (welding, hot-air) | Difficult (patch + cure) | Easy (vulcanised patch) |

## 3.4 Detailed Process Analysis — The Three King-Pin Moulding Methods

### 3.4.1 Compression Moulding (for SMC and BMC thermoset panels)
1. A **charge** of SMC (Sheet Moulding Compound) or BMC (Bulk Moulding Compound) is placed in the lower half of a heated mould.
2. The upper mould half closes under a hydraulic press (typical pressure: 5 – 15 MPa).
3. Heat (~140–160 °C) triggers the **cross-linking cure reaction** of the thermoset resin.
4. After a cure time of 1 – 3 minutes, the mould opens and the rigid part is ejected.
5. Used for: bonnet, roof, doors of trucks, buses, and tractors.

### 3.4.2 Injection Moulding (for thermoplastics like PP, ABS, PA)
1. Plastic granules are fed from a hopper into a heated barrel.
2. A rotating screw shears and melts the polymer (melt temperature: 200–280 °C for PP).
3. The melt is injected under high pressure (50 – 140 MPa) into a closed mould cavity.
4. The mould is water-cooled; the part solidifies.
5. Mould opens → ejector pins push the part out → cycle repeats.
6. Cycle time: 20 – 90 s. Used for: bumpers, dashboards, clips, grilles.

### 3.4.3 Resin Transfer Moulding (RTM) — for high-quality FRP panels
1. Dry fibre preform is placed in a closed mould.
2. Liquid thermoset resin (polyester / epoxy / vinylester) is injected under low pressure (0.1 – 0.4 MPa).
3. Resin flows through and wets out the fibres, displacing air.
4. The mould is heated to cure the resin.
5. After cure (15 – 60 min), the mould opens and the finished FRP part is demoulded.
6. Used for: bus body panels, EV battery enclosures, structural floor pans.

> [!NOTE]
> **RTM vs SMC comparison (often asked as 7-mark KTU question):**
> - RTM gives **lower void content (1–3 %)** vs SMC (3–8 %).
> - RTM allows **more complex shapes** and tighter tolerances.
> - SMC has **faster cycle time** and is better for **high-volume production** (1,000+ parts/day).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Classification of Plastics Used in Vehicle Bodies

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[PLASTICS IN VEHICLE BODY ENGINEERING] --> B[Thermoplastics]
    A --> C[Thermosetting Plastics]
    A --> D[Elastomers]
    A --> E[Reinforced Plastics and Composites]

    B --> B1[PP Polypropylene]
    B --> B2[ABS Acrylonitrile Butadiene Styrene]
    B --> B3[PA Polyamide Nylon]
    B --> B4[PC Polycarbonate]
    B --> B5[PMMA Polymethyl Methacrylate]
    B --> B6[PE Polyethylene HDPE]
    B --> B7[PVC Polyvinyl Chloride]

    C --> C1[Phenol Formaldehyde]
    C --> C2[Melamine Formaldehyde]
    C --> C3[Epoxy Resin]
    C --> C4[Unsaturated Polyester]
    C --> C5[Polyurethane PU]

    D --> D1[Natural Rubber NR]
    D --> D2[Synthetic SBR]
    D --> D3[EPDM]
    D --> D4[Silicone Rubber]
    D --> D5[Nitrile Rubber NBR]

    E --> E1[GRP Glass Fibre Reinforced]
    E --> E2[CRP Carbon Fibre Reinforced]
    E --> E3[Aramid Kevlar Reinforced]
    E --> E4[Natural Fibre Reinforced]

    B1 --> APP1[Bumpers Dashboards Battery Casings]
    B2 --> APP2[Interior Trims Instrument Panels]
    B3 --> APP3[Under Hood Parts Radiator Tanks]
    B4 --> APP4[Headlamp Lenses Sunroofs]
    B5 --> APP5[Tail Lamp Lenses Light Guides]
    B6 --> APP6[Fuel Tanks]
    B7 --> APP7[Cable Insulation Roof Linings]

    C1 --> APC1[Brake Pads Clutch Facings]
    C2 --> APC2[Dashboard Laminates]
    C3 --> APC3[Structural Adhesives Composites]
    C4 --> APC4[Fibreglass Body Panels]
    C5 --> APC5[Seat Foam Energy Absorbing Bumpers]

    D1 --> APD1[Tyres]
    D2 --> APD2[Tyres]
    D3 --> APD3[Window Seals Weatherstrips]
    D4 --> APD4[Gaskets Hoses]
    D5 --> APD5[Fuel Hoses]

    E1 --> APE1[Bus Bodies Truck Cabs Hoods]
    E2 --> APE2[EV Battery Enclosures Racing Bodies]
    E3 --> APE3[Bullet Resistant Panels]
    E4 --> APE4[Door Trims Parcel Shelves]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Typical FRP Body Panel Manufacturing Flow (RTM Process)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F1[Fibre Roving and Mat] --> F2[Preform Cutting and Layup]
    F2 --> F3[Preform Placed in Mould Cavity]
    F3 --> F4[Mould Clamping]
    F4 --> F5[Resin Injection under Low Pressure]
    F5 --> F6[Fibre Wetting and Impregnation]
    F6 --> F7[Heat Assisted Cure 80 to 140 deg C]
    F7 --> F8[Demoulding]
    F8 --> F9[Trimming and Deflashing]
    F9 --> F10[Finished FRP Body Panel]
    F10 --> F11[Quality Inspection]
    F11 --> F12[Paint and Assembly]
```

## 4.3 Schematic Block Diagram — Composite Microstructure (Rule of Mixtures Visual)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    G1[Composite Cross Section] --> G2[Fibre Volume Fraction Vf]
    G1 --> G3[Matrix Volume Fraction 1 minus Vf]
    G2 --> G4[Fibres carry load sigma f times Vf]
    G3 --> G5[Matrix transfers load sigma m times 1 minus Vf]
    G4 --> G6[Total composite strength sigma c by Rule of Mixtures]
    G5 --> G6
    G6 --> G7[sigma c equals sigma f Vf plus sigma m 1 minus Vf]
    G6 --> G8[Specific strength equals sigma c divided by rho c]
    G8 --> G9[Compare with steel aluminium in Ashby Plot]
    G9 --> G10[Select lightest material meeting strength requirement]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** The flowcharts above are designed as **functional flow diagrams** rather than physical drawings, as required by the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 protocol for non-CS engineering topics. Each terminal node (e.g., `APP1`, `APC1`, `APE1`) corresponds to a real vehicle-body application that students must be able to recall in a viva or 3-mark short-answer question.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not confuse** "reinforced plastic" with "composite" — all fibre-reinforced plastics are composites, but not all composites are plastics (e.g., metal-matrix composites are not plastics).
> 2. **Do not** write "PVC is a thermoset" — PVC is a thermoplastic. A common student error in KTU exams.
> 3. **Do not** apply the rule of mixtures for off-axis loading — it is valid **only along the fibre direction**. For transverse loading, the Reuss (inverse) rule is required.
> 4. In 14-mark questions, **the engineering interpretation carries 2 marks** — a student who writes only algebra and skips the concluding engineering comment loses marks.
> 5. Use **standard SI units** (MPa, GPa, g/cm³) consistently. Mixing units is a top-3 reason for mark deductions.

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, May 2024]**
**"Differentiate between thermoplastic and thermosetting plastics with two examples of vehicle-body applications for each."** *(3 marks)*

### Model Answer (3 marks):

| Basis | Thermoplastic | Thermosetting Plastic |
|---|---|---|
| **Structure** | Linear / branched chains; no cross-links | Three-dimensional cross-linked network |
| **Heat response** | Softens on heating, hardens on cooling → **reversible** | Hardens permanently on curing; further heating causes **decomposition** |
| **Recyclability** | Can be re-melted and remoulded | Cannot be re-melted |
| **Example (vehicle)** | **PP** → bumpers, dashboard; **ABS** → interior trims | **Phenolic resin** → brake pads; **Polyester SMC** → body panels |

*(Award 3 marks: 1 mark for structural difference, 1 mark for heat response + recyclability, 1 mark for correct examples.)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**"Define specific strength. Why is it the key parameter for selecting body-panel materials?"** *(3 marks)*

### Model Answer (3 marks):
Specific strength is defined as the **ratio of material strength to its density**:

$$\text{SS} = \dfrac{\sigma_{c}}{\rho_{c}}$$

It is measured in $\text{kNm/kg}$. It is the key parameter for body-panel selection because:
1. The automotive industry target is **mass reduction** to improve fuel efficiency and reduce emissions.
2. A panel must withstand service stresses **without excessive weight**.
3. Specific strength directly captures the **load-carrying capacity per unit mass**, enabling a fair comparison between steel, aluminium, and FRP on the **Ashby material-selection chart**.
4. It accounts for both **strength and weight** in a single metric.

*(Award 3 marks: 1 mark for definition + equation, 2 marks for the four reasons.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 MARKS) — **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a) Classify the plastics used in vehicle body engineering. With neat sketches, explain any two compression-moulding-based methods for producing fibre-reinforced plastic (FRP) body panels. (7 marks)**

**(b) A glass-fibre-reinforced plastic (GRP) car bumper is made of unidirectional E-glass fibres in a polyester matrix. The fibre volume fraction is $V_f = 0.35$. Given $E_f = 72$ GPa, $E_m = 3.5$ GPa, $\sigma_f = 2{,}400$ MPa, $\sigma_m = 50$ MPa, $\rho_f = 2.55$ g/cm³, $\rho_m = 1.20$ g/cm³, calculate the composite modulus, strength, density, and specific strength. Compare the result with mild steel ($\sigma = 350$ MPa, $\rho = 7.85$ g/cm³). (7 marks)**

### Model Answer for Q-A (a) — 7 marks

**Classification of plastics (3 marks):**

Plastics used in vehicle body engineering are classified into **three families**:

1. **Thermoplastics** — Linear/branched chains, reversible softening. Examples: PP, ABS, PA, PC, PMMA, HDPE, PVC.
2. **Thermosetting plastics** — Cross-linked network, irreversible cure. Examples: Phenol-formaldehyde, Melamine, Epoxy, Unsaturated Polyester, Polyurethane.
3. **Elastomers** — Lightly cross-linked, high elasticity. Examples: Natural Rubber, SBR, EPDM, Silicone.

**Compression-moulding-based methods (4 marks):**

#### Method 1: Sheet Moulding Compound (SMC) Compression Moulding
- Pre-impregnated sheets of glass fibre, polyester resin, fillers, and thickeners are placed in a heated steel mould.
- The mould closes under a hydraulic press (5 – 15 MPa) at 140 – 160 °C.
- Heat + pressure → resin cures (cross-links) → rigid panel in 60 – 180 s.
- Used for: car bonnets, doors, bumpers, bus body panels.

#### Method 2: Bulk Moulding Compound (BMC) Compression Moulding
- A dough-like bulk compound of short glass fibres (5 – 25 mm) in a polyester resin paste is placed in the heated mould.
- The mould closes; heat cures the resin under pressure.
- Suitable for smaller, more complex 3-D parts (headlamp housings, electrical boxes).
- Lower fibre length → lower strength than SMC but better flow into complex shapes.

*(Award marks: 1 for the master classification, 2 for SMC explanation, 1 for BMC distinction. Use a flow-arrow sketch in the answer sheet for full marks.)*

### Model Answer for Q-A (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1: Composite Young's Modulus (1 mark).**

$$E_{c} = E_{f} V_{f} + E_{m} (1 - V_{f})$$

$$E_{c} = (72)(0.35) + (3.5)(0.65)$$

$$E_{c} = 25.2 + 2.275 = 27.475 \; \text{GPa}$$

**Step 2: Composite Tensile Strength (1 mark).**

$$\sigma_{c} = \sigma_{f} V_{f} + \sigma_{m} (1 - V_{f})$$

$$\sigma_{c} = (2400)(0.35) + (50)(0.65)$$

$$\sigma_{c} = 840 + 32.5 = 872.5 \; \text{MPa}$$

**Step 3: Composite Density (1 mark).**

$$\rho_{c} = \rho_{f} V_{f} + \rho_{m} (1 - V_{f})$$

$$\rho_{c} = (2.55)(0.35) + (1.20)(0.65)$$

$$\rho_{c} = 0.8925 + 0.7800 = 1.6725 \; \text{g/cm}^3$$

**Step 4: Specific Strength (1 mark).**

$$\text{SS}_{c} = \dfrac{872.5}{1.6725} \approx 521.7 \; \text{kNm/kg}$$

**Step 5: Specific Strength of Mild Steel (1 mark).**

$$\text{SS}_{\text{steel}} = \dfrac{350}{7.85} \approx 44.6 \; \text{kNm/kg}$$

**Step 6: Comparison and Engineering Interpretation (2 marks).**

The GRP composite has a specific strength roughly **11.7 times** that of mild steel. This means that, for the same load-carrying capacity, the composite bumper weighs only about **1/11.7 ≈ 8.5 %** of a steel bumper of equivalent strength. This enormous weight saving is the reason modern automotive bumpers are made of GRP or SMC composites rather than steel. The lower modulus (27.5 GPa vs ~210 GPa for steel) means the panel is less stiff, which is compensated by ribbed moulded geometry in the bumper design.

*(Award 2 marks: 1 for the numerical ratio, 1 for the engineering conclusion.)*

---

### QUESTION B (14 MARKS) — Alternative Choice — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a) Explain the Resin Transfer Moulding (RTM) process for manufacturing FRP vehicle body panels. List its advantages and limitations compared to SMC. (7 marks)**

**(b) Define the glass transition temperature $T_g$. A polymer blend of Polypropylene ($T_{g,1} = -10$ °C, mass fraction 0.6) and Polycarbonate ($T_{g,2} = 150$ °C, mass fraction 0.4) is to be used for a car interior panel. Estimate the effective $T_g$ of the blend using the Fox equation. Comment on the suitability of this blend for an interior dashboard application. (7 marks)**

### Model Answer for Q-B (a) — 7 marks

**RTM Process (4 marks):**

1. **Preform Preparation** — Glass-fibre mats or fabrics are cut and laid in a closed two-sided mould to form a "preform" of the final part.
2. **Mould Closure** — The mould is closed and clamped; sealing is critical to prevent resin leakage.
3. **Resin Injection** — A metered quantity of low-viscosity liquid thermoset resin (polyester / epoxy / vinylester) is injected under low pressure (0.1 – 0.4 MPa) through injection ports.
4. **Impregnation** — The resin flows through the fibre preform, displacing air and wetting out every fibre.
5. **Cure** — The mould is heated (80 – 140 °C) to cross-link the resin; cure time 15 – 60 minutes.
6. **Demoulding and Trimming** — The mould is opened and the solid FRP part is removed; flash is trimmed.

**Advantages of RTM over SMC (2 marks):**
- Lower void content (1 – 3 % vs 3 – 8 % for SMC) → higher mechanical properties.
- Better surface finish (Class A achievable).
- Allows thicker, more complex 3-D shapes with inserts and cores.
- Lower tooling cost for medium volumes (1,000 – 10,000 parts/yr).

**Limitations (1 mark):**
- Slower cycle time than SMC.
- Requires careful resin-flow simulation and mould sealing.
- Higher resin cost (low-viscosity grades needed).

### Model Answer for Q-B (b) — 7 marks

**Definition of $T_g$ (2 marks):**

The **glass transition temperature** $T_g$ is the temperature at which an amorphous polymer transitions from a hard, glassy state to a soft, rubbery state. Below $T_g$, polymer chain segments are frozen; above $T_g$, they gain segmental mobility. It is a second-order thermodynamic transition (no latent heat), distinct from the crystalline melting point $T_m$.

**Fox Equation (1 mark):**

$$\dfrac{1}{T_{g,\text{blend}}} = \dfrac{w_{1}}{T_{g,1}} + \dfrac{w_{2}}{T_{g,2}}$$

**Step 1: Convert $T_g$ values to Kelvin (1 mark).**

$$T_{g,1} = -10 + 273 = 263 \; \text{K}$$

$$T_{g,2} = 150 + 273 = 423 \; \text{K}$$

**Step 2: Substitute into Fox equation (1 mark).**

$$\dfrac{1}{T_{g,\text{blend}}} = \dfrac{0.6}{263} + \dfrac{0.4}{423}$$

$$\dfrac{1}{T_{g,\text{blend}}} = 0.002281 + 0.000946 = 0.003227 \; \text{K}^{-1}$$

**Step 3: Solve for $T_{g,\text{blend}}$ (1 mark).**

$$T_{g,\text{blend}} = \dfrac{1}{0.003227} \approx 309.9 \; \text{K} \approx 36.9 \; ^\circ\text{C}$$

**Step 4: Engineering Interpretation (1 mark):**

A dashboard in a parked car under direct summer sun can reach **80 – 100 °C**. The blend's $T_g$ of **~37 °C** is **far below** this service temperature, meaning the dashboard would soften, lose dimensional stability, and exhibit a tacky surface. The PP/PC blend in this ratio is **not suitable** for a dashboard — a higher-$T_g$ polymer (e.g., pure PC at 150 °C, or ABS at ~105 °C) is required for interior applications exposed to solar heating.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Plastics are polymers + additives** — additives tune properties (fillers, plasticizers, stabilizers, reinforcements).
- **Three families:** Thermoplastics (reversible, recyclable) | Thermosets (irreversible, heat-resistant) | Elastomers (rubbery, lightly cross-linked).
- **Rule of Mixtures** (iso-strain, fibre direction) → $\sigma_c = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m (1 - V_f)$; valid **only along fibres**.
- **Fox equation** → estimate $T_g$ of blends: $\dfrac{1}{T_{g,\text{mix}}} = \sum \dfrac{w_i}{T_{g,i}}$ — *use Kelvin, not Celsius*.
- **Specific strength** is the *key metric* for body-panel material selection: $\text{SS} = \sigma_c / \rho_c$.
- **Common automotive plastics to remember:** PP (bumpers) | ABS (interior) | PA (under-hood) | PC (headlamps) | PMMA (tail lamps) | HDPE (fuel tanks) | PVC (cables) | SMC/GRP (body panels) | PU foam (seats).
- **Three king-pin FRP processes:** SMC compression moulding (high volume, flat panels) | BMC compression moulding (small complex parts) | RTM (medium volume, high quality, complex shapes).
- **Composites win on specific strength & stiffness; lose on absolute stiffness** — design compensates with ribbed geometry.
- **TPU (Thermoplastic Polyurethane)** and **TPO (Thermoplastic Olefin)** are modern hybrid bumper materials — they bridge the rubber-plastic gap.
- **$T_g$ vs $T_m$:** $T_g$ is for amorphous regions (gradual softening); $T_m$ is for crystalline regions (sharp melting). Most semi-crystalline plastics (PP, PA) have both.
- **Conversion of $T_g$ to Kelvin** is the **#1 numerical error** in KTU exams — always add 273.
- **Sustainability:** Bioplastics (PLA, PHA), natural-fibre composites (flax, hemp), and recycled-content PP are the **emerging trends** — expect a 3-mark KTU question on this in upcoming semesters.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Properties of Materials
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT413 - VEHICLE BODY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Properties of Materials

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of **Vehicle Body Engineering**, the term **"Properties of Materials"** refers to the comprehensive set of mechanical, physical, chemical, and manufacturing characteristics that govern the **behaviour, performance, durability, and formability** of engineering materials used in the construction of automobile bodies, chassis sub-assemblies, closures, and structural reinforcements.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PEMUT413, Module 4):**
> *"Properties of Materials"* encompasses all intrinsic and extrinsic attributes — such as strength, stiffness, hardness, ductility, toughness, fatigue resistance, corrosion behaviour, density, and formability — that determine the suitability of a material (steel, aluminium, plastics, composites, glass) for a specific automotive body component.

### Classification of Material Properties

Material properties are broadly classified into **five categories** in automotive body design:

| Property Class | Examples | Relevance to Vehicle Body |
|---|---|---|
| **Mechanical** | Strength, Hardness, Toughness, Ductility | Crashworthiness, structural integrity |
| **Physical** | Density, Thermal conductivity, Specific heat | Weight reduction, thermal management |
| **Chemical** | Corrosion resistance, Oxidation resistance | Long-term durability |
| **Manufacturing** | Formability, Weldability, Machinability | Sheet metal stamping, joining |
| **Service** | Fatigue strength, Creep resistance, Wear resistance | Long-life performance |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Human Body Analogy"

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a vehicle body like a human skeleton wearing a skin:**
> - **Bones** (the chassis frame) need to be **hard** and **stiff** — just like the high-strength steel members of a car's safety cell.
> - **Skin** (the outer panels) needs to be **flexible** enough to be shaped into curves yet **tough** enough to resist dents — like aluminium or mild-steel outer panels.
> - **Cartilage** (the bumpers and crumple zones) must **absorb energy** on impact — like polymer-composite or foam-reinforced components.
> - **Blood** (electrical and fluid systems) needs **conductors** like copper — the wiring harness.

This is why a modern car uses **different materials in different zones** — a *multi-material design strategy* — and why understanding material properties is **the very foundation** of vehicle body engineering.

### Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- **Young's Modulus (E)** for steel: **≈ 200 GPa**
- **Density of mild steel**: **≈ 7850 kg/m³**
- **Density of aluminium alloy**: **≈ 2700 kg/m³**
- **Density of carbon-fibre composite**: **≈ 1600 kg/m³**
- **Poisson's ratio (ν)** for metals: typically **0.25 – 0.35**
- **Universal gas constant (R)** — not directly used but referenced in thermal expansion contexts: **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
- **Standard strain rate in tensile testing (ASTM E8)**: **≈ 0.001 to 0.5 s⁻¹**
- **Standard impact test temperatures**: **−40 °C, 20 °C, 60 °C** (for automotive Charpy tests per ISO 6603)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 Scheme places strong emphasis on the relationship between **material properties and formability** — i.e., *why* a particular grade of steel is chosen for a fender versus a B-pillar reinforcement.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mechanical Properties — The Backbone of Vehicle Body Design

### 2.1.1 Stress (σ) and Strain (ε)

When a body is subjected to an external load, internal resisting forces develop. **Stress** is the intensity of these internal forces per unit area, and **strain** is the relative deformation produced.

- **Why this matters in vehicle bodies:** Every panel and frame member experiences stress from road loads, passenger weight, and crash events. Designers *must* know how much stress a material can tolerate before it permanently deforms or fractures.

### 2.1.2 Elasticity and Plasticity

- **Elasticity:** The ability of a material to return to its original shape after the removal of load. Governed by **Hooke's Law** up to the elastic limit.
- **Plasticity:** The ability to undergo permanent deformation without rupture — *crucial for sheet-metal stamping of bonnets, doors, and fenders.*

### 2.1.3 Strength Characteristics

A material's strength is described by **three critical points** on the stress–strain curve:

1. **Yield Strength (σ_y):** Stress at which plastic deformation begins.
2. **Ultimate Tensile Strength (σ_u / UTS):** Maximum stress the material can withstand.
3. **Fracture Strength (σ_f):** Stress at which the material breaks.

### 2.1.4 Hardness

Hardness is the resistance of a material to **localized plastic deformation** (e.g., a dent or scratch). The three most common scales in automotive QA are:

- **Brinell Hardness Number (BHN)** — used for castings and forgings.
- **Rockwell Hardness (HRC, HRB)** — quick shop-floor testing.
- **Vickers Hardness (HV)** — micro-hardness for thin sheets and coatings.

### 2.1.5 Toughness vs. Brittleness

- **Toughness:** Energy absorbed per unit volume up to fracture (area under the entire σ–ε curve).
- **Brittleness:** Lack of plastic deformation — fracture occurs with little energy absorption. **Cast iron** is brittle; **mild steel** is tough.

### 2.1.6 Fatigue

Under **cyclic loading** (e.g., suspension towers, chassis rails), a material fails at stresses *far below* its yield strength. This is called **fatigue failure**, characterised by the **endurance limit (σ_e)**.

---

## 2.2 Physical and Chemical Properties

| Property | Definition | Vehicle-Body Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| **Density (ρ)** | Mass per unit volume | Fuel economy & weight reduction |
| **Thermal Conductivity (k)** | Rate of heat transfer | Engine-bay shielding, exhaust tunnels |
| **Specific Heat Capacity (c_p)** | Heat stored per kg per °C | Brake-disc thermal management |
| **Coefficient of Thermal Expansion (α)** | Dimensional change with temperature | Bimetallic joints (e.g., aluminium hood on steel hinges) |
| **Corrosion Resistance** | Resistance to oxidation/chemical attack | Underbody coating, galvannealed panels |

---

## 2.3 Manufacturing Properties (Formability-Focused)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formability** is the *single most important* manufacturing property for sheet-metal body panels.

- **Formability:** The ability of a sheet metal to undergo plastic deformation into the desired shape without cracking or wrinkling.
- **Weldability:** Ability to be joined by welding (spot welding for steel, MIG/TIG for aluminium).
- **Castability:** Ability to be poured into a mould to obtain a sound casting (engine blocks, suspension knuckles).
- **Machinability:** Ease of cutting by machining.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table contains every high-yield formula for Module 4 — Properties of Materials. Memorize for Part A and Part B.**

| # | Property | Formula | Variables & Units | Boundary / Note |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Normal Stress** | $\sigma = \dfrac{F}{A_0}$ | $F$ = force (N); $A_0$ = original area (m²) | Valid for axial loading |
| 2 | **Shear Stress** | $\tau = \dfrac{F_s}{A}$ | $F_s$ = shear force (N) | Used in bolted joints |
| 3 | **Engineering Strain** | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L_0}$ | $\Delta L$ = change in length (m) | Dimensionless |
| 4 | **True Strain** | $\varepsilon_t = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{L}{L_0}\right)$ | Used at large deformations | Forming limit analysis |
| 5 | **Hooke's Law** | $\sigma = E \, \varepsilon$ | $E$ = Young's modulus (Pa) | Valid in elastic region only |
| 6 | **Shear Modulus Relation** | $G = \dfrac{E}{2(1+\nu)}$ | $G$ = shear modulus; $\nu$ = Poisson's ratio | Valid for isotropic metals |
| 7 | **Bulk Modulus Relation** | $K = \dfrac{E}{3(1-2\nu)}$ | $K$ = bulk modulus | Hydrostatic loading |
| 8 | **Poisson's Ratio** | $\nu = -\dfrac{\varepsilon_{\text{lat}}}{\varepsilon_{\text{axial}}}$ | Lateral over axial strain | $\nu \approx 0.3$ for steels |
| 9 | **Modulus of Resilience** | $U_r = \dfrac{\sigma_y^{\,2}}{2E}$ | Energy per unit volume to yield | Springs, crash padding |
| 10 | **Modulus of Toughness** | $U_t = \displaystyle\int_0^{\varepsilon_f}\!\sigma\, d\varepsilon$ | Area under σ–ε curve | Total energy absorbed |
| 11 | **% Elongation** | $\%EL = \dfrac{L_f - L_0}{L_0}\times 100$ | Ductility measure | Standard gauge length |
| 12 | **% Reduction in Area** | $\%RA = \dfrac{A_0 - A_f}{A_0}\times 100$ | Ductility indicator | More sensitive than %EL |
| 13 | **Endurance Limit (Steels)** | $\sigma_e \approx 0.5 \, \sigma_u$ | Approximation for steels < 1400 MPa | Rotating-beam (R.R. Moore) test |
| 14 | **Brinell Hardness** | $\text{BHN} = \dfrac{2P}{\pi D\!\left[D - \sqrt{D^2 - d^2}\right]}$ | $P$ = load; $D$ = indenter dia.; $d$ = impression dia. | Standard indenter: 10 mm tungsten carbide |
| 15 | **Linear Thermal Expansion** | $\Delta L = \alpha L_0 \Delta T$ | $\alpha$ = CTE (per °C) | Bimetallic design |
| 16 | **Volumetric Thermal Expansion** | $\Delta V = \beta V_0 \Delta T$ | $\beta \approx 3\alpha$ | For isotropic solids |
| 17 | **Specific Strength** | $\text{SS} = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{\rho}$ | Used for lightweight design | Higher = better (aerospace/auto) |
| 18 | **Specific Stiffness** | $\text{SSt} = \dfrac{E}{\rho}$ | Critical for body-in-white stiffness | Higher = better |
| 19 | **Strain Hardening Exponent** | $\sigma = K \, \varepsilon^{\,n}$ | $K$ = strength coefficient; $n$ = hardening exponent | For deep-drawing steels |
| 20 | **Anisotropy Index (R-bar)** | $\bar{r} = \dfrac{r_0 + 2r_{45} + r_{90}}{4}$ | Sheet-metal drawability | $\bar{r} > 1$ = good drawability |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Crashworthiness:** High-toughness steels (DP600, TRIP) absorb impact energy in crumple zones.
- **Weight Reduction:** Aluminium (low $\rho$, high $\sigma/\rho$) replaces steel in bonnets, tailgates.
- **NVH (Noise-Vibration-Harshness):** Stiffness (E) determines panel resonance frequencies.
- **Corrosion Protection:** Galvannealed and hot-dip galvanized steels use **sacrificial Zn coatings** (electrochemical series principle).
- **Recyclability:** Steel and aluminium have well-established recycling streams — critical for **End-of-Life Vehicle (ELV)** directives in EU/India.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Relationship Between Elastic Constants (E, G, K, ν)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This derivation is a guaranteed 5–7 mark question in KTU Module 4 exams.** Master the algebra and the physical reasoning.

### Statement
For a **homogeneous, isotropic, linearly elastic material**, the three elastic moduli — **Young's Modulus (E)**, **Shear Modulus (G)**, and **Bulk Modulus (K)** — are not independent. They are related through **Poisson's ratio (ν)** via:

$$
G = \frac{E}{2(1+\nu)}
$$

$$
K = \frac{E}{3(1-2\nu)}
$$

$$
E = \frac{9KG}{3K+G}
$$

### Derivation of $G = E / [2(1+\nu)]$

Consider a **pure shear element** of side length $L$, subjected to shear stress $\tau$ on its faces. The shear strain $\gamma$ is:

$$
\gamma = \frac{\Delta L}{L}
$$

By definition of shear modulus:

$$
\tau = G \, \gamma
$$

Now decompose this shear stress into two **principal stresses** using Mohr's circle / stress transformation. A pure-shear state is equivalent to:

$$
\sigma_1 = +\tau, \qquad \sigma_2 = -\tau
$$

The corresponding principal strains (using generalized Hooke's law) are:

$$
\varepsilon_1 = \frac{1}{E}\!\left(\sigma_1 - \nu\sigma_2\right) = \frac{1}{E}\!\left(\tau - \nu(-\tau)\right) = \frac{\tau(1+\nu)}{E}
$$

$$
\varepsilon_2 = \frac{1}{E}\!\left(\sigma_2 - \nu\sigma_1\right) = \frac{1}{E}\!\left(-\tau - \nu\tau\right) = -\frac{\tau(1+\nu)}{E}
$$

The **engineering shear strain** is related to principal strains by:

$$
\gamma = \varepsilon_1 - \varepsilon_2 = \frac{\tau(1+\nu)}{E} - \left(-\frac{\tau(1+\nu)}{E}\right) = \frac{2\tau(1+\nu)}{E}
$$

Substituting $\tau = G\,\gamma$:

$$
\gamma = \frac{2 G \gamma (1+\nu)}{E}
$$

Cancelling $\gamma$ on both sides:

$$
1 = \frac{2G(1+\nu)}{E}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\,G = \frac{E}{2(1+\nu)}\,}
$$

$\blacksquare$

---

### Derivation of $K = E / [3(1-2\nu)]$

Consider a body under **hydrostatic pressure** $p$. The stress state is:

$$
\sigma_x = \sigma_y = \sigma_z = -p
$$

The volumetric strain is:

$$
\frac{\Delta V}{V_0} = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z
$$

Using generalized Hooke's law for each direction:

$$
\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}\!\left[\sigma_x - \nu(\sigma_y + \sigma_z)\right] = \frac{1}{E}\!\left[-p - \nu(-p - p)\right] = \frac{-p(1-2\nu)}{E}
$$

By symmetry:

$$
\varepsilon_x = \varepsilon_y = \varepsilon_z = \frac{-p(1-2\nu)}{E}
$$

Adding all three:

$$
\frac{\Delta V}{V_0} = 3 \cdot \frac{-p(1-2\nu)}{E} = \frac{-3p(1-2\nu)}{E}
$$

By definition, **Bulk Modulus**:

$$
K = -\frac{p}{\Delta V / V_0} = -\frac{p}{\dfrac{-3p(1-2\nu)}{E}}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\,K = \frac{E}{3(1-2\nu)}\,}
$$

$\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Modulus of Resilience from a Linear σ–ε Curve

The **Modulus of Resilience** ($U_r$) is the strain energy stored per unit volume up to the elastic limit.

For a linear-elastic region, the strain energy density is the area of the triangle under the σ–ε line up to $\sigma_y$:

$$
U_r = \int_0^{\varepsilon_y} \sigma \, d\varepsilon
$$

With $\sigma = E\varepsilon$ in the elastic region:

$$
U_r = \int_0^{\varepsilon_y} E\varepsilon \, d\varepsilon = E \cdot \frac{\varepsilon_y^{\,2}}{2}
$$

Using $\varepsilon_y = \sigma_y / E$:

$$
U_r = \frac{E}{2}\left(\frac{\sigma_y}{E}\right)^2 = \frac{\sigma_y^{\,2}}{2E}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\,U_r = \frac{\sigma_y^{\,2}}{2E}\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Significance:** This is the energy absorbed per unit volume *without* permanent deformation. In automotive suspensions and bumper mounts, this energy is *deliberately* stored and released for ride comfort.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem (KTU Model):** A mild-steel tensile specimen has an original diameter of **12 mm** and a gauge length of **50 mm**. Under a tensile load of **45 kN**, the elongation is measured as **0.10 mm**. At fracture, the load drops to **30 kN** with elongation of **15 mm** and the diameter reduces to **8.5 mm**. Calculate: **(a)** Engineering stress and strain at fracture, **(b)** True stress and true strain at fracture, **(c)** % Reduction in Area, **(d)** % Elongation.

**Given:**
- $D_0 = 12\,\text{mm}$
- $L_0 = 50\,\text{mm}$
- At fracture: $F_f = 30\,\text{kN}$, $L_f = 50 + 15 = 65\,\text{mm}$, $D_f = 8.5\,\text{mm}$

### (a) Engineering Stress & Strain at Fracture

Original cross-sectional area:

$$
A_0 = \frac{\pi}{4}(12)^2 = \frac{\pi}{4}\times 144 = 113.10\;\text{mm}^2
$$

Engineering stress at fracture:

$$
\sigma_f = \frac{F_f}{A_0} = \frac{30{,}000}{113.10} \approx 265.3\;\text{MPa}
$$

Engineering strain at fracture:

$$
\varepsilon_f = \frac{L_f - L_0}{L_0} = \frac{65 - 50}{50} = 0.30
$$

### (b) True Stress & True Strain at Fracture

Final cross-sectional area:

$$
A_f = \frac{\pi}{4}(8.5)^2 = \frac{\pi}{4}\times 72.25 = 56.74\;\text{mm}^2
$$

True stress at fracture:

$$
\sigma_{t,f} = \frac{F_f}{A_f} = \frac{30{,}000}{56.74} \approx 528.8\;\text{MPa}
$$

True strain at fracture:

$$
\varepsilon_{t,f} = \ln\!\left(\frac{A_0}{A_f}\right) = \ln\!\left(\frac{113.10}{56.74}\right) = \ln(1.9933) \approx 0.6896
$$

### (c) % Reduction in Area

$$
\%RA = \frac{A_0 - A_f}{A_0}\times 100 = \frac{113.10 - 56.74}{113.10}\times 100 \approx 49.83\%
$$

### (d) % Elongation

$$
\%EL = \frac{L_f - L_0}{L_0}\times 100 = \frac{15}{50}\times 100 = 30\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** A typical **ductile mild steel** exhibits %EL > 20% and %RA > 40% — confirming the chosen material is suitable for **deep-drawn automotive panels**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
