# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the following properties of materials used in vehicle body construction:**
**(a) Ductility** **(b) Malleability** **(c) Toughness**

**Model Answer:**

> **(a) Ductility:** It is the property of a material that enables it to be drawn into wires or elongated under tensile load without fracture. It is measured by **% Elongation** or **% Reduction in Area** in a tensile test. *Example:* Mild steel used in automotive panels is highly ductile.
>
> **(b) Malleability:** It is the property that allows a material to be deformed under **compressive** loads (rolled or hammered into thin sheets) without cracking. *Example:* Aluminium sheets used for bonnets and door panels.
>
> **(c) Toughness:** It is the ability of a material to absorb **energy** and undergo plastic deformation before fracture. Quantitatively, it is the area under the entire stress–strain curve (modulus of toughness). *Example:* Boron steel used in bumper reinforcements.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**Distinguish between Elastic Deformation and Plastic Deformation. State Hooke's Law and its limitations.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Elastic Deformation | Plastic Deformation |
|---|---|---|
| Reversibility | Fully recoverable | Permanent, non-recoverable |
| Atomic mechanism | Atomic bond stretching | Dislocation glide/slip |
| Stress level | Below yield strength | Above yield strength |
| Energy | Stored as strain energy | Dissipated as heat |
| Mathematical law | $\sigma = E\varepsilon$ | Non-linear |

**Hooke's Law:** Within the elastic limit, stress is directly proportional to strain: $\sigma = E\varepsilon$, where $E$ is **Young's Modulus (Pa)**.

**Limitations:**
1. Valid only in the **linear-elastic region**.
2. Applicable only for **isotropic, homogeneous** materials.
3. Fails at **stress concentrations** (notch effect).
4. Not valid at **high temperatures** (creep regime).
5. Not valid for **time-dependent** behaviour (viscoelastic polymers).

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

> **(a)** With the help of a clearly labelled **stress–strain diagram for a ductile material** like mild steel, explain the following points: Proportional Limit, Elastic Limit, Yield Point, Ultimate Tensile Strength, Necking, and Fracture Point. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Derive the relationship between **Young's Modulus (E), Shear Modulus (G), and Poisson's Ratio (ν)** for a homogeneous isotropic material. Given $E = 210\,\text{GPa}$ and $\nu = 0.30$, calculate the value of G. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(a) — 7 Marks

**Stress-Strain Curve for Mild Steel (Ductile):**

```
   Stress (σ)
   ▲
σu │            .  UTS
   │          /  \
σy │---------/----\---------- Yield
   │       /      \
   │      /        \
   │     /          \  Necking
   │    /            \
σf │   /              \●  Fracture
   │  /                  (∥)
   │ /                    
   │/________________________▶  Strain (ε)
   O    εy      εu   εf
```

**Description of each point (one mark per point × 6 = 6 marks + 1 for diagram):**

1. **Proportional Limit (σ_p):** The maximum stress up to which stress is *linearly* proportional to strain. Hooke's law strictly valid.
2. **Elastic Limit (σ_e):** Maximum stress below which the specimen regains its original dimensions on unloading.
3. **Yield Point (σ_y):** Stress at which noticeable plastic deformation begins *without any increase in load* (especially for mild steel, an upper and lower yield point can be observed).
4. **Ultimate Tensile Strength (σ_u or UTS):** Maximum stress the material can withstand; calculated as the maximum load divided by the *original* cross-sectional area.
5. **Necking:** Localised reduction in cross-section beyond UTS; true stress continues to rise but engineering stress falls.
6. **Fracture Point (σ_f):** The stress at which the specimen physically separates into two pieces.

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(b) — 7 Marks

**Given:**
- $E = 210\,\text{GPa} = 210\times 10^9\,\text{Pa}$
- $\nu = 0.30$

**Find:** $G$

**Step 1 — State the relationship** (1 mark):
$$
G = \frac{E}{2(1+\nu)}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute** (1 mark):
$$
G = \frac{210\times 10^9}{2(1+0.30)} = \frac{210\times 10^9}{2\times 1.30}
$$

**Step 3 — Simplify** (1 mark):
$$
G = \frac{210\times 10^9}{2.60} = \frac{210}{2.60}\times 10^9 = 80.77\times 10^9\,\text{Pa}
$$

**Step 4 — Final answer** (1 mark):
$$
\boxed{G \approx 80.77\,\text{GPa}}
$$

**Derivation** (refer to Section 3.1 for the full derivation, worth 3 marks):
- Start with pure shear state $\sigma_1 = +\tau$, $\sigma_2 = -\tau$
- Use generalized Hooke's law for principal strains.
- Obtain engineering shear strain $\gamma = \varepsilon_1 - \varepsilon_2$.
- Substitute $\tau = G\gamma$ and solve.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**`[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`**

> **(a)** Explain in detail the various **mechanical properties** of materials used in automotive body construction. Discuss how **strength, hardness, and toughness** are measured in the laboratory. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A steel component is subjected to a fluctuating stress that varies from **+120 MPa to −60 MPa**. Calculate: **(i)** Mean stress, **(ii)** Stress amplitude, **(iii)** Stress ratio, **(iv)** Amplitude ratio. If the endurance limit of the material is **280 MPa**, comment on its fatigue safety using the **Soderberg criterion**, given the yield strength = **420 MPa**. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(a) — 7 Marks

**Mechanical Properties Discussion (4 marks):**

1. **Strength:** Ability to resist deformation/fracture under load. Quantified by yield, ultimate, and fracture strengths obtained from a **Universal Testing Machine (UTM)** per ASTM E8.
2. **Hardness:** Resistance to localised plastic deformation. Measured by:
   - **Brinell test** — hardened steel/tungsten-carbide ball indenter; for castings/forgings.
   - **Rockwell test** — diamond cone or steel ball; rapid shop-floor use.
   - **Vickers test** — square-pyramidal diamond; micro-hardness for thin sheets.
3. **Toughness:** Energy absorbed up to fracture. Measured by **Charpy V-notch** or **Izod impact test** (ASTM E23). Critical for crash simulation.
4. **Ductility:** Measured by %EL and %RA from tensile test.
5. **Fatigue strength:** Measured by **rotating-beam (R.R. Moore)** test or **axial fatigue test**; gives S–N curve.
6. **Stiffness:** Quantified by Young's modulus E from the slope of the elastic region.

**Laboratory Measurement Details (3 marks):**

- **Tensile Test (UTM):** Standard specimen per ASTM E8; loaded at a controlled rate (typically 0.5–50 mm/min); force–elongation curve is plotted.
- **Impact Test (Charpy/Izod):** V-notched specimen struck by a pendulum; energy absorbed = potential energy difference.
- **Hardness Tests:** Indentation depth/diameter measured optically or by depth gauge.

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(b) — 7 Marks

**Given:**
- $\sigma_{\max} = +120\,\text{MPa}$
- $\sigma_{\min} = -60\,\text{MPa}$
- $\sigma_e = 280\,\text{MPa}$ (endurance limit)
- $\sigma_y = 420\,\text{MPa}$ (yield strength)

### (i) Mean Stress ($\sigma_m$)
$$
\sigma_m = \frac{\sigma_{\max} + \sigma_{\min}}{2} = \frac{120 + (-60)}{2} = \frac{60}{2} = 30\;\text{MPa}
$$

### (ii) Stress Amplitude ($\sigma_a$)
$$
\sigma_a = \frac{\sigma_{\max} - \sigma_{\min}}{2} = \frac{120 - (-60)}{2} = \frac{180}{2} = 90\;\text{MPa}
$$

### (iii) Stress Ratio ($R$)
$$
R = \frac{\sigma_{\min}}{\sigma_{\max}} = \frac{-60}{120} = -0.50
$$

### (iv) Amplitude Ratio ($A$)
$$
A = \frac{\sigma_a}{\sigma_m} = \frac{90}{30} = 3.00
$$

### (v) Soderberg Criterion Check

The Soderberg equation for safe fatigue design is:

$$
\frac{\sigma_a}{\sigma_e} + \frac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_y} \leq \frac{1}{n}
$$

For safety factor $n = 1$ (i.e., check whether at the very limit):

$$
\frac{90}{280} + \frac{30}{420} = 0.3214 + 0.0714 = 0.3928
$$

Since $0.3928 < 1$, the design is **SAFE** at $n = 1$.

The minimum factor of safety is:

$$
n = \frac{1}{0.3928} \approx 2.55
$$

**Comment:** With a factor of safety of **~2.55**, the component has a comfortable margin against both yielding and fatigue failure — acceptable for a long-life automotive structural member.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Sign error in stress ratio:** A common mistake is computing $R = \sigma_{\max}/\sigma_{\min}$ (it should be $\sigma_{\min}/\sigma_{\max}$).
> 2. **Confusing stress amplitude with stress range:** Stress **amplitude** is half the range; **range** = $\sigma_{\max} - \sigma_{\min}$.
> 3. **Forgetting units:** Stress ratio and amplitude ratio are dimensionless — but always state the units (MPa) of the stresses themselves.
> 4. **Soderberg vs. Goodman vs. Gerber:** Know which one applies. KTU often asks for **Soderberg** (most conservative, linear).
> 5. **In the elastic-modulus derivation,** losing 2–3 marks for not stating *assumptions* (homogeneous, isotropic, linear-elastic) is common.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: Properties of Materials**

- **Five categories of properties:** Mechanical, Physical, Chemical, Manufacturing, Service.
- **Hooke's Law:** $\sigma = E\varepsilon$ — valid only in the elastic region.
- **Elastic constant relations:** $G = E/[2(1+\nu)]$, $K = E/[3(1-2\nu)]$ — memorize and know how to derive.
- **Modulus of Resilience:** $U_r = \sigma_y^2/(2E)$ — strain energy stored in elastic region.
- **Modulus of Toughness:** Area under the entire σ–ε curve — energy absorbed up to fracture.
- **Three hardness tests:** Brinell (castings), Rockwell (shop-floor), Vickers (thin sheets/micro).
- **% Elongation vs. %RA:** Both are ductility measures; %RA is more sensitive.
- **Fatigue endurance limit:** $\sigma_e \approx 0.5\,\sigma_u$ for steels (not valid for Al or composites).
- **Specific strength / Specific stiffness:** Critical metrics for lightweight vehicle design — **higher is better**.
- **Formability metrics:** Average strain-hardening exponent $n$ and normal anisotropy $\bar{r}$.
- **Material selection for automotive bodies:**
  - *Outer panels* → low-carbon DC04 / aluminium 5xxx (formability priority).
  - *Structural reinforcements* → HSLA, DP600, TRIP, boron steel (strength priority).
  - *Bumpers & crash boxes* → high-toughness polymers and foams.
  - *Bonnet, doors* → aluminium (weight saving), with hinge design considering differential CTE.
- **Thermal expansion:** $\Delta L = \alpha L_0 \Delta T$ — important for bimetallic (steel-aluminium) joints.
- **Corrosion protection:** Galvanizing, galvannealing, e-coating, and use of weathering steels.
- **Soderberg criterion:** $\dfrac{\sigma_a}{\sigma_e} + \dfrac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_y} \leq \dfrac{1}{n}$ — the safe design inequality.
- **Poisson's ratio:** For most metals $\nu \approx 0.30$; for rubber $\nu \approx 0.49$; for cork $\nu \approx 0$.
- **KTU trap:** Never confuse **true stress** ($\sigma_t = F/A_{\text{instantaneous}}$) with **engineering stress** ($\sigma = F/A_0$).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Paint Types and Characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT413 - VEHICLE BODY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Paint Types and Characteristics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Vehicle Body Engineering**, automotive paint is defined as a **multi-layered polymeric coating system** applied to the exterior and interior surfaces of a vehicle body to provide four simultaneous engineering functions:

1. **Corrosion Protection** — Prevention of oxidation of the underlying sheet metal (typically mild steel, galvanized steel, or aluminium).
2. **Aesthetic Finish** — Provision of colour, gloss, and texture consistent with brand identity and customer expectations.
3. **Mechanical Protection** — Resistance to stone-chip impact, UV radiation, chemical attack (acid rain, bird droppings, fuel splashes), and thermal cycling.
4. **Functional Surface Property** — Provision of specific surface characteristics such as low drag coefficient, anti-scratch behaviour, or self-healing.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEMUT413) Module 4** syllabus, paints are broadly classified by their **binder/resin chemistry**, **physical state during application**, and **curing mechanism**. The paint film is a non-uniform, multi-coat laminate typically comprising four distinct layers:

| Coat Layer | Function | Typical Dry Film Thickness |
|---|---|---|
| **Electrodeposition (E-coat)** | Anti-corrosion primer | 18–25 µm |
| **Primer / Surfacer** | Surface levelling, chip resistance | 25–35 µm |
| **Basecoat** | Colour, metallic/mica effect | 12–25 µm |
| **Clearcoat** | Gloss, UV protection, scratch resistance | 35–45 µm |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition to Memorize:** *"Automotive paint is a complex colloidal system of a polymeric binder (resin), pigment dispersion, solvent/carrier, and functional additives that, upon application and curing, forms a continuous, adherent, and protective film on the vehicle body substrate."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of automotive paint like **a multi-layered sandwich protecting a metal sandwich filling from going stale**:

- The **E-coat** is like the *plastic inner wrapper* touching the metal directly — it stops rust.
- The **Primer/Surfacer** is like the *cheese layer* — it smooths out the bumps (levels the surface) and absorbs small impacts.
- The **Basecoat** is the *visible meat/vegetable layer* — this is what gives the burger its colour and "look" (red, shiny, metallic).
- The **Clearcoat** is the *top bun* — transparent, glossy, hard, and protects everything underneath from being squashed or sun-dried.

Just as you wouldn't want a single thick plastic coat on a burger (it would crack and not protect), automotive engineers use **multiple thin coats** of different chemistry, each doing a specific job, to achieve both protection and beauty.

## 1.3 Key Physical & Performance Metrics

The following standard automotive industry metrics govern paint evaluation:

- **Gloss** (measured at 60°): **> 90 GU (Gloss Units)** for premium OEM finishes
- **DOI (Distinctness of Image)**: **> 80** for high-quality mirror-like finishes
- **Adhesion Cross-Cut Test**: **ISO 2409 Class 0** (no peeling)
- **Hardness**: **> 2H pencil hardness** (Wolff-Wilborn scale)
- **Stone-Chip Resistance**: **SAE J400** rating ≤ 6B (less than 10% paint loss)
- **Curing Temperature**: **140–160°C (metal temperature)** for OEM bake ovens

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (KTU Frequently Tested):** The difference between *drying* (solvent evaporation — physical process) and *curing* (chemical crosslinking of resin molecules — chemical process). **Thermoplastic** paints only dry; **thermosetting** paints both dry AND cure.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Chemical Composition of Automotive Paint

Every automotive paint, regardless of type, consists of **four fundamental components**:

### 2.1.1 Binder (Resin) — 25–60% by weight
The **film-forming polymer** that holds the pigment particles together and adheres to the substrate. The resin type defines the paint family.

| Resin Type | Paint Family | Curing Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| Alkyd | Enamel | Oxidative + solvent evaporation |
| Acrylic thermoplastic | Lacquer | Solvent evaporation only |
| Acrylic melamine | Basecoat/Clearcoat (OEM) | Thermosetting (heat crosslink) |
| Polyurethane (2K) | Refinish / premium | Isocyanate-polyol reaction |
| Epoxy | Primer | Polyamine crosslink |
| Polyester | Powder Coating | Heat-induced crosslink |

### 2.1.2 Pigment — 5–25% by weight
Provides **colour, opacity, and special effects**. Categorized as:
- **Inorganic pigments**: Titanium dioxide (TiO₂ — white), carbon black, iron oxides — high UV and chemical resistance.
- **Organic pigments**: Phthalocyanines, quinacridones — high chroma but lower durability.
- **Effect pigments**: Aluminium flakes (metallic), mica coated with metal oxide (pearlescent), Xirallic (interference).

### 2.1.3 Solvent (Carrier) — 30–70% by weight
Provides **application viscosity**. Modern regulations drive shift to **water-borne** systems (VOC limits: **< 250 g/L** in EU Directive 2004/42/EC).

### 2.1.4 Additives — 0.1–5% by weight
Functional chemicals that modify specific properties:
- **Rheology modifiers** (flow, sag resistance)
- **UV absorbers** (HALS — Hindered Amine Light Stabilizers)
- **Catalysts/Catalysts driers** (for oxidative cure)
- **Defoamers, anti-crater agents, conductivity additives** (for electrostatic application)

## 2.2 Paint Curing Mechanism (Chemistry Level)

### 2.2.1 Thermoplastic Drying (Lacquer)
The resin is already a **pre-formed high-molecular-weight polymer** dissolved in solvent. After spraying, solvent evaporates → polymer chains entangle → solid film forms.

$$\text{Solvent (liquid)} \xrightarrow{\text{Evaporation}} \text{Polymer film (solid)}$$

**Reversible** — re-dissolves in the same solvent. **Not used in OEM** manufacturing due to poor durability.

### 2.2.2 Thermosetting Cure (Enamel, OEM, 2K Polyurethane)
Low-molecular-weight **pre-polymer + crosslinker** are mixed/applied. Heat (or ambient + catalyst) triggers **chemical crosslinking**:

$$\text{Resin-OH} + \text{R-NCO} \longrightarrow \text{Resin-O-CO-NH-R (Urethane link)}$$

This is the **polyol + isocyanate (2K PU)** reaction, forming a **3D network** — irreversible, high chemical and mechanical resistance.

### 2.2.3 Oxidative Cure (Alkyd Enamel)
The alkyd resin reacts with **atmospheric oxygen** in the presence of metallic driers (cobalt, manganese naphthenates):

$$\text{Resin-CH=CH-CH}_2\text{-} + \text{O}_2 \longrightarrow \text{Resin-CH=CH-CH(OOH)-} \rightarrow \text{Crosslinked network}$$

Slow, ambient temperature cure. Used in **aftermarket/touch-up paints**.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are KTU Board-favorite topics. Memorize the units and boundary conditions.**

| # | Formula / Relationship | Description | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **$S = \dfrac{V_s - V_r}{V_s} \times 100\%$** | **% Solids by Volume** (where $V_s$ = solids volume, $V_r$ = resin contribution) | Determines VOC emission and film build per coat |
| 2 | **$\text{VOC} = \dfrac{\rho_{solvent} \times W_{solvent}}{V_{total}}$** | Volatile Organic Compound content (g/L) | Regulatory compliance (EU 2004/42/EC) |
| 3 | **$T_{film} = \sum t_i$** | Total dry film thickness = sum of all coats (µm) | Paint shop quality control |
| 4 | **$A_{coverage} = \dfrac{V \times S}{\rho \times T_{film}}$** | Theoretical coverage area (m²/L) | Production planning |
| 5 | **$R = \dfrac{G_{60}}{G_{specular}}$** | Gloss ratio (60°/specular) | Gloss measurement & control |
| 6 | **$E_a \approx 25\text{–}40 \text{ kJ/mol}$** | Activation energy of crosslink cure | Oven design & cure window |
| 7 | **$\eta = \eta_0 e^{E/RT}$** | Viscosity-temperature (Arrhenius) for paint | Spray atomization viscosity control |
| 8 | **$E_{film} \propto \dfrac{1}{\delta}$** (where $\delta$ is film thickness) | Inverse proportionality — elastic modulus vs. thickness | Stress-cracking in thick films |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE FOR STUDENTS:** In KTU board answer sheets, when writing absolute values, always use $\vert x \vert$ notation, **not** vertical pipes in plain text — many KTU valuation scripts auto-deduct marks for ambiguous notation.

## 2.4 Classification of Automotive Paints (Master Taxonomy)

### 2.4.1 By Resin Chemistry
1. **Lacquer (Nitrocellulose / Acrylic Thermoplastic)** — fast dry, low build, polish required.
2. **Enamel (Alkyd)** — slow oxidative dry, high gloss, traditional.
3. **Acrylic Enamel** — acrylic resin + melamine crosslinker; OEM standard until 1980s.
4. **Polyurethane (2K PU)** — isocyanate + polyol; **gold standard for automotive refinish**.
5. **Water-Borne Basecoat** — water as carrier; environmental compliance.
6. **Epoxy Primer** — excellent adhesion, anti-corrosion, but UV-sensitive (top-coated).
7. **Powder Coating** — 100% solids, electrostatic application, no solvent, very durable.

### 2.4.2 By Curing Mechanism
- **Air-Drying / Oxidative** (Enamel, Lacquer)
- **Forced Air / Low-Bake** (60–80°C — refinish)
- **High-Bake / OEM** (140–160°C — manufacturing)

### 2.4.3 By Film Build
- **Low Build**: < 20 µm
- **Medium Build**: 20–40 µm
- **High Build**: 40–80 µm

## 2.5 Key Paint Characteristics (KTU Board-Listed)

For each paint type, the following properties are **tested and documented**:

### 2.5.1 Drying Time
- **Dust-Free Time (DFT)**: Time until airborne dust does not stick.
- **Touch-Dry Time (TDT)**: Time until light finger contact leaves no mark.
- **Through-Dry Time (THDT)**: Time until full mechanical properties develop.
- **Hard-Dry / Full Cure**: Time until complete chemical crosslink.

### 2.5.2 Film Properties
- **Gloss**: Specular reflection at 60°.
- **DOI**: Sharpness of reflected image.
- **Haze**: Milkiness due to surface micro-roughness.
- **Adhesion**: Cross-cut (ISO 2409) or pull-off (ASTM D4541).
- **Flexibility**: Mandrel bend test (ASTM D522).
- **Hardness**: Pencil hardness (Wolff-Wilborn), König/Persoz pendulum.
- **Impact Resistance**: Falling-weight / Gardner impact (ASTM D2794).

### 2.5.3 Durability
- **Weathering resistance**: Xenon-arc (SAE J1960) or QUV — measured in **kJ/m² (UV dose)** or hours.
- **Chemical resistance**: Acid spot test (10% H₂SO₄, 30 min), solvent rub (MEK double rubs).
- **Humidity resistance**: 1000 hrs at 38°C / 100% RH (ASTM D2247).
- **Stone-chip resistance**: SAE J400 gravelometer (rated A–D, with 6B being best OEM target).

### 2.5.4 Application Properties
- **Viscosity**: 18–25 seconds Ford Cup #4 (or 25–35 s DIN Cup #4) at 20°C.
- **Sprayability**: Atomization quality, sagging, orange peel, dry spray.
- **Pot Life**: Working time after mixing (e.g., **2K PU = 2–4 hrs**).
- **Recoatability**: Time window for next coat without sanding.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **OEM Manufacturing** (e.g., Maruti, Hyundai India): 100% uses **E-coat → Water-borne basecoat → Solvent clearcoat (or 2K clearcoat)** on a **Conveyorised Paint Shop (CPS)** with **30+ robots** at a line speed of **5–7 m/min**.
- **Refinish / Crash Repair**: Uses **2K PU** due to ambient cure, fast cycle, and colour match capability.
- **EV Battery Housings**: Increasingly use **powder coating** for thermal and dielectric properties.
- **Plastic Bumpers**: Use **flexible 2K PU** or **RIM coatings** since the substrate flexes.

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation: VOC Calculation

This is a frequently asked **numerical problem** in KTU board exams.

> **Problem Statement (KTU Style):** A refinish clearcoat has the following formulation: 45% resin (density 1.10 g/cm³), 35% solvent X (density 0.85 g/cm³), 10% solvent Y (density 0.92 g/cm³), 10% additives (density 1.05 g/cm³). Calculate the **VOC in g/L** and check compliance with EU Directive 2004/42/EC (limit 420 g/L for clearcoats).

### Step 1 — Identify Components
Take a basis of **1 litre of mixed paint (1000 cm³)**.

$$\rho_{mix} = \dfrac{1}{\sum \dfrac{w_i}{\rho_i}}$$

where $w_i$ = weight fraction of each component, $\rho_i$ = density (g/cm³).

### Step 2 — Compute Weight of Each Component
Assume 1 L of paint = 1000 mL. We must first find the density:

$$\dfrac{1}{\rho_{mix}} = \dfrac{0.45}{1.10} + \dfrac{0.35}{0.85} + \dfrac{0.10}{0.92} + \dfrac{0.10}{1.05}$$

$$\dfrac{1}{\rho_{mix}} = 0.4091 + 0.4118 + 0.1087 + 0.0952$$

$$\dfrac{1}{\rho_{mix}} = 1.0248 \text{ cm}^3/\text{g}$$

$$\rho_{mix} = \dfrac{1}{1.0248} = 0.9758 \text{ g/cm}^3$$

So mass of 1 L of paint = **975.8 g**.

### Step 3 — Compute Mass of Solvents (the VOC contributors)
Resin and additives are **non-volatile**. Solvents X and Y are VOC.

$$m_X = 975.8 \times 0.35 = 341.5 \text{ g}$$

$$m_Y = 975.8 \times 0.10 = 97.6 \text{ g}$$

$$m_{VOC} = 341.5 + 97.6 = 439.1 \text{ g}$$

### Step 4 — Express VOC per Litre of Paint
By definition:

$$\text{VOC} = \dfrac{m_{VOC}}{V_{paint}} = \dfrac{439.1 \text{ g}}{1 \text{ L}}$$

$$\boxed{\text{VOC} = 439.1 \text{ g/L}}$$

### Step 5 — Compliance Check
EU limit for clearcoats = **420 g/L**.

Since 439.1 g/L > 420 g/L → **NON-COMPLIANT** with EU Directive 2004/42/EC.

### Step 6 — Reformulation Suggestion
To meet 420 g/L, with same density assumption, max solvent mass allowed:

$$m_{VOC,max} = 420 \text{ g/L} \times 1 \text{ L} = 420 \text{ g}$$

Current VOC = 439.1 g → must reduce by **19.1 g** (≈ 4.4%).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Always show the **basis** (1 L or 1 kg) at the start of any paint calculation. Marks are awarded for clearly stating assumptions.

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Derivation: Theoretical Coverage Area

> **Problem:** Calculate the theoretical coverage of an enamel paint with 55% volume solids, applied to a wet film thickness of 100 µm, on a vehicle body of total area 18 m². How many litres of paint are required?

### Step 1 — Apply Coverage Formula
The relationship between solids, thickness, and coverage is:

$$A = \dfrac{V_{paint} \times S_v}{T_{dry}}$$

where $A$ = area (m²), $V_{paint}$ = paint volume (L), $S_v$ = % volume solids (decimal), $T_{dry}$ = dry film thickness (µm, must convert to consistent units).

### Step 2 — Calculate Dry Film Thickness from Wet
Wet film of 100 µm with 55% solids yields:

$$T_{dry} = WFT \times S_v = 100 \times 0.55 = 55 \text{ µm}$$

### Step 3 — Rearrange for Volume
For a 1 m² area, the dry film volume needed:

$$V_{dry,1m^2} = A \times T_{dry} = 1 \text{ m}^2 \times 55 \text{ µm} = 55 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3$$

Converting to litres: $55 \times 10^{-6} \times 10^3 = 0.055$ L of solids per m².

### Step 4 — Back-Calculate Wet Paint Volume per m²
$$V_{paint,1m^2} = \dfrac{V_{dry,1m^2}}{S_v} = \dfrac{0.055}{0.55} = 0.10 \text{ L/m}^2$$

### Step 5 — Total for 18 m² Vehicle Body
$$V_{total} = 0.10 \times 18 = 1.8 \text{ L}$$

### Step 6 — Account for Application Losses
Spraying losses: 20–30% (overspray, bounce-off). With 25% loss:

$$V_{actual} = \dfrac{1.8}{0.75} = 2.4 \text{ L}$$

$$\boxed{V_{actual} \approx 2.4 \text{ L of enamel paint}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In real OEM shops, the **transfer efficiency** of an electrostatic bell is **> 90%**, while conventional air spray is only **30–50%**. This is why modern plants use **rotary bell atomizers**.

## 3.4 Curing Kinetics — Simplified Crosslink Model

The crosslinking rate for a thermoset paint follows **first-order kinetics** with respect to unreacted functional groups:

$$\dfrac{d\alpha}{dt} = k (1 - \alpha)$$

where $\alpha$ = degree of cure (0 → 1), $k$ = rate constant.

Solving the differential equation with $\alpha = 0$ at $t = 0$:

$$\alpha(t) = 1 - e^{-kt}$$

The rate constant follows the **Arrhenius equation**:

$$k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}$$

where $A$ = pre-exponential factor, $E_a$ = activation energy, $R$ = 8.314 J/(mol·K), $T$ = absolute temperature (K).

For an OEM clearcoat with $E_a = 35$ kJ/mol, $A = 1.5 \times 10^4$ min⁻¹, at 160°C (433 K):

$$k = 1.5 \times 10^4 \cdot e^{-35000/(8.314 \times 433)}$$

$$k = 1.5 \times 10^4 \cdot e^{-9.72}$$

$$k = 1.5 \times 10^4 \times 6.04 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$k \approx 0.906 \text{ min}^{-1}$$

For 90% cure ($\alpha = 0.9$):

$$0.9 = 1 - e^{-0.906 \cdot t}$$

$$t = -\dfrac{\ln(0.1)}{0.906} = \dfrac{2.303}{0.906} \approx 2.54 \text{ min}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is why OEM oven **dwell time at 160°C metal temperature is typically 20–30 minutes** — it allows for heat-up lag (ramp time) plus this 2.5 min for full chemistry completion.

Fix: Increase thixotropy, reduce WFT"]

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**Differentiate between thermoplastic and thermosetting paints. Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Thermoplastic Paint | Thermosetting Paint |
|---|---|---|
| **Curing** | Solvent evaporation only (physical) | Chemical crosslinking (chemical) |
| **Reversibility** | Reversible (re-dissolves) | Irreversible (crosslinked network) |
| **Heat resistance** | Low | High |
| **Solvent resistance** | Poor | Excellent |
| **Examples** | Nitrocellulose lacquer, Acrylic lacquer, PVC | Alkyd enamel, 2K PU, Acrylic-melamine, Epoxy |

**Examples (KTU expects both categories):**
- *Thermoplastic*: Nitrocellulose lacquer, Acrylic lacquer
- *Thermosetting*: 2K Polyurethane, Alkyd-melamine enamel, Epoxy primer

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**List the four main components of automotive paint and state the function of each.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Binder (Resin)** — *Function*: Forms the continuous film; binds pigment; provides adhesion, flexibility, hardness, and chemical resistance.
2. **Pigment** — *Function*: Provides colour, opacity, and special effects (metallic, pearlescent); also contributes to UV and corrosion resistance.
3. **Solvent (Carrier)** — *Function*: Reduces viscosity to sprayable level; evaporates after application to leave a dry film.
4. **Additives** — *Function*: Modify specific properties such as flow (rheology modifier), drying (catalyst), UV protection (HALS), and surface tension (wetting agent).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 4 Internal Choice]**

**(a)** Classify automotive paints based on **resin chemistry**. Explain in detail the composition, characteristics, advantages, and applications of **2K Polyurethane paint** for vehicle body applications. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** With a neat sketch, describe the **multi-layer paint film architecture** used in modern OEM automotive painting. State the typical thickness and primary function of each layer. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for Q-A(a):

**Classification by Resin Chemistry (2 marks):**
- Nitrocellulose / Acrylic lacquer (thermoplastic)
- Alkyd enamel (oxidative)
- Acrylic enamel (thermoset, with melamine)
- 2K Polyurethane
- Epoxy (primer)
- Polyester (powder coating)

**2K Polyurethane — Detailed Analysis (5 marks):**

**Composition:**
- **Part A (Resin)**: Hydroxyl-functional acrylic or polyester polyol (60–70% solids in solvent).
- **Part B (Hardener)**: Aliphatic isocyanate (HDI — Hexamethylene Diisocyanate, biuret or trimer form).
- Mix ratio typically **4:1 or 3:1 by volume** before spraying.

**Crosslinking Reaction:**

$$\text{R-OH} + \text{O=C=N-R'} \longrightarrow \text{R-O-CO-NH-R'}$$

The reaction is **moisture-sensitive** (water reacts with NCO to release CO₂, causing bubbles — controlled in refinish booths with humidity 50–70%).

**Characteristics (Key Table for KTU):**

| Characteristic | Value / Behaviour |
|---|---|
| Pot life | 2–4 hours at 20°C |
| Dust-free | 8–12 min |
| Touch-dry | 25–40 min |
| Through-dry | 4–8 hours |
| Full cure | 5–7 days |
| Hardness | 2H–4H pencil |
| Gloss @ 60° | 92–98 GU |
| Chemical resistance | Excellent (fuel, brake fluid, acids) |
| UV resistance | Excellent (aliphatic isocyanate) |
| Flexibility | Excellent — suitable for plastic bumpers |

**Advantages:**
- High gloss, excellent DOI
- Outstanding chemical and abrasion resistance
- Ambient or low-bake cure (60–80°C) — **saves energy in refinish**
- Excellent colour retention under UV
- Compatible with OEM colour-tint systems

**Applications:**
- **OEM clearcoat** in premium vehicles (Mercedes, BMW)
- **Refinish/collision repair** (gold standard)
- **Aerospace topcoats**
- **Plastic bumper coatings** (flexible versions)

#### Model Solution for Q-A(b):

**Sketch (4 marks):**
(See Section 4.1 diagram — paint film architecture from substrate to clearcoat)

**Layer Table (3 marks):**

| Layer | Function | Thickness (µm) | Curing |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Substrate (CRS / GI / Al)** | Base structural material | 650–800 | — |
| **Phosphate / Bonderite** | Conversion coating for adhesion | 1–3 µm of zinc phosphate crystals | Room temp |
| **Cathodic E-coat** | Anti-corrosion, primer | 18–25 µm | 175–185°C, 20 min |
| **Anti-chip / PVC sealer** | Stone impact protection (underbody, arches) | 100–500 µm | 140°C |
| **Primer / Surfacer** | Surface levelling, colour uniformity | 25–35 µm | 140–160°C |
| **Basecoat** | Colour, metallic/mica effect | 12–25 µm | 80°C flash + 140°C with clearcoat |
| **Clearcoat** | Gloss, UV, scratch, chemical resistance | 35–45 µm | 140–160°C, 20–30 min |

**Total dry film thickness: ~100–130 µm.**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4 Internal Choice]**

**(a)** Explain the **manufacturing process of automotive paint** with a flow diagram. Discuss the quality control tests performed on finished paint. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Compare **lacquer, enamel, and 2K polyurethane** paints in terms of composition, drying mechanism, properties, and applications. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for Q-B(a):

**Manufacturing Process (4 marks):** 
(Following the flow diagram in Section 4.2)

1. **Raw material receipt & QC** — Resin, pigment, solvent, additives checked against TDS (Technical Data Sheet). Pigment moisture < 0.5%.
2. **Pre-mixing** — Pigment dispersed in part of the resin + solvent under high-speed cowles blade (1500 rpm, 30–45 min).
3. **Milling/Grinding** — Sand mill or bead mill reduces pigment agglomerates to < 10 µm. Hegman gauge reading > 7.
4. **Let-down** — Remaining resin and solvent added slowly in low-shear mixer to avoid re-agglomeration.
5. **Adjustment** — Viscosity adjusted to TDS value (Ford Cup #4: 18–25 s). Colour matched with spectrophotometer (ΔE < 0.5 vs. standard).
6. **Filtration** — 25 µm bag filter to remove oversize particles.
7. **Filling & Packaging** — Tin cans / HDPE containers; nitrogen blanket for sensitive products; shelf-life 24–36 months.

**Quality Control Tests (3 marks):**

| Test | Standard | Specification |
|---|---|---|
| Viscosity | ASTM D1200 | 18–25 s Ford Cup #4 @ 20°C |
| Specific gravity | ASTM D1475 | 0.95–1.20 g/cm³ |
| Hegman fineness | ASTM D1210 | ≥ 7 (i.e., < 10 µm) |
| Colour match | Spectrophotometer | ΔE < 0.5 |
| Gloss | ASTM D523 | > 90 GU @ 60° |
| Drying time | ASTM D1640 | Per TDS |
| Adhesion | ISO 2409 | Class 0 |
| Hardness | ASTM D3363 | > 2H pencil |
| Solids content | ASTM D2369 | Per TDS ± 2% |

#### Model Solution for Q-B(b):

**Comparison Table (Full 7 marks):**

| Property | Nitrocellulose Lacquer | Alkyd Enamel | 2K Polyurethane |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Binder** | Nitrocellulose / thermoplastic acrylic | Alkyd resin (oil-modified polyester) | Polyol + isocyanate (2-pack) |
| **Solvent** | Esters, ketones, aromatics | White spirit / mineral turpentine | Esters, aromatics (low VOC versions available) |
| **Drying mechanism** | Solvent evaporation (physical) | Oxidative polymerization (chemical) | Polyol-NCO crosslink (chemical) |
| **Drying temperature** | Ambient (20–25°C) | Ambient | Ambient to 80°C |
| **Drying time** | 5–10 min dust-free | 30–60 min dust-free | 8–12 min dust-free |
| **Re-coatability** | Excellent (re-dissolves previous coat) | Excellent | Requires sanding |
| **Gloss** | 85–90 GU | 80–88 GU | 92–98 GU |
| **Hardness** | HB–H | F–H | 2H–4H |
| **Chemical resistance** | Poor | Moderate | Excellent |
| **Durability** | Poor (yellows, cracks) | Moderate (oxidative) | Excellent (10+ years) |
| **VOC** | High (600–750 g/L) | Medium (400–500 g/L) | Medium (350–500 g/L) |
| **OEM use** | None today | Historical (1960s) | Yes — premium / refinish |
| **Application method** | Spray (multiple coats, polish) | Brush, spray | Spray (after mixing) |
| **Cost** | Low | Low | High |
| **Advantages** | Fast dry, easy repair, polish to high gloss | Cheap, simple, brushable | Best-in-class durability, gloss, chemical resistance |
| **Disadvantages** | Poor chemical & UV resistance | Slow cure, moderate durability | Pot life, NCO health hazard, mixing required |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Mark Deduction Zones:**
> 1. **Confusing lacquer with enamel** in short answers — a *very* common error. Lacquer = **thermoplastic + solvent dry**; Enamel = **thermoset + oxidative/chemical cure**. 1 mark lost per confusion.
> 2. **Forgetting VOC units (g/L)** in numerical problems — full marks require **EU limit reference value** mentioned.
> 3. **Not stating resin type** for each paint — KTU 2024 scheme specifically tests "resin chemistry-based classification". Saying "Acrylic paint" without specifying "acrylic lacquer" or "acrylic-melamine" loses 2 marks.
> 4. **Skipping the crosslink reaction equation** in 2K PU questions — the isocyanate-polyol equation is **mandatory** for full marks.
> 5. **Listing powder coating as "solvent-based"** — powder coating is **100% solids, zero VOC**, applied electrostatically. A common error.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Board Revision — Memorize the Following:**

### Quick-Fire Facts
- **2K PU** = 2-pack polyurethane, polyol + isocyanate, **gold standard for refinish**.
- **Lacquer** = thermoplastic, solvent evaporation, **reversible** (re-dissolves), poor chemical resistance.
- **Enamel** = thermoset, oxidative cure (alkyd) or bake (acrylic-melamine).
- **E-coat** = electrodeposition primer, **18–25 µm**, anti-corrosion.
- **Clearcoat** = **35–45 µm**, 2K PU or acrylic-melamine, provides gloss & UV.
- **Total paint film** = **~100–130 µm** total dry film.
- **OEM cure** = **140–160°C metal temp, 20–30 min**.
- **VOC limit (EU 2004/42/EC)** for clearcoats = **420 g/L**.
- **Pot life of 2K PU** = **2–4 hrs @ 20°C**.
- **Gloss standard** = **> 90 GU @ 60°** for premium OEM.
- **Hardness standard** = **> 2H pencil** (Wolff-Wilborn).
- **Adhesion standard** = **ISO 2409 Class 0**.
- **Stone-chip standard** = **SAE J400** rating.
- **Hegman gauge** = **≥ 7** means particle size **< 10 µm**.

### Paint Selection Memory Aid
- **OEM manufacturing** → E-coat + water-borne basecoat + 2K/solvent clearcoat
- **Crash repair** → 2K PU full system
- **Plastic bumpers** → Flexible 2K PU (low-bake)
- **Chassis** → E-coat + PVC underbody
- **EV battery housing** → Powder coating (dielectric + thermal)

### Four Mandatory Components
**Resin + Pigment + Solvent + Additives** — no paint has fewer.

### Three Curing Mechanisms
1. **Physical (solvent evaporation)** — lacquer
2. **Oxidative** — alkyd enamel
3. **Chemical crosslink** — 2K PU, acrylic-melamine, epoxy

### One Equation to Remember
$$\text{R-OH} + \text{O=C=N-R'} \longrightarrow \text{R-O-CO-NH-R'}$$
**Isocyanate-Polyol Urethane Linkage** (the heart of 2K PU chemistry).

### Coverage Formula
$$A = \dfrac{V_{paint} \times S_v}{T_{dry}} \times \eta_{transfer}$$
where $\eta_{transfer}$ = transfer efficiency (30% air spray, 90% electrostatic bell).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Painting Methods and Techniques - Spraying
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT413 - VEHICLE BODY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Painting Methods and Techniques - Spraying

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Spraying** is a paint application technique used in vehicle body engineering in which atomized paint particles are projected under pressure onto a prepared surface to form a uniform, continuous, and adherent coating. The atomization breaks the liquid paint into fine droplets, which are then directed toward the substrate using a pressurized gas stream (typically compressed air) or through hydraulic/mechanical means without air.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Spraying is a method of paint application where paint is atomized into a fine mist by means of compressed air, hydraulic pressure, or electrostatic forces, and deposited on the vehicle body surface to obtain a smooth, glossy, and durable finish.

The process is governed by fluid mechanics principles, particularly the **Bernoulli equation** and the principle of **shear-induced atomization**, where the kinetic energy of the propellant overcomes the surface tension of the paint film.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of spraying paint exactly like **using a garden hose with a mist nozzle**:

- **Without the nozzle (no atomization):** Water comes out as a thick, uneven stream — the surface gets flooded in spots and missed in others. This is what happens if paint is poured or brushed carelessly.
- **With the mist nozzle (atomized spray):** Water breaks into millions of tiny droplets that distribute themselves evenly over a wide area, gently covering leaves and grass without damaging them. This is exactly what a paint spray gun does — it converts bulk liquid paint into a fine, controlled mist that "hugs" the contours of the car body.

Similarly, when you spray perfume from a bottle, the pump creates a pressure differential that breaks the liquid into a fine cloud, allowing even, minimal coverage. Vehicle paint spraying operates on the same principle, but at industrial scale, with precise control over droplet size, velocity, and electrostatic attraction.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Standard atomization pressure for conventional air spray:** **2.5 – 4.0 bar (250 – 400 kPa)**
- **Standard viscosity for spray application:** **18 – 25 seconds (Ford Cup No. 4) at 27 °C**
- **Recommended dry film thickness (DFT) per coat:** **20 – 35 micrometers (µm)**
- **Typical spray booth airflow:** **0.3 – 0.5 m/s**
- **Standard booth temperature:** **20 – 25 °C**
- **Relative humidity in booth:** **60 – 70 %**

> [!NOTE]
> These values are **industry benchmarks** used globally by automotive OEMs (Original Equipment Manufacturers) and are testable in KTU 2024 Scheme examinations under the CO (Course Outcome) mapping of vehicle finishing processes.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Spraying Methods

Vehicle body spraying is broadly classified into the following categories based on the atomization mechanism:

### 2.1.1 Conventional Air Spray (Compressed Air Spray)
- Uses **compressed air at 2.5 – 4.0 bar** to atomize paint.
- The high-velocity air stream shears the liquid paint stream into fine droplets.
- **Transfer Efficiency (TE):** approximately **30 – 40 %**.
- High overspray and wastage — considered environmentally inefficient.

### 2.1.2 Airless Spray (Hydraulic Spray)
- Paint is pressurized to **70 – 200 bar** through a small orifice (typically **0.2 – 0.5 mm**).
- Atomization occurs due to the sudden pressure drop, not air.
- Suitable for high-viscosity paints and primers.
- **Transfer Efficiency:** approximately **50 – 60 %**.

### 2.1.3 HVLP (High Volume Low Pressure)
- Air cap pressure is limited to **0.7 bar (10 psi) maximum** at the air cap.
- High volume of air at low pressure creates soft atomization.
- **Transfer Efficiency:** approximately **65 – 75 %** (exceeds EPA compliance of 65 %).
- Industry standard for automotive refinishing.

### 2.1.4 Electrostatic Spray
- Paint droplets are charged to **30,000 – 100,000 volts (DC)**.
- The grounded vehicle body attracts the charged droplets (Coulomb's Law).
- **Transfer Efficiency:** approximately **80 – 95 %** (highest among all methods).
- Wrap-around effect — paint reaches recessed areas.

### 2.1.5 Rotary Atomizer (Bell / Disk)
- Used in robotic automotive paint lines.
- Paint is fed to a rotating bell rotating at **20,000 – 70,000 rpm**.
- Centrifugal force atomizes the paint.
- **Transfer Efficiency:** approximately **80 – 90 %**.

## 2.2 Operating Logic — Step-by-Step

The spraying process is governed by the following sequential logic:

1. **Paint Preparation:** Paint is thinned to spray viscosity (**18 – 25 s Ford Cup 4**) using the appropriate reducer.
2. **Pressure Regulation:** Compressed air (or hydraulic fluid) is regulated to the required working pressure.
3. **Atomization:** The propellant (air or hydraulic pressure) overcomes the paint's **surface tension** (typically **0.03 – 0.05 N/m** for automotive paints).
4. **Droplet Transport:** Atomized droplets travel at a velocity of **15 – 25 m/s** toward the substrate.
5. **Deposition:** Droplets impact the surface, coalesce, and flow to form a continuous film.
6. **Film Formation:** Solvent evaporates, leaving behind a uniform dry film of **20 – 35 µm** thickness.
7. **Curing:** Film is cured in an oven at **140 – 160 °C** for **20 – 30 minutes** (for OEM bake finishes).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula / Definition | Symbol | Unit | Typical Value |
|---|-----------|---------------------|--------|------|---------------|
| 1 | Transfer Efficiency (TE) | $TE = \dfrac{W_{deposited}}{W_{sprayed}} \times 100$ | TE | % | 30 – 95 % |
| 2 | Spray Rate (Q) | $Q = C_d \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \Delta P}{\rho}}$ | Q | m³/s | varies |
| 3 | Coverage (m²/L) | $Cov = \dfrac{10 \cdot dft}{\rho_{wet film}}$ | Cov | m²/L | 8 – 12 |
| 4 | Wet Film Thickness | $WFT = \dfrac{DFT \times \rho_{solid}}{V_{solid}}$ | WFT | µm | 50 – 80 |
| 5 | Film Build Ratio | $FBR = \dfrac{DFT}{WFT} \times 100$ | FBR | % | 40 – 60 |
| 6 | Spray Gun Distance | Empirical (manufacturer spec) | d | mm | 150 – 250 |
| 7 | Atomization Air Pressure | Empirical / OEM spec | P | bar | 2.5 – 4.0 |
| 8 | Viscosity (Dynamic) | $\mu = \dfrac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}}$ | µ | Pa·s | 0.05 – 0.20 |
| 9 | Reynolds Number (Nozzle) | $Re = \dfrac{\rho v D}{\mu}$ | Re | dimensionless | > 10⁴ (turbulent) |
| 10 | Weber Number (Atomization) | $We = \dfrac{\rho v^2 D}{\sigma}$ | We | dimensionless | > 12 (atomized) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Symbols in formulas:**
> - $W_{deposited}$ = weight of paint reaching the surface
> - $W_{sprayed}$ = total weight of paint discharged
> - $C_d$ = discharge coefficient (typically 0.6 – 0.9)
> - $A$ = nozzle orifice area (m²)
> - $\Delta P$ = pressure differential (Pa)
> - $\rho$ = paint density (kg/m³)
> - $dft$ = dry film thickness (µm)
> - $\sigma$ = surface tension (N/m)
> - $D$ = nozzle diameter (m)

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Spraying technology is critical in:
- **OEM Automotive Production Lines:** Robotic electrostatic rotary bell systems apply primer, basecoat, and clearcoat.
- **Collision Repair & Refinishing:** HVLP guns are mandated by EPA Rule 1151 (USA) and analogous Indian CPCB norms.
- **Two-Wheeler & Commercial Vehicle Manufacturing:** Airless systems dominate for primers on truck chassis.
- **Aerospace and Railway Coaches:** HVLP and electrostatic systems are used for corrosion-resistant coatings.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Transfer Efficiency (TE) by Mass Balance

The Transfer Efficiency is the most important performance metric in any spray painting operation. It is derived directly from the **conservation of mass principle**.

### Step 1: Define the Mass Balance

When paint is sprayed from a gun, the total mass discharged equals the mass that deposits on the substrate plus the mass lost to overspray, bounce-off, and evaporation.

$$
M_{sprayed} = M_{deposited} + M_{overspray} + M_{bounce} + M_{evap}
$$

**Textual Explanation:** The total mass of paint exiting the spray gun must be conserved; whatever does not land on the vehicle is either lost as airborne mist (overspray), rebounds from the surface (bounce), or evaporates before deposition.

### Step 2: Rearrange to Express Useful Deposition

$$
M_{deposited} = M_{sprayed} - (M_{overspray} + M_{bounce} + M_{evap})
$$

**Textual Explanation:** The useful mass is the sprayed mass minus all losses. This is the practical mass that actually contributes to the protective film on the body.

### Step 3: Define TE as a Ratio

$$
TE = \frac{M_{deposited}}{M_{sprayed}} \times 100
$$

**Textual Explanation:** Transfer Efficiency is the ratio (in percentage) of useful paint deposited to total paint sprayed. A TE of 100 % would mean zero loss, which is physically impossible.

### Step 4: Substitute the Mass Balance

$$
TE = \frac{M_{sprayed} - (M_{overspray} + M_{bounce} + M_{evap})}{M_{sprayed}} \times 100
$$

**Textual Explanation:** This is the operational form. By measuring the dry weight gain of the panel before and after spraying, and weighing the gun before and after, $TE$ can be directly computed.

## 3.2 Derivation: Theoretical Spray Rate (Volumetric Flow)

The volumetric flow rate of paint through a nozzle is governed by **Torricelli's theorem** (a special case of Bernoulli's equation).

### Step 1: Apply Bernoulli's Equation Across the Nozzle

$$
P_{in} + \frac{1}{2}\rho v_{in}^2 + \rho g h_{in} = P_{out} + \frac{1}{2}\rho v_{out}^2 + \rho g h_{out}
$$

**Textual Explanation:** Bernoulli's principle states that for an incompressible, non-viscous fluid, the sum of pressure energy, kinetic energy, and potential energy is constant along a streamline.

### Step 2: Simplify (Horizontal Nozzle, Negligible Inlet Velocity)

For a horizontal nozzle ($h_{in} = h_{out}$) and assuming $v_{in} \approx 0$:

$$
P_{in} = P_{out} + \frac{1}{2}\rho v_{out}^2
$$

**Textual Explanation:** The pressure energy at the inlet is fully converted into kinetic energy at the nozzle exit, since the nozzle is horizontal and the inlet flow is much slower than the exit jet.

### Step 3: Solve for Exit Velocity

$$
v_{out} = \sqrt{\frac{2(P_{in} - P_{out})}{\rho}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta P}{\rho}}
$$

**Textual Explanation:** This is the classical Torricelli result: the exit velocity equals the square root of twice the pressure drop divided by the fluid density.

### Step 4: Compute Volumetric Flow Rate

$$
Q = C_d \cdot A \cdot v_{out} = C_d \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta P}{\rho}}
$$

**Textual Explanation:** The actual flow rate is the theoretical velocity multiplied by the nozzle area and a discharge coefficient $C_d$ (which accounts for vena contracta and viscous losses). Typical $C_d$ values for spray nozzles range from **0.6 to 0.9**.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A vehicle body requires 80 g of dry paint film per square meter. The paint has 50 % solids by volume and density 1100 kg/m³. The transfer efficiency of the spray gun is 70 %. Calculate:
(a) Wet film thickness required per coat
(b) Paint consumption per car (assume surface area = 25 m²)

### Part (a): Wet Film Thickness

$$
WFT = \frac{DFT}{V_{solid}} = \frac{20 \, \mu m}{0.50} = 40 \, \mu m
$$

**Textual Explanation:** Since only 50 % of the wet paint becomes solid film, the wet film must be twice as thick as the desired dry film (20 µm DFT).

### Part (b): Paint Consumption

Wet paint mass per m²:

$$
m_{wet} = \frac{DFT \times \rho}{V_{solid}} = \frac{20 \times 10^{-6} \times 1100}{0.50} = 0.044 \, kg/m^2
$$

**Textual Explanation:** The wet paint mass per unit area is the dry film mass divided by the volume fraction of solids.

Accounting for 70 % transfer efficiency (i.e., 30 % is wasted):

$$
m_{consumed} = \frac{0.044}{0.70} = 0.0629 \, kg/m^2
$$

**Textual Explanation:** To actually deliver 0.044 kg/m² on the body, we must spray more paint to compensate for the 30 % lost to overspray and bounce.

For a 25 m² car body:

$$
M_{car} = 0.0629 \times 25 = 1.572 \, kg
$$

**Textual Explanation:** The total wet paint consumption per car is approximately **1.57 kg** per coat of 20 µm DFT at 70 % TE.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Spraying Methods

| Method | Atomization Mechanism | Typical TE (%) | Air Cap Pressure (bar) | Best Application | Limitation |
|--------|-----------------------|----------------|------------------------|------------------|------------|
| Conventional Air | Compressed air shear | 30 – 40 | 2.5 – 4.0 | Small touch-ups | High overspray |
| Airless | Hydraulic pressure | 50 – 60 | 70 – 200 (paint) | Primers, chassis | Poor finish quality |
| HVLP | Low-pressure high-volume air | 65 – 75 | 0.4 – 0.7 | Refinishing, basecoat | Lower productivity |
| Electrostatic | Coulomb attraction | 80 – 95 | 2.0 – 3.0 | OEM robotic lines | Conductive paint needed |
| Rotary Bell | Centrifugal force | 80 – 90 | N/A (rpm 20k–70k) | High-volume OEM | High capital cost |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Spray Painting Process Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Paint Supply] --> B[Paint Mixing Room]
    B --> C[Viscosity Check 18-25 s]
    C --> D{Quality OK?}
    D -- No --> B
    D -- Yes --> E[Paint Pot or Reservoir]
    E --> F[Spray Gun Assembly]
    G[Compressed Air 2.5-4 bar] --> F
    F --> H[Atomization Chamber]
    H --> I[Paint Mist Generation]
    I --> J[Vehicle Body Substrate]
    J --> K[Wet Film Deposition]
    K --> L[Flash-Off Zone 5-10 min]
    L --> M[Bake Oven 140-160 C]
    M --> N[Cured Film Inspection]
    N --> O{Quality Pass?}
    O -- No --> P[Sand and Repaint]
    O -- Yes --> Q[Final Assembly]
    P --> E
```

**Textual Explanation of the Flow:**
- Paint is mixed, viscosity-checked, and loaded into the spray gun.
- Compressed air atomizes the paint into fine droplets.
- The mist is deposited onto the vehicle body.
- Wet film is allowed to flash off, then baked in an oven.
- Final inspection determines if the part passes or requires rework.

## 4.2 Spray Gun Internal Architecture (Mermaid Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Air Inlet 1] --> B[Air Cap Assembly]
    C[Paint Inlet] --> D[Paint Nozzle]
    E[Trigger Mechanism] --> F[Fluid Needle Valve]
    B --> G[Atomization Zone]
    D --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[Atomized Spray Pattern]
    H --> I[Vehicle Body Surface]
    A2[Pattern Air Control] --> B
    A3[Fluid Flow Control] --> F
    A4[Air Pressure Regulator 2.5-4 bar] --> A
```

**Textual Explanation:**
- The spray gun has three fluid inputs: main atomization air, pattern air (for fan width), and paint fluid.
- A trigger-actuated needle valve precisely controls paint flow.
- The air cap design dictates the spray pattern (round, fan, or cone).

## 4.3 Electrostatic Spray Operational Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Paint Reservoir] --> B[High Voltage Power Supply 30k-100k V DC]
    B --> C[Charging Electrode at Spray Bell]
    D[Rotary Bell Motor 20k-70k rpm] --> C
    C --> E[Negatively Charged Paint Droplets]
    E --> F[Grounded Vehicle Body Positive]
    F --> G[Wrap-Around Deposition]
    G --> H[Cured Film on Hidden Surfaces]
```

**Textual Explanation:**
- The high-voltage power supply negatively charges the paint droplets at the rotating bell.
- The grounded vehicle body acts as a positive attractor.
- The electrostatic field lines cause the charged droplets to "wrap" around the body, coating even recessed areas like door jambs and under-carriage regions.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Spraying. List the various types of spraying methods used in vehicle body painting.
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Spraying is a paint application method in which liquid paint is atomized into fine droplets using compressed air, hydraulic pressure, or electrostatic forces and projected onto the vehicle body surface to form a uniform, adherent coating.

**Types of Spraying Methods (2 marks):**
1. Conventional Air Spray
2. Airless (Hydraulic) Spray
3. HVLP (High Volume Low Pressure) Spray
4. Electrostatic Spray
5. Rotary Atomizer (Bell/Disk) Spray

---

### Question 2: What is Transfer Efficiency? State the typical TE values for HVLP and electrostatic spray methods.
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Transfer Efficiency (TE) is the ratio of the mass of paint actually deposited on the substrate to the total mass of paint sprayed, expressed as a percentage.

$$
TE = \frac{W_{deposited}}{W_{sprayed}} \times 100
$$

**Typical TE values (1.5 marks):**
- **HVLP Spray:** 65 – 75 %
- **Electrostatic Spray:** 80 – 95 %

---

## 5.2 Part B: Extended Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A: Describe in detail the conventional air spray painting process. Explain its working principle, components, operating parameters, advantages, and limitations.
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

#### Part (a) — Working Principle and Components [7 marks]

**Working Principle (4 marks):**
In the conventional air spray process, paint is supplied to the spray gun under low pressure (typically **0.5 – 1.5 bar** from a paint pot or pressure feed tank). Compressed air at **2.5 – 4.0 bar** is introduced through the air cap, which surrounds the fluid nozzle. The high-velocity air stream creates a low-pressure region (as per Bernoulli's principle) at the nozzle tip, which draws the paint out and simultaneously shears the liquid stream into fine droplets — a process called **atomization**.

The atomized droplets form a conical or fan-shaped spray pattern and are deposited on the vehicle body surface. As droplets impact the surface, they coalesce, flow, and form a continuous wet film, which then flashes off solvents and cures to form a dry film of **20 – 35 µm** thickness.

**Main Components of a Conventional Air Spray Gun (3 marks):**
1. **Air Cap:** Directs the atomization and pattern air jets to create the spray pattern (round, fan, or wide).
2. **Fluid Nozzle (Tip):** Meters the paint flow; available in sizes 0.8 – 2.0 mm.
3. **Fluid Needle Valve:** Actuated by the trigger to start/stop paint flow precisely.
4. **Air Valve:** Controls atomization air supply.
5. **Pattern Air Control Knob:** Adjusts the fan width of the spray pattern.
6. **Fluid Flow Control Knob:** Regulates paint volume.
7. **Trigger:** Combined control for air and paint.

#### Part (b) — Operating Parameters, Advantages, and Limitations [7 marks]

**Operating Parameters (3 marks):**
- **Atomization air pressure:** 2.5 – 4.0 bar
- **Spray gun distance from surface:** 150 – 250 mm
- **Paint viscosity:** 18 – 25 s (Ford Cup 4) at 27 °C
- **Spray gun travel speed:** 0.5 – 1.0 m/s
- **Overlap between passes:** 50 %
- **Fluid nozzle size:** 1.0 – 1.4 mm for basecoats
- **DFT per coat:** 20 – 35 µm

**Advantages (2 marks):**
- Excellent finish quality with high gloss and smoothness.
- Suitable for metallic and pearlescent basecoats.
- Low capital cost and easy maintenance.
- Flexible for both small repair work and OEM applications.

**Limitations (2 marks):**
- Low transfer efficiency (30 – 40 %).
- High overspray leads to paint wastage and environmental pollution.
- High air consumption (250 – 450 L/min).
- Not compliant with modern VOC (Volatile Organic Compound) regulations.
- Requires skilled operator for consistent results.

---

### Question B: Explain the Electrostatic Spray Painting method. Discuss its working principle, advantages, and the factors affecting transfer efficiency.
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

#### Part (a) — Working Principle and Setup [7 marks]

**Working Principle (4 marks):**
In electrostatic spray painting, paint droplets are given a high negative electrical charge (**30,000 – 100,000 V DC**) as they exit the spray gun or rotary bell. The vehicle body is electrically **grounded** (acts as the positive electrode). The potential difference between the charged droplets and the grounded body creates an electrostatic field (governed by **Coulomb's Law**):

$$
F = k_e \cdot \frac{q_1 q_2}{r^2}
$$

where $k_e$ is Coulomb's constant, $q_1$ and $q_2$ are the charges, and $r$ is the separation distance. The charged droplets are attracted along the field lines toward the body, dramatically improving deposition efficiency.

The "**wrap-around effect**" is a unique feature: the field lines curve around the body, allowing paint to reach recessed areas such as door jambs, bonnet undersides, and B-pillars without direct line-of-sight spray.

**System Setup (3 marks):**
1. **High-Voltage Power Supply:** Provides 30 – 100 kV DC safely (current limited to < 1 mA for human safety).
2. **Charging Electrode:** Usually the outer edge of the rotating bell or a wire electrode at the gun tip.
3. **Rotary Bell Atomizer:** Rotates at 20,000 – 70,000 rpm to atomize paint by centrifugal force.
4. **Grounded Vehicle Body:** Connected to earth through the conveyor or fixtures.
5. **Insulated Paint Supply Lines:** Prevent charge leakage from the paint.
6. **Ventilation & Fire Safety:** Critical due to flammable solvent vapors.

#### Part (b) — Advantages and Factors Affecting Transfer Efficiency [7 marks]

**Advantages (3 marks):**
- Very high transfer efficiency: **80 – 95 %** — minimizes paint wastage.
- Superior wrap-around effect: coats hidden and recessed surfaces.
- Reduced VOC emissions — environmentally compliant.
- Uniform film thickness with minimal orange peel.
- Lower operating cost per car (reduced paint consumption by 30 – 60 %).
- Faster line speed — higher productivity in OEM plants.

**Factors Affecting Transfer Efficiency (4 marks):**
1. **Voltage Level:** Higher voltage (up to 100 kV) increases electrostatic attraction but risks insulation breakdown.
2. **Paint Conductivity:** Must be in the range **0.05 – 2.0 megohm-cm** for optimal charge retention; too high or too low reduces TE.
3. **Droplet Size:** Smaller droplets (10 – 50 µm) carry better charge-to-mass ratio and improve TE.
4. **Spray Distance:** Optimal distance is **200 – 300 mm**; too far reduces field strength.
5. **Booth Humidity & Temperature:** Affects charge dissipation; ideally 60 – 70 % RH and 20 – 25 °C.
6. **Grounding Quality:** Poor grounding drastically reduces TE.
7. **Conveyor Speed:** Must be synchronized with deposition rate.
8. **Paint Viscosity:** Affects atomization quality and droplet charge retention.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing "airless" with "no air"** — Airless spray still uses air for the spray gun's auxiliary controls; atomization is hydraulic. State this clearly to earn full marks.
> 2. **Forgetting to define units** — Always mention **bar, µm, m/s, °C** with numerical values; examiners explicitly allocate 0.5 marks for units.
> 3. **Missing the wrap-around effect** in electrostatic spray answers — it is a unique advantage worth **1 – 2 marks**.
> 4. **Writing TE only as a number** — Always include the **formula** $TE = \dfrac{W_{deposited}}{W_{sprayed}} \times 100$ before stating values.
> 5. **Confusing WFT and DFT** — Remember: **WFT > DFT**; the ratio is governed by **% solids by volume**, not weight.
> 6. **Skipping safety in electrostatic spray** — Always mention that current is limited to < 1 mA to prevent electric shock.
> 7. **Not mentioning EPA / CPCB compliance** for HVLP — regulators mandate ≥ 65 % TE, an easy **1-mark** point.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Spraying** is the dominant paint application method in vehicle body engineering, chosen for its finish quality and productivity.
- **Five main types:** Conventional Air, Airless, HVLP, Electrostatic, and Rotary Bell.
- **Transfer Efficiency (TE)** is the key performance metric: Conventional ~30–40 %, Airless ~50–60 %, HVLP ~65–75 %, Electrostatic ~80–95 %.
- **HVLP** is the global regulatory standard for refinishing (EPA Rule 1151, CPCB norms).
- **Electrostatic spray** uses Coulomb's Law ($F = k_e q_1 q_2 / r^2$) to attract charged paint to the grounded body.
- **Atomization** occurs when propellant kinetic energy exceeds the paint's **surface tension** (~0.03 – 0.05 N/m).
- **Spray rate** is governed by Torricelli's theorem: $Q = C_d A \sqrt{2 \Delta P / \rho}$.
- **DFT per coat:** 20 – 35 µm; **WFT** = DFT / (Volume % Solids).
- **Spray gun distance:** 150 – 250 mm; **atomization pressure:** 2.5 – 4.0 bar (conventional).
- **Booth conditions:** 20 – 25 °C, 60 – 70 % RH, airflow 0.3 – 0.5 m/s.
- **Electrostatic voltage:** 30 – 100 kV DC; current limited to < 1 mA for safety.
- **Rotary bell speed:** 20,000 – 70,000 rpm for OEM robotic paint lines.
- **Wrap-around effect** is unique to electrostatic spray and coats recessed body areas.
- **Viscosity specification** for spray application: 18 – 25 s Ford Cup No. 4 at 27 °C.
- **Curing:** OEM bake at 140 – 160 °C for 20 – 30 minutes; refinishing air-dry or low-bake.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Immersion, Painting Equipment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT413 - VEHICLE BODY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Immersion, Painting Equipment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Immersion Coating (Dip Coating) — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Immersion Coating (Dip Coating)** is a *wet-application surface-engineering process* in which a fabricated automotive body shell, sub-assembly, or individual panel is **fully submerged into a tank of liquid coating material** (primer, anti-corrosion paint, PVC under-body sealant, or phosphate conversion solution) and subsequently withdrawn under controlled kinematic and rheological conditions to deposit a uniform, adherent wet film whose thickness is governed by gravity drainage, surface tension, withdrawal speed, and bath viscosity.

In the context of **Vehicle Body Engineering (PEMUT413)**, immersion is most frequently encountered as:

- **Cathodic/Electro-Dip Coating (e-Coating / CED)** — body-in-white (BIW) is dipped in a paint tank and acts as an electrode.
- **Phosphating / Pre-treatment Immersion** — zinc/iron/manganese phosphate deposition for corrosion resistance.
- **PVC Under-body / Wax-based Dipping** — anti-chip and anti-corrosion protection in cavities and sills.

## 1.2 Painting Equipment — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Painting Equipment** in automotive manufacturing refers to the integrated *material-feed, atomization, delivery, and film-deposition hardware* used to apply decorative topcoats (basecoat, clearcoat), primers, sealants, and protective coatings onto the vehicle body. Equipment selection is dictated by **transfer efficiency, film uniformity, environmental compliance (VOC), capital cost, and substrate geometry**.

The principal categories examined in this module are:

1. **Conventional Air-Spray (Compressed Air Atomization)**
2. **Airless Spray (Hydraulic Atomization)**
3. **High-Volume Low-Pressure (HVLP) Spray**
4. **Electrostatic Spray (Rotational Bell / Disk)**
5. **Dip / Immersion Systems**
6. **Powder Coating Booths**
7. **Curtain / Flow Coating**

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 1 — Dip Coating is like dipping a tea bag into hot water:** Just as tea compounds infuse outward from the bag into the water, the coating liquid "clings" to the panel as it is withdrawn. The faster you pull it out, the thicker the liquid layer that drains down — exactly like the **Landau–Levich–Derjaguin (LLD) film-thickness law** used in paint engineering.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 2 — Spray Painting is like an aerosol perfume dispenser:** Pressurized fluid is forced through a tiny orifice, breaks into fine droplets (atomization), and is propelled toward the panel. The droplet size, velocity, and electrostatic charge determine how much paint actually reaches the body versus how much is wasted as **overspray**.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics Highlighted

- **Transfer Efficiency (TE):** **30–90 %** depending on equipment class.
- **Film thickness for CED:** typically **18–25 μm**.
- **Phosphate coating weight:** **1.5–3.0 g/m²**.
- **VOC emission (conventional air-spray):** ~ **45 % of paint mass**; with HVLP/electrostatic reduced to ~ **15–20 %**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Immersion Process — Operational Logic

The dip-coating operation is a **seven-stage sequential process** performed in a pre-treatment tunnel:

1. **Pre-degreasing** — removes stamping oils and shop soils via alkaline spray.
2. **Degreasing (Immersion)** — full submersion in 40–60 °C alkaline tank; residence time ≈ 2–3 min.
3. **Water Rinse (1 & 2)** — counter-flow rinses to eliminate alkaline carryover.
4. **Surface Conditioning (Titanium Phosphate Activation)** — increases nucleation sites.
5. **Phosphating (Immersion or Spray)** — forms crystalline Zn/Fe phosphate layer.
6. **DI Water Rinse** — removes soluble salts.
7. **Passivation / Seal Rinse** — improves corrosion resistance.

> **Why the order matters:** Each bath chemistry is incompatible with the next; incomplete rinsing causes *drag-out contamination* and powdery, non-adherent coatings.

## 2.2 Film Formation During Withdrawal — The Landau–Levich–Derjaguin Model

As the body is withdrawn at velocity *U* from a Newtonian bath of viscosity *η* and density *ρ*, a capillary-entrained film of thickness *h* is deposited:

- **Capillary number:** $Ca = \dfrac{\eta U}{\gamma_{LV}}$ where $\gamma_{LV}$ is liquid–vapor surface tension.
- **Film thickness (LLD regime):** $h \approx 0.94 \,\ell_c \, Ca^{2/3}$ where $\ell_c = \sqrt{\gamma_{LV}/(\rho g)}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Takeaway:** To obtain a *thicker* anti-chip dip-coat, the production engineer must **either increase withdrawal speed *U* (subject to dripping instability) or use a higher-viscosity bath**. Modern CED lines operate with computer-controlled hoist speeds of **0.5–3.0 m/min**.

## 2.3 Cathodic Electro-Deposition (CED) — Why & How

In CED, the vehicle body is the **cathode** and the tank walls are the **anode** (insulated). A DC voltage of **180–320 V** is applied. Paint resin (positively charged, e.g., epoxy-amine) migrates to the cathode, neutralizes by H⁺ reduction, and deposits as an insoluble film.

- Anode (tank): $2H_2O \rightarrow O_2 + 4H^+ + 4e^-$
- Cathode (body): $2H_2O + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2 + 2OH^-$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Typical Unit |
|---|----------|---------|----------------|--------------|
| 1 | Capillary Number | $Ca = \eta U / \gamma_{LV}$ | η = dynamic viscosity, U = withdrawal speed, γ_LV = surface tension | dimensionless |
| 2 | Capillary Length | $\ell_c = \sqrt{\gamma_{LV}/(\rho g)}$ | ρ = density, g = 9.81 | m |
| 3 | Dip-coat Film Thickness | $h \approx 0.94 \ell_c Ca^{2/3}$ | LLD equation | m |
| 4 | Transfer Efficiency | $TE = (m_{substrate}/m_{total}) \times 100$ | m_substrate = paint reaching body | % |
| 5 | Theoretical Coverage | $C = (V \times f_s \times TE) / (t_d \times A)$ | V = paint volume, f_s = volume solids fraction, t_d = dry film thickness, A = area | m²/L |
| 6 | Throw Power (CED) | $TP = (h_{interior}/h_{exterior}) \times 100$ | h = film thickness at recessed vs. outer panel | % |
| 7 | Reynolds No. (Spray) | $Re = \rho v d / \eta$ | v = droplet velocity, d = droplet diameter | dimensionless |
| 8 | Spray Rate | $Q = C_d A_o \sqrt{2 \Delta P / \rho}$ | C_d = discharge coeff, A_o = orifice area, ΔP = pressure drop | m³/s |
| 9 | Electrodeposition Mass (Faraday) | $m = (M \, I \, t)/(n \, F)$ | M = molar mass, I = current, t = time, n = electrons, F = 96485 | kg |
| 10 | Curing Time (Epoxy) | $t_c = A \exp(E_a/RT)$ | Arrhenius relation, A = pre-factor, E_a = activation energy | s |

> [!WARNING]
> **Absolute value safety:** All vertical bars in the table have been replaced with `\vert` or implicit notation; do **not** use plain `\|x\|` inside KTU answer sheets — always write `\vert x \vert`.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **CED** is the global *de facto* anti-corrosion primer for > **95 %** of mass-produced cars (Toyota, Tata, Maruti, Hyundai lines).
- **HVLP + Electrostatic Bell** combinations achieve > **75 % transfer efficiency**, saving ~ **30 % paint cost** per vehicle.
- **Dip-coat phosphate** is the *only* reliable method to reach internal box-sections (A-, B-, C-pillars) where spray cannot penetrate.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Withdrawal Film Thickness in Dip Coating

We start from the steady-state Navier–Stokes balance on a thin film entrained on a flat plate withdrawn vertically from a large reservoir.

**Step 1 — Capillary number definition.**
The dimensionless competition between viscous forces and surface tension is:

$$
Ca = \frac{\eta U}{\gamma_{LV}}
$$

**Step 2 — Capillary length definition.**
A natural length scale balancing gravity and capillarity:

$$
\ell_c = \sqrt{\frac{\gamma_{LV}}{\rho g}}
$$

**Step 3 — Match the viscous-capillary meniscus region.**
In the dynamic meniscus, the film thickness grows as $Ca^{2/3}$. Landau & Levich (1942) obtained the exact prefactor 0.944; Derjaguin (1943) added the thick-film correction:

$$
h = 0.944 \,\ell_c \, Ca^{2/3} \left(1 - \frac{h^3 \rho g}{3 \eta U}\right)^{-1/3}
$$

**Step 4 — Thin-film limit (gravity drainage negligible).**
For typical automotive paint ($\eta \sim 0.05$ Pa·s, $U \sim 0.01$ m/s, $\rho \sim 1100$ kg/m³), the second term is small, and we obtain:

$$
h \approx 0.94 \,\ell_c \, Ca^{2/3}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute back to dimensional form.**

$$
h \approx 0.94 \left(\frac{\gamma_{LV}}{\rho g}\right)^{1/2} \left(\frac{\eta U}{\gamma_{LV}}\right)^{2/3}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the result:** $h$ increases with $\eta$ and $U$ but decreases with $\rho$ and $g$. This is precisely why lighter solvents (lower $\rho$) and slower hoist speeds give *thinner* primer coats in paint shops.

## 3.2 Derivation: Theoretical Paint Coverage

A paint engineer must estimate how many square metres of body shell can be coated by 1 litre of wet paint.

**Step 1 — Volume of solids only.**  
Of the liquid paint, a fraction $f_s$ becomes solid film; the rest is solvent:

$$
V_{solids} = V \cdot f_s
$$

**Step 2 — Account for transfer efficiency (paint lost to overspray).**  
Only fraction $TE$ reaches the body:

$$
V_{effective} = V \cdot f_s \cdot TE
$$

**Step 3 — Spread over area at dry film thickness $t_d$.**

$$
C = \frac{V \cdot f_s \cdot TE}{t_d}
$$

**Step 4 — Insert numerical example.**  
Given $V = 1$ L, $f_s = 0.45$, $TE = 0.75$, $t_d = 25 \,\mu m = 25 \times 10^{-6}$ m:

$$
C = \frac{1 \times 0.45 \times 0.75}{25 \times 10^{-6}} = 13500 \text{ m}^2/\text{L} \text{ (theoretical)}
$$

**Step 5 — Practical coverage with wastage factor (typically ×2):**  
$\approx 6750 \,\text{m}^2/\text{L}$ — still vastly higher than the actual area of a car body (~ **80–100 m²**), confirming that paint cost is dominated by **labour/booth time**, not material.

## 3.3 Derivation: Faraday's Law Applied to CED

**Step 1 — Faraday's first law of electrolysis:**

$$
m = \frac{M \, I \, t}{n \, F}
$$

**Step 2 — For a cathodic epoxy-amine resin** (assume $M_{avg} = 800$ g/mol, $n = 1$, $F = 96485$ C/mol).

**Step 3 — Compute mass per m² at $20 \,\mu m$ and $\rho_{film} = 1.4 \times 10^3$ kg/m³:**

$$
m/A = \rho_{film} \cdot t_d = 1400 \times 20 \times 10^{-6} = 0.028 \text{ kg/m}^2 = 28 \text{ g/m}^2
$$

**Step 4 — Current required per m²:**

$$
I/A = \frac{nF}{Mt} = \frac{1 \times 96485 \times 0.028}{800} = 3.38 \text{ A·s/m}^2
$$

**Step 5 — For a 2.5 min CED cycle ($t = 150$ s):**

$$
I/A = \frac{0.028 \times 96485}{800 \times 150} \approx 22.5 \text{ A/m}^2
$$

> [!NOTE]
> A typical car body (≈ 80 m² surface area) thus draws ~ **1800 A** during the CED dip — handled by a **rectifier bank** of 2000 A capacity.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Equipment Selection vs. Requirement

| Requirement | Best Equipment | TE (%) | VOC Penalty | Capital Cost |
|-------------|----------------|--------|-------------|--------------|
| Anti-corrosion primer (full body) | CED (e-coat) | 95 | Low | High |
| Topcoat (large flat panels) | Rotary Bell Electrostatic | 80 | Low | High |
| Touch-up / repair | Conventional Air-Spray | 30 | High | Low |
| Anti-chip under-body | Airless Spray | 50 | Medium | Medium |
| Cavity wax | Airless / Pressure-fed wand | 40 | Medium | Low |
| Box-section internal coating | Dip-CED (only feasible) | 95 | Low | High |
| Powder coat (alloys/wheels) | Electrostatic Fluidized Bed | 95 | None | Medium |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Immersion / CED Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[BIW Entry] --> A2[Pre Degrease Spray]
    A2 --> A3[Immersion Degrease Tank 60C]
    A3 --> A4[Water Rinse 1]
    A4 --> A5[Water Rinse 2]
    A5 --> A6[Surface Conditioner Activation]
    A6 --> A7[Zinc Phosphate Immersion Tank]
    A7 --> A8[DI Water Rinse]
    A8 --> A9[Passivation Rinse]
    A9 --> A10[CED Cathode BIW Anode Tank Walls]
    A10 --> A11[UF Rinse 1]
    A11 --> A12[UF Rinse 2]
    A12 --> A13[Staging Dry Off Oven]
    A13 --> A14[Primer Surfacer Spray Booth]
    A14 --> A15[Basecoat Booth]
    A15 --> A16[Clearcoat Booth]
    A16 --> A17[Paint Bake Oven 140C]
    A17 --> A18[Polishing and Inspection]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Spray Painting Equipment Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FEED[Paint Feed System]
        P1[Pressure Pot] --> P2[Paint Heater]
        P2 --> P3[Inline Filter 50 micron]
    end
    P3 --> ATOM
    subgraph ATOM[Atomization Module]
        A1[Air Cap Conventional]
        A2[Reverse Air HVLP Cap]
        A3[Rotary Bell Electrostatic]
        A4[Airless Nozzle 0.013 inch]
    end
    ATOM --> SHAP
    subgraph SHAP[Shaping Air and Pattern Control]
        S1[Fan Air Jets]
        S2[Horn Air Nozzles]
    end
    SHAP --> SUB
    subgraph SUB[Substrate and Recovery]
        R1[Vehicle Body in Booth]
        R2[Grounded Booth Floor]
        R3[Water Wash Scrubber]
        R4[Overspray Recovery Filters]
    end
    ATOM --> R1
    R1 --> R2
    R3 --> R4
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block — Dip-Coating Withdrawal Physics

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B1[Bath of Newtonian Liquid density rho viscosity eta] --> B2[Vertical Plate Submerged]
    B2 --> B3[Hoist Withdraws at Velocity U]
    B3 --> B4[Dynamic Meniscus Region]
    B4 --> B5[Capillary Number Ca computed]
    B5 --> B6[LLD Equation h equals 0.94 Lc Ca to the 2/3]
    B6 --> B7[Liquid Film Drains by Gravity]
    B7 --> B8[Film Solidifies to Final Thickness t d]
    B8 --> B9[Quality Check Uniformity DFT]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** The block diagrams above deliberately abstract the *physical geometry* (tanks, spray guns, robots) into functional flows so the engineering logic — feed → atomize → transfer → recover — is unambiguous for KTU board answers.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define "Transfer Efficiency" of a painting process. Compare the transfer efficiency of conventional air-spray, HVLP, and electrostatic bell-disc methods.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
